25131.extract: The archaeological site of Libisosa is located in the "Cerro del Castillo" in the municipality of Lezuza. As a result of archaeological excavations, it is now known that the site covers 30 hectares of remains ranging from the Late Bronze Age to the Late Middle Ages, the latter presided over by the watchtower that bears the name of the hill, and to which also corresponds a building of the military orders. From the Roman period, the late Republican wall and the forum of the Roman colony mentioned by Pliny the Elder stand out, while the final phase of the Iberian oppidum assigned to the regio oretana by Ptolemy stands out. The excellent preservation of structures and materials of the Ibero-Roman stage of Libisosa is due to the "burial effect" caused by its hasty and systematic destruction, which has allowed a frozen image of its state prior to the devastation to reach the present day, which constitutes a mine for research on the Final Iberian and Republican Hispania, most of which has yet to be discovered.

25148.extract: Wasit was an early Islamic city in Iraq. It was founded in the 8th century by the Umayyad viceroy of Iraq, al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf, to serve as the region's seat and as the garrison of the Syrian troops who enforced Umayyad rule there. It was situated between the two traditional administrative centers and garrisons of Iraq, Kufa and Basra, hence its name Wasit. The city was abandoned centuries later and its ruins are located in the eponymous Wasit Governorate, southeast of Kut in southeastern Iraq.

25151.extract: Shushtar is a city in the Central District of Shushtar County, Khuzestan province, Iran, serving as capital of both the county and the district.

25158.extract: Segermes is an ancient town in Tunisia. Under the Roman Empire, the town belonged to the province of Byzacena. The town is identified with ruins at Henchir Harat, Zaghouan.

25166.extract: Nicosia, also known as Lefkosia or Lefkoşa, is the capital of Cyprus. Its northern part is the de facto capital of Northern Cyprus. It is the southeasternmost capital city among European Union member states.
25166.romanEraExtract: Nicosia, also known as Lefkosia or Lefkoşa, is the capital of Cyprus. Its northern part is the de facto capital of Northern Cyprus. It is the southeasternmost capital city among European Union member states.

25167.extract:
Heraklion, Herakleion or Iraklion, is the largest city and the administrative capital of the island of Crete and capital of Heraklion regional unit. It is the fourth largest city in Greece, and the largest city in the Greek islands, with a municipal population of 179,302 (2021) and 211,370 in its wider metropolitan area, according to the 2011 census.
The greater area of Heraklion has been continuously inhabited since at least 7000 BCE, making it one of the oldest inhabited regions in Europe. It is also home to the ancient Knossos Palace, a major center of the Minoan civilization dating back to approximately 2000-1350 BCE, often considered Europe's oldest city. The palace is one of the most significant archaeological sites in Greece, second only to the Parthenon in terms of visitor numbers.
25167.romanEraExtract: Heraklion (Iraklion, Crete) lies near the ancient Minoan palace of Knossos — the legendary labyrinth of King Minos and the Minotaur. In the Roman period, the port of Heracleum served the island of Crete, which Rome annexed in 67 BC after suppressing Cretan piracy. Crete and Cyrenaica were governed as a single province. The island was a relatively peaceful backwater under Roman rule, known for its wine, honey, and medicinal herbs.

25168.extract: Olous or Olus was a city of ancient Crete; now sunken, it was situated at the site of present day town of Elounda, Crete, Greece. According to the Stadiasmus Maris Magni, it had a harbour and was located 260 stadia from Chersonasus and 15 stadia from Camara.

25169.extract: Praesus or Praisos, also Prasus or Prasos (Πρᾶσος), was a Greek city in ancient Crete. Strabo reports that it belonged to the Eteocretes, and contained the temple of the Dictaean Zeus, for Mount Dicte was in the territory of Praesus. Strabo reports Praesus as lying between the promontories Samonium and Chersonesus, at a distance of 60 stadia from the sea, and close to Mount Dicte. However, Strabo confuses Praesus with Priansus, when he says it bordered upon the territory of Leben, and was distant 70 stadia from the sea, and 180 from Gortyn.

25170.extract: Priansus or Priansos, also Pransus or Pransos (Πράνσος), was a town and polis (city-state) in ancient Crete.

25171.extract: Rhithymna or Rithymna or Rhithymnia (Ῥιθυμνία), was a town of ancient Crete, Greece, which is mentioned by Ptolemy and Pliny the Elder as the first town on the north coast to the east of Amphimalla, and is spoken of as a Cretan city by Stephanus of Byzantium, in whose text its name is written Rhithymnia; Stephanus gives the city's ethnonyms as Ῥιθυμνιάτης and Ῥιθύμνιος. It is also alluded to by Lycophron (76). Modern Rethymno retains the name of the ancient city, upon whose site it stands. Rhithymna minted coins in antiquity; maritime emblems are found on them. It is believed that Arsinoe is the same town as Rhithymna.

25172.extract: Knidos or Cnidus was a Greek city in ancient Caria and part of the Dorian Hexapolis, in south-western Asia Minor, modern-day Turkey. It was situated on the Datça peninsula, which forms the southern side of the Sinus Ceramicus, now known as Gulf of Gökova. By the 4th century BC, Knidos was located at the site of modern Tekir, opposite Triopion Island. But earlier, it was probably at the site of modern Datça.

25173.extract: Ambrysus or Ambrysos or Ambrosus or Ambrosos (Ἄμβροσσος) or Amphrysus or Amphrysos (Ἄμφρυσος) was a town of ancient Phocis, situated 60 stadia from Stiris, northeast of Anticyra, at the southern foot of Mount Cirphis, and in a fertile valley, producing abundance of wine and the coccus, or kermes berry, used to dye scarlet. It was located in the southern part of Phocis, bordering on the territory of Parapotamii. It was destroyed by order of the Amphictyons, but was rebuilt and fortified by the Thebans with a double wall, in their war against Philip II of Macedon, who had during the Third Sacred War taken Ambrysus among other cities in Phocis (346 BCE). In c. 228-224 BCE, it was attacked by the Aetolians, like the city of Daulis. Its fortifications were considered by Pausanias the strongest in Greece, next to those of Messene. It was taken by the Romans in the Second Macedonian War, 198 BCE. During the visit of Pausanias, in the second century, the town had an agora of small size and statues of stone, most of which were broken.

25174.extract: Argura, called Argissa (Ἄργισσα) in Homer's Iliad, was a town and polis (city-state) in Pelasgiotis in ancient Thessaly, on the Peneus, and near Larissa. The name of the town was also given as Argusa (Ἆργουσσα) in some ancient sources. The distance between this place and Larissa is so small as to explain the remark of the Scholiast on Apollonius of Rhodes, that the Argissa of Homer was the same as Larissa. The editors of the Barrington Atlas of the Greek and Roman World and The Princeton Encyclopedia of Classical Sites identify the site of Agura with a place called Gremnos Magoula, approximately 7 kilometres (4.3 mi) west of Larissa, which has a nearby tumulus.

25175.extract: Daphnus or Daphnous was a city on the Euboean Sea, originally belonging to ancient Phocis, which thus extended from the Corinthian Gulf to the Euboean sea. Its narrow territory separated the Locri Epicnemidii from the Locri Opuntii; but it was afterwards assigned to the Opuntii. The town was in ruins in the time of Strabo, who fixes its site by describing it as 20 stadia distant from Cynus and 120 from Elateia, and as having a harbour. Daphnus appears in an inscription dated to 407 BCE. Daphnus lay at the head of a pass that was one of the major arteries from northern to central Greece.

25176.extract: Hyettus or Hyettos was a village in ancient Boeotia, Greece. According to Pausanias, who visited the site in the 2nd century CE, it was located near Lake Copais, at 7 stadia from Olmones and 20 stades from Cyrtones. There was a temple of Heracles in the village, frequented by the sick for the cure of their diseases, with the deity worshiped in the form of a rude stone. An inscription built into the chapel of Agios Nikolaos south of the acropolis attests to a cult of Savior Asklepios in the 2nd century CE.

25178.extract:
Medeon was a town of ancient Phocis, destroyed by Philip II of Macedon along with the other Phocian towns at the termination of the Third Sacred War, and never again restored. Strabo places it on the Crissaean Gulf, at the distance of 160 stadia from Boeotia; and Pausanias says that it was near Antikyra.
According to ancient writers it was named after Medeon the son of Electra and Pylades.

25180.extract: Trichonium or Trichonion was a town of ancient Aetolia, from which Lake Trichonis derived its name. William Martin Leake identified its location in the 19th century south of the lake at a place called Gavala (Gavalou). Strabo mentions Trichonium along with Stratus as situated in a fertile plain. It was evidently a place of importance, and several natives of this town are mentioned in history.

25183.extract: Kadirli, historically Kars or Karsbazar, is a city in Osmaniye Province in the Mediterranean region of Turkey. It is the seat of Kadirli District. Its population is 98,469 (2022). It is located in the Çukurova plain, 41 kilometres from the province's capital, Osmaniye. It is located near the ancient site of Flaviopolis.

25187.extract: Trimithis was an ancient Egyptian city in the Dakhla Oasis in the Western Desert. The site of the city is known today as Amheida and is located in the New Valley Governorate of Egypt. Trimithis was a prominent urban center in Roman Egypt, with earlier occupation dating back to the Old and New Kingdoms. Under Roman rule, it featured temples, residential areas, and public buildings. Excavations have revealed a 4th-century house belonging to the wealthy councilor Serenos, a rare case of an ancient school, and a church complex.

25188.extract: Karanog was a Kushite town in Lower Nubia on the west bank of the Nile. It was probably a provincial capital under its own peshto (governor) in the second and third centuries AD. It was excavated between 1907 and 1910 by David Randall-MacIver and Leonard Woolley of the University of Pennsylvania Museum.

25189.extract: Wad ben Naga is the name of an ancient town in the Kushitic Kingdom in present-day Sudan. The village lies on the eastern bank of the Nile, about 80 kilometers upstream of Meroë and about 40 km southwest of Shendi.
25189.romanEraExtract: Wad ben Naga is the name of an ancient town in the Kushitic Kingdom in present-day Sudan. The village lies on the eastern bank of the Nile, about 80 kilometers upstream of Meroë and about 40 km southwest of Shendi.

25190.extract: Meroë was an ancient city on the east bank of the Nile about 6 km north-east of the Kabushiya station near Shendi, Sudan, approximately 200 km north-east of Khartoum. Near the site is a group of villages called Bagrawiyah. This city was the capital of the Kingdom of Kush for several centuries from around 590 BC, until its collapse in the 4th century AD. The Kushitic Kingdom of Meroë gave its name to the "Island of Meroë", which was the modern region of Butana, a region bounded by the Nile, the Atbarah and the Blue Nile.

25191.extract: Napata was a city of ancient Kush at the fourth cataract of the Nile founded by the Egyptian Amun cult for Egyptian pilgrims given by its, as suggested, Egyptian name. It is located approximately 1.5 kilometers from the east side of the river at the site of modern Karima, Sudan.

25193.extract: al-Ula, officially AlUla, is an ancient Arabian oasis city and governorate located in Medina Province, Saudi Arabia, 350 kilometres northwest of the city of Medina. Situated in the Hejaz, a region that features prominently in the history of Islam as well as several pre-Islamic Semitic civilizations, al-Ula was a market city on the historic incense trade route that linked India and the Persian Gulf to the Levant and Europe.

25195.extract: Anaphlystus or Anaphlystos was a coastal (paralia) deme of ancient Athens, belonging to the Antiochis phyle, on the west coast of Attica, opposite the island of Eleussa, and a little north of the promontory of Sunium, between that promontory and that of Astypalaea. It bordered on Aegilia to the west, to Atene in the south-east and to Amphitrope to the east. To the northwest, it was separated from Phrearrhioi by the Astike Hodos.

25196.extract: Euonymeia, also known by its medieval name Trachones, and by its modern colloquial Ano Kalamaki, is a historic settlement and currently a residential neighborhood within the suburban town of Alimos in the southern part of the Athens urban area, Greece. The area is an inland part of the south Athenian plain, situated between the foothills of Mount Hymettus and the southern coastal zone of Athens on the Saronic Gulf. The land is characterized by limestone hills and streams running from Hymettus toward the coast.

25199.extract: Álora is a town and municipality in southern Spain in the province of Málaga, in the autonomous community of Andalusia. Located c. 40 km from Málaga, on the right bank of the river Guadalhorce and on the Córdoba-Málaga railway, within the comarca of Valle del Guadalhorce. It is a typical pueblo blanco, a whitewashed village nestled between three rocky spurs topped by the ruins of the castle.

25213.extract: Jerez de los Caballeros is a town of south-western Spain, in the province of Badajoz. It is located on two hills overlooking the River Ardila, a tributary of the Guadiana, 18 km east of the Portuguese border. The old town is surrounded by a Moorish wall with six gates. The town is said to have been founded by Alfonso IX of Leon in 1229; in 1232 it was extended by his son Ferdinand III the Saint, who gave it to the Knights Templar. Hence the name Jerez de los Caballeros, Jerez of the Knights.

25222.extract: Thyrium or Thyrion, or Thyreum or Thyreon (Θύρεον), or Thurium or Thourion (Θούριον), or Thyrreium or Thyrreion (Θύρρειον), was a city in ancient Acarnania. Cicero tells us that in sailing from Alyzia to Leucas, he touched at Thyrium, where he remained two hours; and from this statement, as well as from the history of the events in which Thyrium is mentioned, we may infer that it was situated on or near the Ionian Sea, and that it was the first town on the coast south of the channel which separated Leucas from the mainland.

25224.extract: Qabala is a city and the administrative centre of the Qabala District of Azerbaijan. The municipality consists of the city of Gabala and the village of Küsnat. Before the city was known as Kutkashen, but after the Republic of Azerbaijan's independence the town was renamed in honour of the much older city of Gabala, the former capital of Caucasian Albania, the archaeological site of which is about 20 km southwest.

25225.extract: Dvin was a large commercial city and the capital of early medieval Armenia. It was situated north of the previous ancient capital of Armenia, the city of Artaxata, along the banks of the Metsamor River, 35 km to the south of modern Yerevan. It is claimed it was one of the largest cities east of Constantinople prior to its destruction by the Mongols in the 13th century, but with an overall area of approximately 1 km2, it was far smaller than many of the great cities of Asia. It had an estimated population of 45,000 in 361, 47,000 in 622, and around 100,000 at its height in the 8th-9th centuries. Nyura Hakobyan proposed a peak population of 100,000 to 150,000.
25225.romanEraExtract: Dvin (Duin) was a major Armenian and Sasanian city near modern Artashat, Armenia. Founded in the 4th century AD as the capital of Arsacid Armenia, it later served as the seat of the Persian marzban (governor) and the Armenian Catholicos. The city was one of the largest in the Caucasus, with a population perhaps reaching 100,000. It was repeatedly contested between the Roman/Byzantine and Sasanian empires as part of the struggle for control of Armenia.

25226.extract: Vagharshapat is the 5th-largest city in Armenia and the most populous municipal community of Armavir Province, located about 18 km (11 mi) west of the capital Yerevan, and 10 km (6 mi) north of the closed Turkish-Armenian border. It is commonly known as Ejmiatsin, which was its official name between 1945 and 1995. It is still commonly used colloquially and in official bureaucracy, a case of dual naming.

25238.extract: Reishahr or Rev Ardashir was a city on the Persian Gulf in medieval Iran and is currently an archaeological site near Bushehr. It may be identical to the Antiochia-in-Persis of the Seleucid period, but was refounded by Ardashir I (d.AD224), the first ruler of the Sasanian Empire. In the Church of the East, it was seat of the metropolitan bishop of the province of Fars from at least 424. The name "Rew-Ardashir" means "Rich is Ardashir".

25240.extract: Thāj is an archaeological site and ancient town located west of Jubail in the Eastern Province, Saudi Arabia, located approximately 95 km from the coast of the Arabian Gulf. It is associated with the Hellenistic era. Many artifacts including pottery and jewellery have been discovered at Thāj, some of which are currently held in the British Museum. The site is noted for its potential identification with the lost ancient city of Gerrha.

25242.extract: Nishapur or Neyshabur is a city in the Central District of Nishapur County, Razavi Khorasan province, Iran, serving as capital of both the county and the district.
25242.romanEraExtract: Nishapur (modern Iran) was a major city in Khorasan, one of the four great cities of the Sasanian east (alongside Merv, Herat, and Balkh). Though beyond direct Roman reach, Nishapur sat on the Silk Road trade routes that connected China with the Roman Mediterranean. The city was founded or refounded by Shapur I of the Sasanian dynasty and grew into one of the largest cities in the medieval Islamic world.

25244.extract: Mashhad, historically also known as Mashad, Meshhed, or Meshed, is the second-most-populous city in Iran, located in the north-east of the country about 740 kilometres east of Tehran. In the Central District of Mashhad County, it serves as the capital of Razavi Khorasan province, the county, and the district. It has a population of about 3,400,000, which includes the areas of Mashhad Taman and Torqabeh.

25245.extract: Balkh is a city in northern Afghanistan, with an estimated population of 148,972 people. It is within the jurisdiction of Balkh District and its current mayor is Maulvi Mohammad Qasim Fazal Subhan. Located approximately 24 km (15 mi) to the northwest of Mazar-i-Sharif and approximately 74 km (46 mi) to the south of the Amu Darya or the Afghanistan–Uzbekistan border, it is the second largest city of Balkh Province.

25246.extract: Ai-Khanoum is the archaeological site of a Hellenistic city in Takhar Province, Afghanistan. The city, whose original name is unknown, was likely founded by an early ruler of the Seleucid Empire and served as a military and economic centre for the rulers of the Greco-Bactrian Kingdom until its destruction c. 145 BC. Rediscovered in 1961, the ruins of the city were excavated by a French team of archaeologists until the outbreak of conflict in Afghanistan in the late 1970s.

25270.extract: Dilbat was an ancient Near Eastern city located 25 kilometers south of Babylon on the eastern bank of the Western Euphrates in modern-day Babil Governorate, Iraq. It lies 15 kilometers southeast of the ancient city of Borsippa. The site of Tell Muhattat, 5 kilometers away, was earlier thought to be Dilbat. The ziggurat E-ibe-Anu, dedicated to Urash, a minor local deity distinct from the earth goddess Urash, was located in the center of the city and was mentioned in the Epic of Gilgamesh.

25274.extract: Qasr Shemamok is an ancient Near East archaeological site about 30 kilometers south of modern Erbil in Erbil Governorate, in the Kurdistan Region of Iraq on the west bank of the Shiwazor river. It is about 25 kilometers from the ancient site of Nimrud. Remains at the site date mainly to the Hurrian, Middle Assyrian, Neo-Assyrian, Parthian, and Sassanian times. Under the Assyrians, it was named Kilizu and was a provincial capital. It is not far from the ancient sites of Kurd Qaburstan, Tell Halawa, Tell Aliawa, and Tell Baqrta.

25827.extract: The Kingdom of Aksum, or the Aksumite Empire, was a kingdom in East Africa and South Arabia from classical antiquity to the Middle Ages, based in what is now northern Ethiopia and Eritrea, and spanning present-day Djibouti, Sudan, and Yemen. Emerging from the earlier Dʿmt civilization, the kingdom was founded in the 1st century. The city of Axum served as the kingdom's capital for many centuries until it relocated to Kubar in the 9th century due to declining trade connections and recurring invasions.

26674.extract: Vienna is the capital, most populous city, and one of the nine states of Austria. It is Austria's primate city, with just over two million inhabitants. Its larger metropolitan area has a population of nearly 2.9 million, representing nearly one-third of the country's population. Vienna is the cultural, economic, and political centre of the country, the fifth-largest city by population in the European Union, and the most populous of the cities on the river Danube.

It is the second-largest town in Mallorca, after the capital of Palma. The municipality features tourist attractions such as Porto Cristo, site of the famous Caves of Drach, and Cales de Mallorca. Manacor has one of the busiest street markets on the island, held every Monday morning. Manacor is famous for high-quality wood furniture manufacturing and artificial pearls.

29002.extract: Pataliputra, adjacent to modern-day Patna, Bihar, was a city in ancient India, originally built by Magadha ruler Ajatashatru in 490 BCE, as a small fort near the Ganges river. Udayin laid the foundation of the city of Pataliputra at the confluence of two rivers, the Son and the Ganges. He shifted his capital from Rajgriha to Pataliputra due to the latter's central location in the empire.

32190.extract: Anuradhapura is a major city located in the north central plain of Sri Lanka. It is the capital city of North Central Province and the capital of Anuradhapura District. The city lies 205 kilometers (127 mi) north of the current capital of Colombo in the North Central Province, on the banks of the historic Malwathu Oya. The city is now a World Heritage Site famous for its well-preserved ruins of the ancient Sinhalese civilisation.

32197.extract: Banavasi is an ancient temple town located near Sirsi in Karnataka. Banavasi was the ancient capital of the Kadamba dynasty that ruled all of modern-day Karnataka state. They were the first native empire to bring Kannada and Karnataka to prominence. It is 24 km (15 mi) away from its nearest large city Sirsi through SH 77.

They were the first native empire to bring Kannada and Karnataka to prominence. It is 24 km (15 mi) away from its nearest large city Sirsi through SH 77.

32236.extract: Kalyan is a city on the banks of Ulhas River in Thane district of Maharashtra state in Konkan division. It is governed by Kalyan-Dombivli Municipal Corporation. Kalyan is a subdivision (Taluka) of Thane district. Kalyan and its neighbouring township of Dombivli jointly form Kalyan-Dombivli Municipal Corporation, abbreviated as KDMC. It is a founding city of the Mumbai Metropolitan Region.
32236.romanEraExtract: Kalyan is a city on the banks of Ulhas River in Thane district of Maharashtra state in Konkan division. It is governed by Kalyan-Dombivli Municipal Corporation. Kalyan is a subdivision (Taluka) of Thane district. Kalyan and its neighbouring township of Dombivli jointly form Kalyan-Dombivli Municipal...

32274.extract: Kanchipuram district is one of the 38 districts in the state of Tamil Nadu in India. The area comprising the present day Kancheepuram district was earlier a part of Chingleput district. The original Chingleput district was split in 1997 into form the present day Kanchipuram and Tiruvallur districts.
32274.romanEraExtract: Kanchipuram district is one of the 38 districts in the state of Tamil Nadu in India. The area comprising the present day Kancheepuram district was earlier a part of Chingleput district. The original Chingleput district was split in 1997 into form the present day Kanchipuram and Tiruvallur districts.

32309.extract: Madurai is a major tier II city in the Indian state of Tamil Nadu. It is the cultural capital of Tamil Nadu and the administrative headquarters of Madurai district, which is governed by the Madurai Municipal Corporation established on 1 November 1866. As of the 2011 census, it is the third largest metropolis in Tamil Nadu after Chennai and Coimbatore in terms of population and 27th largest urban agglomeration in India. Located on the banks of Vaigai River, Madurai has been a major settlement for two millennia and has a documented history of more than 2500 years. It is often referred to as "Thoongaa Nagaram", meaning "the city that never sleeps".
32309.romanEraExtract: Madurai is a major tier II city in the Indian state of Tamil Nadu. It is the cultural capital of Tamil Nadu and the administrative headquarters of Madurai district, which is governed by the Madurai Municipal Corporation established on 1 November 1866. As of the 2011 census, it is the third largest m...

32338.extract: Muciṟi, ,, commonly anglicized as Muziris, was an ancient harbour and urban centre on India's Malabar Coast. It was the major ancient port city of the Chera dynasty of Ancient Tamilakam.
32338.romanEraExtract: Muciṟi, ,, commonly anglicized as Muziris, was an ancient harbour and urban centre on India's Malabar Coast. It was the major ancient port city of the Chera dynasty of Ancient Tamilakam.

32437.extract: Arikamedu is an archaeological site in Southern India, in Kakkayanthope, Ariyankuppam Commune, Puducherry. It is 4 kilometres (2.5 mi) from the capital, Pondicherry of the Indian territory of Puducherry.
32437.romanEraExtract: Arikamedu is an archaeological site in Southern India, in Kakkayanthope, Ariyankuppam Commune, Puducherry. It is 4 kilometres (2.5 mi) from the capital, Pondicherry of the Indian territory of Puducherry.

40998.extract: Zintan is a city in northwestern Libya, situated roughly 136 kilometres (85 mi) southwest of Tripoli, in the Nafusa Mountains area. The city and its surrounding area have a population of 16,024.
40998.romanEraExtract: Zintan is a city in northwestern Libya, situated roughly 136 kilometres (85 mi) southwest of Tripoli, in the Nafusa Mountains area. The city and its surrounding area have a population of 16,024.

41089.extract: The Principality of Apselia was an ancient polity located on the Black Sea coast of the northwest Caucasus near the estuary of Kodori river. The earliest known historical references to Apselia are from the writings of Pliny the Elder and Flavius Arrian, who referred to the region as Apsilae.
41089.romanEraExtract: The Principality of Apselia was an ancient polity located on the Black Sea coast of the northwest Caucasus near the estuary of Kodori river. The earliest known historical references to Apselia are from the writings of Pliny the Elder and Flavius Arrian, who referred to the region as Apsilae.

45304.extract: Methylion was a town and polis (city-state) in ancient Thessaly. Methylion minted coins dated to the 5th and 4th centuries BCE with the legends «ΜΕΘΥ» and «ΜΕΘΥΛΙΕΩΝ». In addition, there is epigraphic evidence dated to 230-220 BCE of a theorodokos of Methylion. Stephanus of Byzantium refers to the town as Methydrion (Μεθύδριον).
45304.romanEraExtract: Methylion was a town and polis (city-state) in ancient Thessaly. Methylion minted coins dated to the 5th and 4th centuries BCE with the legends «ΜΕΘΥ» and «ΜΕΘΥΛΙΕΩΝ». In addition, there is epigraphic evidence dated to 230-220 BCE of a theorodokos of Methylion. Stephanus of Byzantium refers to the town as Methydrion (Μεθύδριον).

45308.extract: Ekkara is a village and a community in the municipal unit Thessaliotida, Phthiotis, Greece. The population in 2011 was 522 for the community, which includes the village Ano Agoriani. Ekkara is situated at the southern end of the Thessalian Plain, near the border of the Karditsa regional unit. The Piraeus–Platy railway passes south of the village. Ekkara is located 2 km southeast of Gavrakia, 4 km west of Velesiotes, 10 km northwest of Domokos, 33 km southeast of Karditsa and 36 km northwest of Lamia. There are farmlands in the plains to the north, while there are some forests in the hills to the south. The majority of the population works in agriculture.
45308.romanEraExtract: Ekkara is a village and a community in the municipal unit Thessaliotida, Phthiotis, Greece. The population in 2011 was 522 for the community, which includes the village Ano Agoriani. Ekkara is situated at the southern end of the Thessalian Plain, near the border of the Karditsa regional unit. The Pi...

45324.extract: Oichalia Greek: Οιχαλία, before 1981: Νεοχώρι - Neochori) is a town and a former municipality in the Trikala regional unit, Thessaly, Greece. Since the 2011 local government reform it is part of the municipality Farkadona, of which it is a municipal unit. The municipal unit has an area of 85.628 km2. Oichalia is located 8 km west of Farkadona centre, and 20 km east of the city of Trikala. It is situated on the edge of the Thessalian Plain near the ancient city of Oechalia. The Greek National Road 6 passes south of Oichalia.
45324.romanEraExtract: Oichalia Greek: Οιχαλία, before 1981: Νεοχώρι - Neochori) is a town and a former municipality in the Trikala regional unit, Thessaly, Greece. Since the 2011 local government reform it is part of the municipality Farkadona, of which it is a municipal unit. The municipal unit has an area of 85.628 km2...

21949.extract: Delphi, in legend previously called Pytho, was an ancient sacred precinct in central Greece. It was the seat of Pythia, the major oracle who was consulted about important decisions throughout the ancient classical world. The ancient Greeks considered the centre of the world to be in Delphi, marked by the stone monument known as the Omphalos of Delphi.
21949.romanEraExtract: Delphi was the most important oracle site in the ancient Greek world, sacred to Apollo, where the Pythia delivered prophecies that shaped the decisions of city-states and kings. The Romans took control of Delphi in 191 BC. The site was sacked by Lucius Cornelius Sulla in 86 BC during the Mithridatic Wars and later visited by Nero in AD 66. Despite restoration efforts by later emperors, the sanctuary gradually lost its former prominence. The oracle was permanently shut down in AD 381 when Emperor Theodosius I prohibited pagan practices. Today the extensive archaeological site is a UNESCO World Heritage Site within Parnassos National Park.

22760.extract: Marathon is a town in Greece and the site of the Battle of Marathon in 490 BC, in which the outnumbered Athenian army defeated the Persians. Legend has it that Pheidippides, a Greek herald, was sent running from Marathon to Athens to announce the victory, inspiring the modern marathon race.
22760.romanEraExtract: Marathon is best known as the site of the Battle of Marathon in 490 BC, where the Athenian army and a small Plataean force decisively defeated a Persian invasion commanded by Datis and Artaphernes. The Greek victory demonstrated that Persian forces could be beaten, boosted Greek confidence, and launched Athens into its golden age. The battlefield and its burial mound (Soros) became a place of commemoration throughout antiquity, and the town remained inhabited through the Roman period as part of the province of Achaea.

21166.extract: Miletus was an influential ancient Greek city on the western coast of Anatolia, near the mouth of the Maeander River in present-day Turkey. Renowned for its wealth, maritime power, and extensive network of colonies, Miletus was a major center of trade, culture, and innovation from the Bronze Age through the Roman period.
21166.romanEraExtract: Miletus was a major Greek city on the western coast of Anatolia and the birthplace of Western philosophy — home to Thales, Anaximander, and Anaximenes of the Milesian school, who pioneered rational inquiry in the 6th century BC. The city became part of the Roman province of Asia in 133 BC and flourished under Roman administration. Most of its surviving monuments date to the Roman period, including the grand Theatre, the Baths of Faustina, and the Market Gate (now in Berlin's Pergamon Museum). The Apostle Paul visited Miletus around AD 57, meeting with Ephesian church elders as recorded in Acts.

21137.extract: Memphis was the ancient capital of Inebu-hedj, the first nome of Lower Egypt. Its ruins are located near the present-day village of Mit Rahina, Giza Governorate, Egypt.
21137.romanEraExtract: Memphis was the ancient capital of Lower Egypt and one of the most important cities of the ancient world for over three millennia. Under the Ptolemies, Memphis was eclipsed by the new capital Alexandria but remained a significant religious center. With the Roman conquest, Memphis lost its place permanently in favor of Alexandria, which connected Egypt to the wider empire. The rise of the cult of Serapis and the emergence of Christianity spelled the complete ruin of the ancient cults of Memphis, leading to its abandonment by late antiquity. Its ruins near modern Mit Rahina include colossal statues and the remains of the great Temple of Ptah.

22328.extract: Babylon was an ancient city located on the lower Euphrates river in southern Mesopotamia, within modern-day Hillah, Iraq. Babylon functioned as the main cultural and political centre of Babylonia and was one of the most important urban centres of the ancient Near East.
22328.romanEraExtract: Babylon was an ancient city on the Euphrates in Mesopotamia, one of the most renowned cities of the ancient world and capital of the Old Babylonian and Neo-Babylonian empires. Alexander the Great captured Babylon in 331 BC and it briefly flourished again as a center of learning and commerce, but after his death in the city in 323 BC, it passed to the Seleucid Empire and gradually declined. Under the Parthians, Babylon became a provincial center. The Romans under Trajan briefly captured it in AD 116, but Hadrian withdrew to the Euphrates frontier. The site, now a UNESCO World Heritage Site near modern Hillah, Iraq, preserves the foundations of its ancient temples and palaces.

21952.extract: Thebes is a city in Boeotia, Central Greece, and one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world. It is the largest city in Boeotia and a major center for the region.
21952.romanEraExtract: Thebes (Thebai) was one of the greatest cities of ancient Greece and held profound mythological significance as the legendary birthplace of Dionysus, Heracles, and Oedipus, with a foundation legend centered on the Phoenician prince Cadmus. The city rose to political dominance under Epaminondas, defeating Sparta at the Battle of Leuctra in 371 BC. After an unsuccessful revolt, Alexander the Great destroyed Thebes in 335 BC and sold its inhabitants into slavery, though he spared temples and the house of the poet Pindar. Cassander restored the city around 315 BC. Thebes fell under Roman control after 146 BC and was incorporated into the Province of Achaea in 27 BC.

21564.extract: Amathus was one of the ancient royal cities of Cyprus until about 300 BC. Its ancient cult sanctuary of Aphrodite was the second most important in Cyprus, after Paphos.
21564.romanEraExtract: Amathous was one of the ancient royal cities of Cyprus, renowned for its sanctuary of Aphrodite, the second most important on the island after Paphos. The term 'Amathusia' became synonymous with 'Cypriot' in Roman literature, testifying to the city's lasting prestige. In the Roman era, Amathous became the capital of one of the four administrative regions of Cyprus, and a new temple was constructed in the 1st century AD atop earlier Hellenistic foundations. The remains of the city, including the acropolis and the port, are visible on the southern coast of Cyprus near modern Limassol.

22885.extract: Kamarina or Camarina was an ancient city on the southern coast of Sicily in Magna Graecia. The ruins and an archaeological museum are located south of the modern town of Scoglitti.
22885.romanEraExtract: Camarina was a Greek colony on the southern coast of Sicily, founded by Syracuse in 599 BC. The city experienced repeated destruction and refoundation throughout its turbulent history. During the First Punic War, Camarina fell to the Carthaginians in 259 BC, then came under Roman control in 258 BC following the Battle of Camarina. The settlement was largely depopulated by the time of the geographer Strabo in the 1st century BC. Its archaeological site near modern Scoglitti preserves remains from its Greek and Roman periods.

22750.extract: Lamia is a city in central Greece, the capital of the regional unit of Phthiotis. The city dates back to antiquity and has been continuously inhabited since the Bronze Age.
22750.romanEraExtract: Lamia was a strategically important ancient city controlling the narrow coastal plain above Thermopylae that connected southern Greece with Thessaly and the Balkans. During the Lamian War (323-322 BC), the Macedonian regent Antipater took refuge behind Lamia's fortifications after Alexander the Great's death. The city flourished under the Aetolian League in the 3rd century BC until the Roman general Manius Acilius Glabrio sacked it in 190 BC. Under Roman rule, Lamia continued as a regional center in the province of Achaea.

21904.extract: Dion is a village and archaeological site in Pieria, Greece, located at the foot of Mount Olympus. It was the primary Macedonian sanctuary dedicated to Zeus.
21904.romanEraExtract: Dion was the sacred city of the Macedonians, located at the foot of Mount Olympus and dedicated to Zeus. Macedonian kings performed annual sacrifices there, and Alexander the Great assembled his armies and performed magnificent sacrifices at Dion on the eve of his campaign to Asia in 334 BC. During the Roman period, Octavian established a Roman colony at Dion around 32/31 BC, and the city flourished again under 2nd and 3rd-century emperors who admired Alexander. The extensive archaeological site preserves temples, baths, a theater, and remarkable mosaic floors from both the Macedonian and Roman periods.

21962.extract: Olynthus is an ancient city in Chalcidice, Greece. It was built on two flat-topped hills in a fertile plain at the head of the Gulf of Torone, near the neck of the peninsula of Pallene.
21962.romanEraExtract: Olynthos was the leading city of the Chalcidian League from 432 BC onward, a major political and military center in northern Greece that at one point controlled territory including Pella, Macedon's chief city. The Athenian orator Demosthenes delivered his famous Olynthiac orations urging Athens to defend the city against Philip II of Macedon. In 348 BC, Philip besieged and destroyed Olynthos, selling its population into slavery after its leaders betrayed the city. The catastrophic destruction preserved the city's Classical-era urban plan, making it one of the most important archaeological sites for understanding everyday life in ancient Greece.

16728.extract: Velia was the Roman name of an ancient city on the coast of the Tyrrhenian Sea, near the modern village of Ascea in the Province of Salerno, Italy.
16728.romanEraExtract: Velia (Greek: Elea) was a Greek colony in southern Italy, famous as the home of the Eleatic school of philosophy founded by Parmenides in the 5th century BC. His student Zeno of Elea gained fame for his paradoxes challenging conventional thinking about motion and infinity. The city joined an alliance with Rome in 273 BC and supplied naval vessels during the Punic Wars. Velia became a Roman municipium in 88 BC while retaining the right to maintain the Greek language and mint its own coins. During the imperial period it was a popular resort for Roman aristocrats.

21608.extract: Utica was an ancient Phoenician and Carthaginian city near the outflow of the Medjerda River into the Mediterranean, traditionally considered the first Phoenician colony in North Africa.
21608.romanEraExtract: Utica was traditionally the first Phoenician colony in North Africa, founded even before Carthage. After Carthage's defeat in the Third Punic War (146 BC), Utica became the capital of the newly created Roman province of Africa, with the governor's residence and garrison stationed there. The city's prominence gradually diminished when Augustus moved the provincial capital to rebuilt Carthage, though Utica remained a major city and eventually achieved full colony status under Septimius Severus. Its archaeological remains lie near modern Henchir-bou-Chateur in Tunisia.

21890.extract: Edessa was an ancient city in Upper Mesopotamia, in what is now Şanlıurfa, Turkey. Founded during the Hellenistic period by Seleucus I Nicator, it later became capital of the Kingdom of Osroene.
21890.romanEraExtract: Edessa was a major city in Upper Mesopotamia (modern Şanlıurfa, Turkey), founded by Seleucus I Nicator and named after the Macedonian capital. It became the capital of the Kingdom of Osroene around the mid-2nd century BC. The Romans began exerting influence from 69 BC, and Edessa became a Roman colonia in AD 212-213. It developed into a prominent center of early Christianity, hosting the Catechetical School of Edessa. The Battle of Edessa in AD 260 was a historic disaster for Rome when the Sasanian emperor Shapur I defeated and captured Emperor Valerian alive. During the Crusades, Edessa served as the capital of the County of Edessa.

21170.extract: Colophon was an ancient city in Ionia, founded around the end of the 2nd millennium BC. It was likely one of the oldest of the twelve cities of the Ionian League.
21170.romanEraExtract: Colophon was one of the oldest and initially strongest of the twelve cities of the Ionian League, renowned for its cavalry and the inhabitants' luxurious lifestyle. It was conquered by Gyges of Lydia in the 7th century BC and later came under Persian, Athenian, and Seleucid control. Lysimachus destroyed the city in the 3rd century BC, and its importance was gradually transferred to the nearby port settlement of Notium. The city was home to the elegiac poet Mimnermus and was traditionally claimed as a birthplace of Homer.

22701.extract: Mende was an ancient Greek city on the western coast of the Pallene peninsula in Chalcidice, near the modern town of Kalandra.
22701.romanEraExtract: Mende was an ancient Greek city on the Pallene peninsula in Chalcidice, founded around the 9th century BC by Eretrian colonists. It became renowned for its wine — the celebrated Mendaeos oinos — and was one of the cities controlling trade routes along the coast of Thrace. Mende joined the Athenian Delian League and was a strategic prize during the Peloponnesian War. The city was depopulated around 315 BC when King Cassander forced its inhabitants to resettle in his newly built city of Cassandreia.

21381.extract: Apollonia Pontica (modern Sozopol, Bulgaria) was one of the largest and richest Greek colonies on the Black Sea coast, founded in the 7th century BC by colonists from Miletus.
21381.romanEraExtract: Apollonia Pontica (modern Sozopol, Bulgaria) was one of the largest and richest Greek colonies on the Black Sea, founded in the 7th century BC by colonists from Miletus. Initially called Antheia, it was renamed Apollonia after a temple to Apollo that housed a colossal 13-meter statue by the sculptor Calamis, one of the wonders of the ancient world. In 72 BC, Marcus Lucullus conquered the city and transported the Apollo statue to Rome. Apollonia never recovered from the devastation, remaining a small town without significant trade or cultural importance during the Roman period.

23115.extract: Acraephia was a town of ancient Boeotia on the slope of Mount Ptoum and on the eastern bank of Lake Copais.
23115.romanEraExtract: Akraiphiai (Acraephia) was a town of ancient Boeotia on the slope of Mount Ptoum and on the eastern bank of Lake Copais. It was closely connected with the nearby oracle of Apollo Ptoios, one of the oldest oracular shrines in Boeotia. The town persisted through the classical and Hellenistic periods and continued into Roman times as part of the province of Achaea.

22220.extract: Kysis (modern Dush) was a Ptolemaic and Roman fortress in Egypt's Western Desert within the Kharga Oasis.
22220.romanEraExtract: Kysis (modern Qasr Dush) was a Ptolemaic and Roman fortress in Egypt's Western Desert within the Kharga Oasis, strategically positioned at a convergence of ancient desert trade routes. The fortress was built under the Ptolemies and expanded by Roman emperors Domitian, Trajan, and Hadrian. The site preserves two sandstone temples, multiple cemeteries, and numerous pottery fragments. Third-century papyri discovered here have provided important evidence for life on Rome's desert frontier.

22744.extract: Atrax was a Perrhaebian town in ancient Thessaly, situated above the river Peneius, at a distance of about 10 miles from Larissa.
22744.romanEraExtract: Atrax was a Perrhaebian town in ancient Thessaly, strategically situated above the river Peneius about 10 miles from Larissa. Described by Livy as a significant fortified settlement, Atrax played a role in the conflicts between Rome and Macedon. The town continued as a settlement through the Roman period within the province of Thessaly.

22780.extract: Styra was a town of ancient Euboea, on the west coast, north of Carystus and nearly opposite the promontory of Cynosura in Attica.
22780.romanEraExtract: Styra was a town of ancient Euboea on the western coast, north of Carystus and nearly opposite the Attic promontory of Cynosura. It contributed ships to the Greek fleet at the Battle of Artemisium and the Battle of Salamis in 480 BC. The settlement continued through the Hellenistic and Roman periods as part of the island of Euboea.

21925.extract: Hermione was a town at the southern extremity of Argolis, an independent city during the Classical period of Greek history.
21925.romanEraExtract: Hermione was an independent Greek city at the southern extremity of Argolis, with a territory known as Hermionis. The city was noted for its sanctuary of Demeter Chthonia and was believed to possess a shortcut to the underworld, eliminating the need to pay Charon's fare for the dead. Hermione participated in the Persian Wars and maintained its independence through the Hellenistic period before incorporation into the Roman province of Achaea.

21217.extract: Lysimachia was an important Hellenistic Greek town on the north-western extremity of the Thracian Chersonese, near the neck where the peninsula joins the mainland.
21217.romanEraExtract: Lysimacheia was a major Hellenistic city founded in 309 BC by King Lysimachus on the Thracian Chersonese (modern Gallipoli peninsula) to command strategic trade routes. Lysimachus made it the capital of his kingdom, and it rapidly rose to great splendor. After his death, the city passed through Seleucid and Ptolemaic control before declining under Roman rule. Emperor Justinian restored it as a fortified outpost known as Hexamilion in the 6th century AD.

22706.extract: Tharros was an ancient city on the west coast of Sardinia, Italy. It is currently an archaeological site near the village of San Giovanni di Sinis.
22706.romanEraExtract: Tharros was an ancient city on Sardinia's west coast, originally settled by Nuragic peoples in the Bronze Age before Phoenicians established it as a major port in the 8th century BC. Under Punic and Roman rule, it remained one of the most important places on the island, serving as a significant port and administrative center. The archaeological site, now an open-air museum, preserves the tophet, bath installations, temple foundations, and a residential quarter with artisan workshops. Artifacts from Tharros are housed in museums in Cagliari, Cabras, and London's British Museum.

21992.extract: Stratos was the capital of ancient Acarnania, located on the right bank of the river Achelous in west-central Greece.
21992.romanEraExtract: Stratos was the largest and best-fortified city of ancient Acarnania, strategically located on the right bank of the river Achelous. Its position on the fertile river plain and the strategic maritime route to Italy, combined with the navigability of the river up to the city, made it a place of great military importance. Stratos served as the capital of the Acarnanian federal state where judicial proceedings and assemblies were held. The city's main monuments include an unfinished Temple of Zeus (begun 321 BC), a theater, and an agora. With the founding of Nicopolis by Octavian in 28 BC, much of the population was forced to relocate, and Stratos fell into decline.

21210.extract: Soli, often rendered Soli/Pompeiopolis, was an ancient city and port in Cilicia, 11 km west of Mersin in present-day Turkey.
21210.romanEraExtract: Soloi was an ancient city and port in Cilicia, founded by Achaean and Rhodian colonists around 700-690 BC. The English word 'solecism' — meaning a grammatical error — derives from the city, whose Greek dialect was considered a corrupted form of Attic Greek. When Pompey the Great subdued the Cilician pirates in 67 BC, he resettled surrendered pirates in the depopulated city and renamed it Pompeiopolis. Under Roman rule, the city flourished with an expanded harbor, theater, baths, and a colonnade still partially visible today.

21403.extract: Celenderis was an ancient port city in Cilicia Tracheia on the Mediterranean coast of modern Turkey, near present-day Aydıncık.
21403.romanEraExtract: Celenderis was an ancient port city in Rough Cilicia (Cilicia Tracheia) on the Mediterranean coast of modern Turkey, near present-day Aydıncık. Founded as a Greek colony, it served as an important harbor along the southern Anatolian coast. The city was incorporated into the Roman province of Cilicia and continued to function as a port through the imperial period and into late antiquity.

23494.extract: Merv was a major city in Central Asia, located on the historical Silk Road, near today's Mary, Turkmenistan.
23494.romanEraExtract: Alexandria Margiana (later known as Merv) was one of the many cities founded by Alexander the Great, located at the crossroads of the Silk Road in Central Asia near modern Mary, Turkmenistan. Under the Achaemenid Empire it had been the center of the satrapy of Margiana. The city was subsequently ruled by Hellenistic kings and the Parthian Empire, serving as a major eastern terminus of the trade networks that connected Rome with Central Asia and China. Human settlement at Merv spans from the 3rd millennium BC to the 18th century AD.

22640.extract: Enna, known in antiquity as Henna, is a city located roughly at the center of Sicily, earning the nickname 'navel of Sicily'.
22640.romanEraExtract: Henna (modern Enna) was a strategically important city at the center of Sicily, renowned as the sacred center of the cult of Demeter (Roman Ceres), whose grove was known as the umbilicus Siciliae — the 'navel of Sicily'. The myth of Persephone's abduction by Hades was traditionally set at nearby Lake Pergusa. During the First Servile War (134-132 BC), enslaved people under the leader Eunus seized control of Henna, making it the last stronghold to hold out against the Roman proconsul Rupilius. The city remained an important inland center throughout the Roman period.

22341.extract: Dara was an important East Roman fortress city in northern Mesopotamia on the border with the Sassanid Empire.
22341.romanEraExtract: Dara was an important East Roman fortress city in northern Mesopotamia, built in AD 505 by Emperor Anastasius I as a strategic counterweight to the Sasanian-held city of Nisibis just 18 km away. The fortress provided refuge for the Roman army and preparation for military campaigns against Persia. Under Justinian I, Dara was extensively rebuilt with reinforced walls, strengthened towers, and an innovative water management system featuring a diverted river and arch dam. The city featured prominently in Roman-Persian conflicts, hosting major battles in AD 530, 540, 544, 573, and 604 before falling to the Arab conquest in 639.

21870.extract: The Siwa Oasis is situated in Egypt's Western Desert, famed for its role as the home of the Oracle of Amun.
21870.romanEraExtract: The Oracle of Ammon at Siwa Oasis was one of the most revered oracles of the ancient world, rivaling Delphi in prestige. Alexander the Great famously made a dangerous journey across the desert to consult the oracle in 331 BC, where the priests reportedly declared him the son of Zeus-Ammon. The oasis remained a significant pilgrimage destination through the Ptolemaic and Roman periods. Located in Egypt's Western Desert between the Qattara Depression and the Great Sand Sea, the ruins of the oracle temple at Aghurmi remain a popular archaeological attraction today.

21386.extract: Montana is a town in northwestern Bulgaria, administrative centre of Montana Province.
21386.romanEraExtract: Montana (ancient Civitas Montanensium) was a significant Roman military and civilian center in the province of Moesia Inferior in northwestern Bulgaria. The settlement acquired city rights around AD 160 and became the second most important city in the province after Ratiaria. Its economy was based on agriculture, gold mining, and trade, with a Romanized local population alongside settlers from Italy and Anatolia.

16579.extract: Amelia (ancient Ameria) is a town in central Italy, part of the province of Terni in Umbria.
16579.romanEraExtract: Ameria (modern Amelia) is one of the oldest towns in Umbria, Italy, famous for its massive pre-Roman polygonal walls dating to the 6th-4th centuries BC — limestone blocks exceeding 2 meters cut with great precision into irregular polygons and stacked without mortar. The city became an important Roman center after the Via Amerina opened in 240 BC. During the Second Punic War, troops from Ameria fought to oppose Hannibal's advance. It became a prosperous Roman municipium, and Cicero defended a citizen of Ameria, Sextus Roscius, in one of his most famous legal orations.

27291.extract: Adulis was an ancient city and port along the Red Sea in the Gulf of Zula, about 40 km south of Massawa in modern Eritrea.
27291.romanEraExtract: Adulis was a major ancient port city on the Red Sea coast of modern Eritrea, serving as the primary commercial hub of the Kingdom of Aksum. It was a vital emporium for trade in ivory, hides, slaves, and other goods from the African interior. Roman merchants actively used the port during the 2nd and 3rd centuries AD, and the Periplus of the Erythraean Sea describes it as an important stop on the Indian Ocean trade route. The city declined after the 7th century AD with the rise of Islam and shifting trade routes.

21718.extract: Udhruh is a town in southern Jordan, 15 km east of Petra, site of a major Roman fortress.
21718.romanEraExtract: Adrou (modern Udhruh, known to the Romans as Augustopolis) was a Roman fortress in southern Jordan, 15 km east of Petra. The fort was likely built after Rome annexed the Nabataean Kingdom in AD 106. In the late 3rd or early 4th century, the Legio VI Ferrata was headquartered at Udhruh, and the fortress was reconstructed around AD 303-304. It served as an important military outpost on the eastern frontier of the province of Arabia Petraea.

13913.extract: Aequum was a Roman colony near modern Čitluk, Croatia, founded by Emperor Claudius after AD 45.
13913.romanEraExtract: Aequum was the only Roman colony in the interior of the province of Dalmatia, founded by Emperor Claudius after AD 45 and settled with veterans of Legio VII Claudia Pia Fidelis near modern Čitluk, Croatia. The planned city covered 32 hectares and served as an administrative, cultural, religious, and social hub for the surrounding region. Archaeological discoveries at the partially excavated site include forum remains, fortification towers, and notable marble statuary fragments.

22452.extract: Ammaia was a Roman city in Lusitania, now an archaeological site near São Salvador da Aramenha in the municipality of Marvão, Portugal.
22452.romanEraExtract: Ammaia was a Roman city in the province of Lusitania, located in the mountainous Serra de São Mamede near the modern Portuguese-Spanish border. The city received the status of a civitas in the 1st century AD and grew into a prosperous settlement with a forum, baths, and temples. Its well-preserved ruins within the Natural Park of the Serra de São Mamede include visible city walls, a monumental gateway, and a forum area. Archaeological research has revealed a planned Roman urban center that flourished until the 5th century AD.

21705.extract: Clysma was an ancient city at the head of the Gulf of Suez in Egypt.
21705.romanEraExtract: Klysma (Clysma) was a Roman port city strategically positioned at the head of the Gulf of Suez in Egypt, founded by Emperor Trajan in the 2nd century AD. It served as a crucial hub for trade along routes connecting Sinai, Palestine, and Egypt. The city's importance was enhanced by its connection to the Amnis Traianus — a canal linking the Nile to the Red Sea with its outlet near Clysma — making it ideal for exporting textiles and grain. Trade peaked during the 4th and 5th centuries after Emperor Diocletian disrupted rival southern ports.

23388.extract: Berenice Troglodytica is an ancient seaport of Egypt on the western shore of the Red Sea, founded in 275 BC by Ptolemy II Philadelphus.
23388.romanEraExtract: Berenike was a major Red Sea trade port in Egypt, founded in 275 BC by Ptolemy II and named after his mother. From the 1st to 2nd centuries AD, it was a critical hub connecting Rome with India, Arabia, and East Africa, facilitating trade in spices, textiles, gemstones, and exotic goods. Archaeologists recovered the largest single concentration of black peppercorns (7.55 kg) ever found in the classical Mediterranean, along with Tamil Brahmi script, Indian ceramics, and a marble Buddha statue in an Isis temple. The site also yielded what may be the world's oldest pet cemetery, with some 600 animal remains dating to the 1st-2nd centuries AD.

15297.extract: Fermo (ancient Firmum Picenum) is a town in the Marche region of Italy, in the Province of Fermo.
15297.romanEraExtract: Firmum Picenum (modern Fermo) was a Roman colony founded in 264 BC in the Marche region of Italy on the Adriatic coast. It served as a strategic stronghold for Roman control of the Picenum territory and contributed soldiers during the Punic Wars. The city grew into a prosperous municipium and retains substantial Roman remains including underground cisterns (piscinae), one of the best-preserved Roman water systems in Italy.

21771.extract: Lixus is an ancient city founded by Phoenicians before the city of Carthage, continuously occupied from antiquity to the Islamic Era.
21771.romanEraExtract: Lixus was one of the earliest Phoenician settlements in the western Mediterranean, founded on Morocco's Atlantic coast in the 8th-7th century BC, even before Carthage. After Carthage's fall, it transitioned to Roman control as part of Mauretania Tingitana, becoming a flourishing Roman colony under Claudius around AD 50. Fishing and viticulture were the city's main economic resources, with fish products packaged in amphorae for international trade. The archaeological site preserves a Roman amphitheater, forum, a monumental temple quarter spanning 165 by 250 meters, and remains from Phoenician through Islamic periods.

21185.extract: Stratonicea was one of the most important towns in the interior of ancient Caria, Anatolia.
21185.romanEraExtract: Stratonikeia was a major city in the interior of ancient Caria (modern southwestern Turkey), founded by the Seleucid king Antiochus I Soter and named after his wife Stratonice. The city incorporated the nearby sanctuary of Hecate at Lagina, where residents made annual religious processions to a monumental temple. Under Roman rule, Stratonikeia earned the favor of Augustus and the Roman Senate by successfully resisting an attack by Quintus Labienus and his Parthian forces in 40 BC. The well-preserved archaeological site at modern Eskihisar includes a 10,000-seat theater, city gates, and extensive Roman-era remains.

21856.extract: Barca was an ancient city located at the site of modern Marj in northeastern Libya.
21856.romanEraExtract: Barke (Latin: Barca) was an ancient Greek colony in Cyrenaica (modern northeastern Libya), founded around 560 BC as a daughter city of Cyrene. It became a significant settlement in the Pentapolis, the five major Greek cities of the region. Under Roman rule, the city was incorporated into the province of Creta et Cyrenaica and continued as a bishopric. Its site at modern Marj preserves traces of the ancient settlement.

21240.extract: Comana was a city of Cappadocia famed for its temple of the goddess Ma (Enyo/Bellona).
21240.romanEraExtract: Comana was an ancient city in Cappadocia, famous for its magnificent temple dedicated to the goddess Ma (identified with Roman Bellona). The temple was a religious and economic powerhouse, with upwards of 6,000 hierodouloi (temple slaves) serving the sanctuary even in Strabo's time, supporting vast estates that generated enormous revenue. Emperor Caracalla elevated Comana to the status of a Roman colony. Strabo described the city as situated in a deep valley on the Sarus river, emphasizing its strategic location and the temple's ceremonial grandeur.

16458.extract: Chersonesus was an ancient Greek colony founded approximately 2,500 years ago in the southwestern part of the Crimean Peninsula.
16458.romanEraExtract: Chersonesos was an ancient Greek colony in the southwestern Crimean Peninsula, founded by settlers from Heraclea Pontica around 422 BC. The city came under Roman influence and received a garrison from the mid-1st century BC until the 370s AD, serving as a key Roman outpost on the Black Sea. UNESCO designated the archaeological site a World Heritage Site in 2013 for its evidence of cultural exchange between Greeks, Romans, and Byzantines. The extensive ruins include a Roman amphitheater, Greek temples, and early Christian basilicas.

21171.extract: Klazomenai was one of the 12 cities of ancient Ionia on the south coast of the Gulf of Smyrna.
21171.romanEraExtract: Klazomenai was one of the twelve cities of the Ionian League, located on the southern coast of the Gulf of Smyrna in western Anatolia (modern Urla near İzmir, Turkey). It was one of the first cities to issue silver coinage and was the birthplace of the philosopher Anaxagoras, who taught in Athens. The city was incorporated into the Roman province of Asia and continued as a settlement through the imperial period. Its ruins near modern Urla preserve remains from multiple periods of occupation.

21149.extract: Kayseri, historically known as Caesarea, is a large city in Central Anatolia and the historical capital of Cappadocia.
21149.romanEraExtract: Caesarea of Cappadocia (originally Mazaca, modern Kayseri) was the capital of the Cappadocian kingdom and a crucial trade hub at the foot of Mount Argaeus (Erciyes). King Archelaus renamed it Caesarea in AD 14 to honor Augustus. Under Roman and Byzantine rule, the city housed approximately 50,000 inhabitants and became the highest-ranked diocese up to the Council of Chalcedon. Saint Basil of Caesarea, one of the Cappadocian Fathers, established the Basiliad — a major charitable complex — making it a premier center of early Christianity.

21757.extract: Avella (ancient Abella) is a town in the province of Avellino, Campania, southern Italy.
21757.romanEraExtract: Abella (modern Avella) was an ancient Italic town in Campania, southern Italy, known since antiquity for its hazelnut cultivation — the scientific name of the common hazelnut, Corylus avellana, derives from this town. The city was originally an Oscan-speaking settlement that came under Roman control during the Samnite Wars. The famous Cippus Abellanus, a boundary stone inscribed in the Oscan language, was discovered here and is one of the most important documents of ancient Italic languages.

5890.extract: Penne (ancient Pinna) is a town in the province of Pescara, in the Abruzzo region of Italy.
5890.romanEraExtract: Pinna (modern Penne) was an ancient Italic town of the Vestini people in Abruzzo, central Italy. It became a Roman ally and later a municipium, serving as a local administrative center. The hilltop town's strategic position overlooking the surrounding territory made it an important center in the Roman period. Today it is recognized as one of Italy's most beautiful historic villages (I Borghi più belli d'Italia).

22248.extract: Stuberra (also Stymbara) was a town on the frontier of ancient Macedonia, assigned to either Deuriopus or Pelagonia.
22248.romanEraExtract: Stuberra (also Stymbara) was a frontier town in ancient Macedonia in the region between Deuriopus and Pelagonia (modern North Macedonia). In 200 BC it served as the third encampment of the Roman consul Sulpicius during the First Macedonian War. The site was also the scene of action during the Third Macedonian War. The archaeological site near modern Chepigovo preserves remains from the Macedonian and Roman periods.

21959.extract: Bylazora was a Paeonian city from the period of early classic antiquity, near modern Knezhje in North Macedonia.
21959.romanEraExtract: Bylazora was the largest Paeonian city in antiquity, strategically located in the Vardar river valley in modern North Macedonia. It was mentioned by the Roman historian Livy as the place where the Roman army camped during the Third Macedonian War against King Perseus in 168 BC. After the Roman conquest, the city continued as a settlement in the province of Macedonia. The archaeological site near the village of Knezhje has revealed fortifications, a monumental palace, and artifacts spanning from the 7th century BC to the Roman period.

21345.extract: Saturnia is a spa town in Tuscany, central Italy, inhabited since ancient times.
21345.romanEraExtract: Saturnia (ancient Aurinia) is one of the oldest settlements in Tuscany, first occupied by Pelasgi and then by the Etruscans according to Dionysius of Halicarnassus. A Roman colony was established in 183 BC, making it a prefecture within the Roman system. Remains of the ancient city walls in the polygonal style survive, along with Roman gateways, urban structures, and tombs. The town is also famous for its natural hot springs, which have attracted visitors since antiquity.

22563.extract: Cori (ancient Cora) is a city in the province of Latina, Lazio, central Italy.
22563.romanEraExtract: Cora (modern Cori) was an ancient Latin city in Lazio that was part of the Latin League. In 503 BC it sought unsuccessfully to revolt against Rome. After the Social War (90-88 BC), Cora became a Roman municipium. The town preserves remarkable ancient monuments including the Temple of Hercules (early 1st century BC, now a national monument) and remains of the Temple of Castor and Pollux (a 1st-century BC restoration of a 5th-century BC sanctuary). Its impressive polygonal masonry walls and Roman-era Ponte della Catena survive.

22713.extract: Potenza (ancient Potentia) is a city in the region of Basilicata, southern Italy.
22713.romanEraExtract: Potentia (modern Potenza) was an ancient Lucanian city that became a Roman municipium after the Social War. Located at the highest regional capital in Italy (819 m elevation), it served as a strategic center in Basilicata. Under the Roman Empire, the city was a stopping point along the Via Herculia connecting the Appia and Traiana roads. It remained occupied through late antiquity and the medieval period.

21443.extract: Laodicea Combusta was a Hellenistic city in central Anatolia, in the region of Pisidia.
21443.romanEraExtract: Laodikeia Katakekaumene (Laodicea Combusta, 'the Burnt') was a Hellenistic city in central Anatolia near modern Ladik, Konya Province, Turkey. Its epithet 'Burnt' referred to the volcanic landscape of the surrounding Lycaonian plateau. Under Roman rule, it was renamed Claudiolaodicea in honor of Emperor Claudius and served as a regional center in the province of Galatia. The city continued as a bishopric in the Byzantine period.

21090.extract: Petra, originally known as Raqmu, is an ancient city and archaeological site in southern Jordan. Famous for its rock-cut architecture and water conduit systems, it is one of the New Seven Wonders of the World and a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
21090.romanEraExtract: Petra was the capital of the Nabataean Kingdom, a wealthy trading state that controlled caravan routes connecting Arabia, Egypt, and the Mediterranean. Famous for its stunning rock-cut architecture carved from rose-colored sandstone, Petra became known as the 'Rose City'. Rome annexed the Nabataean Kingdom in AD 106, incorporating it into the province of Arabia Petraea. The city continued to flourish under Roman rule with new monumental buildings, but gradually declined as Palmyra grew in commercial importance and maritime trade routes drew away the caravan commerce that had sustained Petra's prosperity. A major earthquake in AD 363 devastated much of the city.

21116.extract: Tyre is a city in Lebanon and one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world. It was one of the earliest Phoenician metropolises and the legendary birthplace of Europa and the founder of Carthage.
21116.romanEraExtract: Tyre (Tyrus) was the greatest of the Phoenician city-states, renowned throughout antiquity for its purple dye industry, maritime commerce, and as the legendary birthplace of Europa and Dido, founder of Carthage. Alexander the Great famously besieged the island city in 332 BC by constructing a causeway, permanently changing its geography. Under Roman rule, Tyre received monumental buildings including a famous hippodrome, one of the largest in the Roman world. The city was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1984, with extensive Roman and Byzantine ruins including the hippodrome, a colonnaded road, a necropolis, and an aqueduct.

22335.extract: Ur was a major Sumerian city-state in ancient Mesopotamia, located at the site of modern Tell el-Muqayyar in Iraq.
22335.romanEraExtract: Ur was one of the most important cities of ancient Sumer, famous for its massive Ziggurat built during the reign of Ur-Nammu in the 21st century BC and later restored by the Neo-Babylonian king Nabonidus. The city reached its greatest extent during the Third Dynasty of Ur (c. 2112-2004 BC) when it served as the capital of a vast empire. Sir Leonard Woolley's excavations (1922-1934) uncovered approximately 1,850 burials including 16 royal tombs with spectacular artifacts like the Standard of Ur. The city declined around 530 BC following Persia's conquest of Babylonia and was abandoned by the early 5th century BC due to drought and changing river patterns.

21659.extract: Hippos (Sussita) was an ancient Greco-Roman city and member of the Decapolis, located on a hill east of the Sea of Galilee.
21659.romanEraExtract: Hippos (Aramaic: Sussita) was a Greco-Roman city of the Decapolis, founded in the mid-2nd century BC as Antioch of Hippos on a dramatic hilltop 2 km east of the Sea of Galilee. Under Roman rule, the city became an important urban center with a Kalybe (imperial shrine), theater, odeon, basilica, and new city walls. Hippos later became a predominantly Christian city and seat of a bishopric before being devastated by the earthquake of AD 749. Ongoing excavations have revealed a remarkably well-preserved classical city with stunning views over the Sea of Galilee.

22432.extract: Osuna (ancient Urso) is a town in the province of Seville, southern Spain.
22432.romanEraExtract: Urso (modern Osuna) was an ancient Iberian settlement in the province of Baetica (southern Spain) that became a Roman colony — Colonia Genetiva Julia — established by Julius Caesar or his successor Octavian after the defeat of Pompey's sons at the Battle of Munda in 45 BC. The famous Lex Coloniae Genetivae Iuliae, a bronze inscription found here, is one of the most important surviving Roman colonial charters, providing detailed regulations for civic governance. The archaeological site has also yielded notable Iberian reliefs and sculptures.

21509.extract: Cyrrhus was a city in ancient Syria founded by Seleucus Nicator, one of Alexander the Great's generals.
21509.romanEraExtract: Cyrrhus was an important Hellenistic and Roman city in northern Syria, founded by Seleucus Nicator and serving as the capital of the extensive district of Cyrrhestica between the plain of Antioch and Commagene. Under Rome, it became a major administrative and military hub, hosting the Legio X Fretensis and minting its own coins. Bishop Theodoret (423-458) reported that his small diocese contained approximately 800 churches, indicating a densely populated region. The archaeological site near modern Nebi Houri preserves one of the largest Roman theaters in Syria, two still-functioning Roman bridges, fortification walls, and a basilica church.

26682.extract: Brigetio was a Roman legionary fortress and associated civil settlement on the Danube in Pannonia, at modern Szőny, Hungary.
26682.romanEraExtract: Brigetio was a Roman legionary fortress and civil settlement on the south bank of the Danube in the province of Pannonia Superior (modern Szőny, Hungary). It served as the permanent base of the Legio I Adiutrix from the late 1st century AD and became one of the most important military and administrative centers on the Danubian frontier. The settlement included a legionary camp, an extensive canabae (civilian town), and an amphitheater. Brigetio's strategic position made it a key link in the chain of fortifications guarding Rome's northern border.

22707.extract: Nora is an ancient pre-Roman and Roman town on a peninsula near Pula, Sardinia.
22707.romanEraExtract: Nora was one of the most important cities of ancient Sardinia, founded by Phoenicians on a strategic peninsula near modern Pula, south of Cagliari. It contains the oldest known Phoenician inscription in the western Mediterranean, the Nora Stone. Under Roman rule, Nora became the capital of the province of Sardinia and Corsica. The well-preserved archaeological site on its dramatic coastal peninsula includes a Roman theater, forum, baths with elaborate mosaics, and a temple of Aesculapius.

22307.extract: Ray (ancient Rhagai/Rhagae/Europos) is a historic city now absorbed into metropolitan Tehran, Iran.
22307.romanEraExtract: Rhagai (also Rhagae or Europos) was one of the great cities of ancient Media, located near modern Tehran, Iran. It was mentioned by both Greek and Persian sources as a major population center and stopping point on the east-west trade routes. Alexander the Great passed through Rhagai during his pursuit of Darius III in 330 BC. Under the Seleucids it was renamed Europos, and later under the Parthians it served as a royal residence and mint city. The site, now within Tehran's metropolitan area, has been inhabited for over 8,000 years.

21702.extract: Buto (ancient Egyptian: Per-Wadjet) was a city in the Nile Delta of Egypt, 95 km east of Alexandria.
21702.romanEraExtract: Bouto (Egyptian: Per-Wadjet) was an ancient city in the Nile Delta of Egypt, serving as the symbolic capital of Lower Egypt in predynastic times. It was sacred to the cobra goddess Wadjet, patron of Lower Egypt, whose oracle was consulted alongside that of the goddess Bast. Under the Ptolemies and Romans, Bouto continued as a religious center though its political importance had long since waned. The archaeological site at modern Tell al-Fara'in preserves mudbrick structures and temples spanning millennia of Egyptian history.

21569.extract: Arqa is a Lebanese village near Miniara in Akkar Governorate, 22 km northeast of Tripoli.
21569.romanEraExtract: Arca (modern Arqa, Lebanon) was an ancient Phoenician and Roman city in northern Lebanon, near the coast 22 km northeast of Tripoli. Under the Romans, it was elevated to colonial status as Caesarea Libani and served as a regional center. The town was the birthplace of Emperor Alexander Severus (r. AD 222-235), a connection that brought it imperial patronage. The site preserves a medieval Crusader fortress built atop earlier fortifications at Tell Arqa, one of the largest archaeological mounds in Lebanon.

23368.extract: Hersonissos (ancient Chersonasos) is a town on the north coast of Crete, bordering the Aegean Sea.
23368.romanEraExtract: Chersonasos was an ancient port city on the northern coast of Crete. In the Roman period it served as one of the harbors of the region, with remains of ancient port installations still visible beneath the modern resort town of Hersonissos. The city was also an episcopal see in late antiquity. Today, the archaeological site includes a partially submerged Roman harbor, a fountain house, and a 2nd-century Roman mosaic floor near the harbor.

22326.extract: Hit (ancient Idu/Is) is a city on the Euphrates River in Al Anbar Governorate, Iraq.
22326.romanEraExtract: Idu (later known as Is, modern Hit) was an ancient Mesopotamian city on the Euphrates River in what is now Iraq. It was famous in antiquity for its bitumen springs — Herodotus and other ancient writers described the natural petroleum seeps that provided material used in construction throughout Mesopotamia. The city served as a waystation on the Euphrates trade route and fell within the contested zone between the Roman and Parthian/Sasanian empires throughout the imperial period.

21441.extract: Seleucia Pieria was a Hellenistic seaport serving as the harbor of Antioch, the Seleucid capital.
21441.romanEraExtract: Seleucia Pieria was the seaport of Antioch on the Orontes, the great Seleucid and later Roman capital. Founded by Seleucus I Nicator around 300 BC, it served as the primary naval base and commercial port for one of the largest cities in the Roman East. The Romans constructed the remarkable Titus Tunnel — a massive rock-cut channel to divert flood waters away from the harbor — which remains one of the most impressive Roman engineering works. The site near modern Samandağ, Turkey, preserves the tunnel, rock-cut tombs, and remains of the ancient harbor.

21498.extract: Epiphania or Epiphaneia was a city in Cilicia Secunda, in Anatolia.
21498.romanEraExtract: Epiphaneia was an ancient city in Cilicia Secunda (southeastern Anatolia), originally known by its pre-Hellenistic name before being renamed by the Seleucid king Antiochus IV Epiphanes in the 2nd century BC. Under Roman rule, the city served as a regional center in the province of Cilicia and minted its own coins. It later became a bishopric and continued into the Byzantine period. The site is near modern Gözene in Turkey's Osmaniye province.

16824.extract: Ilici was a Roman colony at the archaeological site of La Alcudia near Elche, Spain.
