16824.romanEraExtract: Ilici (modern La Alcudia near Elche, Spain) was an Iberian settlement that became one of the most important Roman colonies in Hispania Tarraconensis — Colonia Iulia Ilici Augusta. It is most famous as the findspot of the Lady of Elche (Dama de Elche), an iconic Iberian bust sculpture from the 4th century BC, now in Madrid's National Archaeological Museum. Under Roman rule, the colony flourished with a forum, baths, and temples, and continued as an important settlement through the Visigothic period.

16799.extract: Hatria (modern Atri) was an ancient city in the Abruzzo region of central Italy.
16799.romanEraExtract: Hatria (modern Atri) was a Praetuttian and later Roman city in Abruzzo, central Italy, traditionally believed to have given its name to the Adriatic Sea (Mare Hadriaticum). It was an important Roman colony, founded in 289 BC, and one of the earliest to strike its own heavy bronze coinage. Under the Empire, the Hadria gens — the family of Emperor Hadrian — traced its origins to this city, though the connection remains debated. The town preserves Roman cisterns, walls, and a medieval cathedral built over Roman foundations.

23006.extract: Rabba (ancient Areopolis) is a town in the Karak Governorate of Jordan.
23006.romanEraExtract: Areopolis (modern Rabba, Jordan) was a Roman city in the province of Arabia Petraea, located along the King's Highway south of the Dead Sea. The city's Greek name ('City of Ares') may reflect the presence of a temple to the war god. Under Rome, it served as a garrison town and bishopric. Areopolis was part of the network of fortified settlements that guarded the empire's eastern frontier in Transjordan.

22752.extract: Domokos (ancient Thaumakoi/Thaumacus) is a town in Phthiotis, Greece.
22752.romanEraExtract: Thaumakoi (Thaumacus/Thaumace, modern Domokos) was a fortified town in ancient Thessaly, Greece, controlling a strategic pass between Thessaly and central Greece. Livy described it as a well-fortified position during the Roman-Macedonian wars. Under Roman rule, the town was part of the province of Achaea and continued as a regional center through the Byzantine period. The acropolis hill preserves ancient fortification walls.

21690.extract: Anthedon was a town in Boeotia, on the coast of the Gulf of Euboea.
21690.romanEraExtract: Anthedon was a coastal town in ancient Boeotia, situated on the Gulf of Euboea about 15 km west of Chalcis at the foot of Mount Messapius. It served as the primary port for crossing to the island of Euboea. Pausanias described it as a small town with a sanctuary of the Kabeiroi and a temple of Demeter. The town was known in myth as the home of the sea-god Glaucus, said to have been transformed after eating a magical herb on its shores.

21458.extract: Phellus is the site of an ancient Lycian city near Çukurbağ in Antalya Province, Turkey.
21458.romanEraExtract: Phellos (Phellus) was an ancient Lycian city situated in a mountainous area near modern Çukurbağ in Antalya Province, Turkey. The site is known for its remarkable collection of Lycian rock-cut tombs, including sarcophagi with pointed arch lids characteristic of Lycian funerary architecture. The city was a member of the Lycian League and continued under Roman rule as part of the province of Lycia et Pamphylia.

21531.extract: Germa or Germokoloneia was an ancient city in the Roman province of Galatia Secunda.
21531.romanEraExtract: Germa (Colonia Iulia Augusta Felix Germa) was a Roman colony in the province of Galatia in central Anatolia, established as a veteran settlement near modern Babadat in Eskişehir Province, Turkey. Its full name — Colonia Iulia Augusta Felix Germa — indicates its foundation under Augustus. The city later became an episcopal see and at a later period took the name Myriangeli. Few archaeological remains survive at the site, but ancient coins from Germa document its status and activity through the imperial period.

21978.extract: Nea Halos was an ancient city in Thessaly, Greece, near modern Neos Platanos.
21978.romanEraExtract: Nea Halos (New Halos) was a Hellenistic city in Thessaly, Greece, founded in the 4th century BC as a refoundation of the older city of Halos. The city was situated near the coast of the Pagasetic Gulf. Dutch-Greek archaeological excavations have revealed a well-planned Hellenistic city with a regular grid plan, defensive walls, houses, and public buildings. The city was destroyed by an earthquake around 265 BC and never fully rebuilt, making it an important time-capsule for understanding Hellenistic urban planning.

21209.extract: Pompeiopolis was an ancient city in Paphlagonia, modern Taşköprü, Turkey.
21209.romanEraExtract: Pompeiopolis was an ancient city in Paphlagonia (modern Taşköprü, Turkey), named after Pompey the Great who reorganized the region in 64 BC. The city served as an important center in the Roman province of Pontus et Bithynia. The ancient Roman bridge, from which the modern Turkish name Taşköprü ('stone bridge') derives, still spans the Gökırmak River. The city was a bishopric in the Byzantine period and preserves scattered Roman and Byzantine remains.

21662.extract: Acre (ancient Ake/Ptolemais) is a city on the coast of the Mediterranean Sea in northern Israel.
21662.romanEraExtract: Ake (later Ptolemais, modern Acre) was a major port city on the Levantine coast of the Mediterranean, one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world. Originally a Phoenician settlement, it was renamed Ptolemais under Ptolemy II and became an important Roman colony — Colonia Ptolemais — under Claudius. The city served as a key military staging point for Roman campaigns in the region and was the main port for travelers to Jerusalem. During the Crusades, it became one of the most important Crusader cities as Saint-Jean-d'Acre.

21499.extract: Hierapolis-Castabala was an ancient city in Cilicia, near modern Osmaniye, Turkey.
21499.romanEraExtract: Hierapolis-Castabala was an ancient city in Cilicia Pedias (modern Osmaniye province, Turkey), known for its temple of Artemis Perasia served by priestesses who reportedly walked barefoot on burning coals. Under the Seleucids and Romans, the city enjoyed periods of prosperity as a regional center. The archaeological site preserves a colonnaded street, theater, and Roman bath complex. The city was a bishopric in the Byzantine period.

22336.extract: Opis was an ancient Babylonian city on the Tigris, near modern Baghdad.
22336.romanEraExtract: Opis was an ancient Babylonian city on the Tigris River near the Diyala confluence, close to modern Baghdad, Iraq. It was the site of major historical events: the Battle of Opis in 539 BC where Cyrus the Great's forces defeated the Babylonians, opening the way to the conquest of Babylon. Alexander the Great also used Opis as a staging ground, and it was here in 324 BC that the Macedonian army mutinied against Alexander's policy of integrating Persian soldiers. The city lay in the contested zone between the Roman and Parthian empires.

22654.extract: Charax Spasinu was an ancient port at the head of the Persian Gulf in what is now Iraq and the capital of the kingdom of Characene.
22654.romanEraExtract: Charax Spasinu (also called Alexandria or Antiochia in Susiana) was an ancient port city at the head of the Persian Gulf in modern Iraq and the capital of the kingdom of Characene. Originally founded by Alexander the Great, it was refounded by the Seleucid general Hyspaosines who established Characene as an independent kingdom. The city was a major terminus of Indian Ocean trade — merchants from India, Arabia, and the Mediterranean converged here. Under Roman influence from Trajan's Mesopotamian campaigns onward, it served as a crucial link between Roman and eastern commerce, with 7,560 mentions in ancient texts reflecting its importance.

22641.extract: Phintias was an ancient Greek city on the south coast of Sicily, modern Licata.
22641.romanEraExtract: Phintias (modern Licata) was a Greek city on Mt. Eknomos at the mouth of the Himera River on the south coast of Sicily, founded in 282 BC by the tyrant Phintias of Akragas, who relocated the entire population of Gela to this new settlement. The city occupied a strategic position between Gela and Agrigento. Under Roman rule following the conquest of Sicily, it continued as a port town. Recent archaeological excavations have uncovered ancient altars and statue fragments indicating significant religious activity and a possible sudden abandonment.

21768.extract: Gholaia (modern Bu Njem) was a Roman fort in Libya, part of the Limes Tripolitanus.
21768.romanEraExtract: Gholaia (modern Bu Njem) was a Roman castellum in the Libyan desert, one of the best-preserved forts of the Limes Tripolitanus frontier system. Founded under Septimius Severus (193-211 AD), the fort's walls enclosed an area of 135 x 90 meters. It contained a bathhouse erected in 202 AD and the only scriptorium positively identified in a Roman fort. The garrison, drawn from the Legio III Augusta, left behind the remarkable 'Bu Njem ostraca' — hundreds of inscribed potsherds documenting daily military life on the frontier, from supply requests to patrol reports.

21832.extract: Amphipolis was an important ancient Greek and Roman city in Macedonia.
21832.romanEraExtract: Amphipolis was a major Greek and Roman city in eastern Macedonia, strategically located at the mouth of the Strymon River near the Pangaean gold mines. Founded as an Athenian colony in 437 BC, it was later conquered by Philip II of Macedon. Under Rome, it became the capital of Macedonia Prima after the division of the province in 167 BC. The Via Egnatia, Rome's great east-west highway, passed through the city. The apostle Paul visited Amphipolis on his second missionary journey. The site is most famous today for the monumental Kasta Tomb (discovered 2012-2014), the largest ancient tomb found in Greece, and the iconic Lion of Amphipolis.

21944.extract: Elis was the capital city of the ancient polis of Elis in the Peloponnese.
21944.romanEraExtract: Elis was the capital of the ancient Greek city-state of Elis in the northwestern Peloponnese, and the administrative center responsible for organizing the Olympic Games at nearby Olympia. Pausanias described it as one of the most populous and splendid cities of the Greek world. Athletes trained here for the required month before competing at Olympia. Under Roman rule, the city continued to oversee the Games and maintained its prosperity. The archaeological site preserves a theater, gymnasium, agora, and Roman-era baths.

21624.extract: Madauros was a Roman-Berber city in ancient Numidia, present-day Algeria.
21624.romanEraExtract: Madauros (modern M'Daourouch, Algeria) was a Roman-Berber city in Numidia, most famous as the birthplace and school-city of Apuleius, author of 'The Golden Ass' — the only Latin novel to survive in its entirety. The city was also where the young Augustine of Hippo studied rhetoric before moving to Carthage. Established as a veteran colony under the Flavian dynasty, Madauros possessed a forum, theater, baths, and numerous mosaics. The extensive ruins visible today testify to its prosperity as a center of Roman-African culture and learning.

21452.extract: Arycanda (Arykanda) is an ancient Lycian city in Antalya Province, Turkey.
21452.romanEraExtract: Arykanda was an ancient Lycian city built on five dramatic terraces high on a mountain slope in what is now Antalya Province, Turkey. Its name ending in -anda indicates pre-Greek Anatolian origins dating to the 2nd millennium BC. The city was known in antiquity for the extravagant spending habits of its citizens. Under Roman rule, it flourished with a monumental bath-gymnasium complex, theater, stadium, and odeon — all remarkably well-preserved due to the city's remote mountain location. The citizens notably petitioned Emperor Maximinus Daia in 312 AD to persecute Christians, one of the few such petitions known.

21435.extract: Olympus or Olympos is an ancient city in Lycia, Turkey.
21435.romanEraExtract: Olympos was an ancient Lycian city near the coast of southwestern Turkey, a member of the Lycian League. In the 1st century BC, it was occupied by the Cilician pirate chief Zenicetes, who used it as a base until Publius Servilius Vatia drove him out in 78 BC. Under Rome, the city became part of the province of Lycia et Pamphylia. Nearby burns the Chimaera — natural gas vents that have been alight for millennia, which ancient writers associated with the myth of the fire-breathing Chimaera slain by Bellerophon. The atmospheric ruins today lie within a forested river valley leading to the sea.

21457.extract: Cyaneae was a town of ancient Lycia, Turkey.
21457.romanEraExtract: Kyaneai (Cyaneae) was an ancient Lycian city perched on a hilltop in southwestern Turkey, surrounded by one of the densest concentrations of Lycian rock-cut tombs in the region — over 170 sarcophagi dot the surrounding landscape. The city was a member of the Lycian League with three votes, indicating its importance. Under Roman rule, it received a theater and other public buildings. German archaeological excavations since the 1990s have revealed it as a key site for understanding Lycian settlement patterns and the transition from Lycian to Roman provincial culture.

22331.extract: Isin is an archaeological site in Iraq, occupied from the late 4th millennium BC.
22331.romanEraExtract: Isin was an ancient Mesopotamian city in modern Al-Qādisiyyah Governorate, Iraq, continuously occupied from the Uruk period (late 4th millennium BC) through the Neo-Babylonian era. It served as the capital of the Dynasty of Isin (c. 2017-1794 BC), one of the most important early Mesopotamian states. The city lay within the Parthian Empire during the Roman period, in the contested zone of Mesopotamia between Rome and Parthia/Sasanian Persia. German-Iraqi excavations have revealed temples, administrative buildings, and thousands of cuneiform tablets from the site.

10847.extract: Gorsium-Herculia was a Roman city in Pannonia, modern Tác, Hungary.
10847.romanEraExtract: Gorsium (later Herculia) was a Roman city in the province of Pannonia Inferior, modern Tác in Hungary. It served as the religious center of the provincial imperial cult — the concilium provinciae met here annually. The city was destroyed during the Sarmatian-Iazyges incursions of 260 AD but rebuilt and renamed Herculia under Diocletian. Today, an open-air museum presents the well-preserved ruins, including the forum, an early Christian basilica, and the remains of the provincial temple where Roman governors were venerated.

22063.extract: Uchi Maius was an ancient Roman city in Tunisia near Dougga.
22063.romanEraExtract: Uchi Maius (modern Henchir-Ed-Douamès, Tunisia) was a Roman-African city in the province of Africa Proconsularis, located 120 km southwest of Tunis near the better-known site of Dougga. Originally a Numidian settlement, it became a Roman colony under Severus Alexander. Italian-Tunisian archaeological excavations have revealed extensive ruins including a forum, capitolium, triumphal arches, cisterns, and olive oil presses — the latter reflecting the region's importance in supplying olive oil to Rome.

21415.extract: Apollonia was an ancient Greek colony and later Roman city in southern Illyria.
21415.romanEraExtract: Apollonia (near modern Fier, Albania) was a major Greek colony founded by Corinthians and Corcyraeans in 588 BC, which became one of the most important cities of Illyria. Cicero called it magna urbs et gravis ('a great and important city'). It served as the western terminus of the Via Egnatia and a key base during Caesar's civil war — the young Octavian (future Augustus) was studying here when he received news of Caesar's assassination in 44 BC. The city declined from the 3rd century AD as its harbor silted up. The archaeological park preserves the Monument of the Agonothetes, a bouleuterion, library, and early Christian churches.

25281.extract: Hulwan was an ancient town on the Zagros Mountains in western Iran.
25281.romanEraExtract: Hulwan was an ancient town at the entrance of the Paytak Pass in the Zagros Mountains, modern Sarpol-e Zahab in western Iran. It occupied a strategic position on the road from Mesopotamia into the Iranian plateau. The town was on the eastern frontier of the Roman sphere of influence — it changed hands multiple times between Rome and the Parthian/Sasanian empires during their centuries of conflict over Mesopotamia. Its position controlling access through the Zagros passes made it a perpetual flashpoint of imperial rivalry.

25266.extract: Anah (ancient Anatho) is an Iraqi town on the Euphrates.
25266.romanEraExtract: Anatho (modern Anah, Iraq) was an ancient fortified island-town on the Euphrates, located approximately midway between the Mediterranean and the Persian Gulf. The Roman historian Ammianus Marcellinus described it as an island settlement in the river, fortified and self-sustaining. It was captured by the Roman emperor Julian during his ill-fated campaign against Sasanian Persia in 363 AD. The town served as a waystation on the Euphrates trade route and was repeatedly contested between Rome and the Parthian/Sasanian empires.

21754.extract: Heshbon (ancient Esbous) was an ancient town east of the Jordan River.
21754.romanEraExtract: Esbous (biblical Heshbon, modern Tell Hesban, Jordan) was an ancient city east of the Jordan River, historically within the territories of ancient Ammon and Moab. Under Roman rule, it was part of the province of Arabia Petraea and served as a regional center. The city became an episcopal see in the Byzantine period. American archaeological excavations since the 1960s have revealed occupation layers from the Iron Age through the Mamluk period, including Roman-era public buildings and an early Byzantine church.

21632.extract: Siga was an ancient city in Mauretania, near modern Ain Temouchent, Algeria.
21632.romanEraExtract: Siga (near modern Ain Temouchent, Algeria) was the royal capital of the Numidian king Syphax, who allied with Carthage against Rome during the Second Punic War. After Syphax's defeat by Scipio Africanus and Masinissa in 203 BC, the city passed to the Numidian kingdom of Masinissa. Under Roman rule, it became a colony in the province of Mauretania Caesariensis. The city was noted by Strabo as a significant port. Its ruins include Roman-era structures and a harbor that facilitated trade across the western Mediterranean.

23362.extract: Domavia was a Roman mining town near modern Srebrenica, Bosnia.
23362.romanEraExtract: Domavia (near modern Srebrenica, Bosnia and Herzegovina) was a Roman mining settlement in the province of Dalmatia, serving as the administrative center (procurator metallorum) for the rich silver and lead mines of the region. The name Srebrenica itself derives from the Serbian word for silver, reflecting the continuity of mining at this location. Under Roman administration, the mines were worked by convict labor and free miners, producing significant quantities of silver for the imperial treasury. The town possessed the standard Roman amenities including baths and temples despite its remote mountain location.

21689.extract: Maresha (Marisa) was an ancient city in the Judean lowlands of Israel.
21689.romanEraExtract: Marisa (Tel Maresha, Israel) was an ancient city in the Shephelah (Judean lowlands), one of the most important Hellenistic-era cities in the region. Originally an Edomite/Idumean center, it was conquered by John Hyrcanus I in 112 BC and its inhabitants were forcibly converted to Judaism. The city was destroyed by the Parthians in 40 BC and never rebuilt, making it an exceptional time-capsule of Hellenistic urban life. The UNESCO World Heritage site preserves extraordinary underground complexes — over 500 caves including columbaria (dovecotes), olive presses, water cisterns, and tombs with remarkable painted murals.

22729.extract: Arsinoe was an ancient city in the Fayum, Egypt.
22729.romanEraExtract: Arsinoe (earlier Crocodilopolis/Shedet, modern Medinet el-Fayum, Egypt) was the capital of the Fayum region, renamed in honor of Queen Arsinoe II in the 3rd century BC. The city was famous for its temple of the crocodile god Sobek, where sacred crocodiles were kept adorned with gold and jewels — Strabo described seeing them being fed by priests. Under Roman rule, the Fayum became one of Egypt's most productive agricultural regions, and Arsinoe served as its administrative center. The region has yielded thousands of papyri and the famous Fayum mummy portraits, some of the most striking examples of ancient portraiture.

21853.extract: Hadrianopolis was an ancient city in Paphlagonia, modern Eskipazar, Turkey.
21853.romanEraExtract: Hadrianopolis was a Roman city in Paphlagonia (modern Eskipazar, Karabük Province, Turkey), named after Emperor Hadrian who founded or refounded the settlement during his reign (117-138 AD). The city served as a regional center in the province of Paphlagonia. Turkish archaeological excavations since 2003 have revealed a well-preserved bath complex, churches, a rock-cut theater, and extensive Roman-era mosaics, making it an increasingly important site for understanding Roman urbanism in northern Anatolia.

22751.extract: Heraclea Lyncestis was an ancient Greek city in Macedonia, near modern Bitola, North Macedonia.
22751.romanEraExtract: Heraclea Lyncestis was an ancient city founded by Philip II of Macedon in the mid-4th century BC, situated 2 km south of present-day Bitola, North Macedonia. The city was named after Heracles, from whom the Macedonian dynasty claimed descent. Under Roman rule, it became an important station on the Via Egnatia — the great highway connecting Rome to Byzantium. In the early Christian period, Heraclea was a significant episcopal see. The archaeological site preserves remarkable floor mosaics in the episcopal basilica, a theater, Roman baths, and the forum.

22388.extract: Rhosos (modern Arsuz, Turkey) was an ancient port city in Cilicia/Syria.
22388.romanEraExtract: Rhosos (modern Arsuz, Hatay Province, Turkey) was an ancient port city on the Gulf of Issus, at the junction of Cilicia and Syria. The city is known from a famous inscription — the 'Letter of Octavian to Rhosus' — in which the future Augustus granted privileges to a naval veteran named Seleukos of Rhosos for his service at the Battle of Actium in 31 BC. This document is one of the most important surviving examples of Roman grants of citizenship and immunity. The city served as a coastal trading port and bishopric through the Byzantine period.

25103.extract: Tipasa was a Roman colonia in Mauretania Caesariensis, modern coastal Algeria.
25103.romanEraExtract: Tipasa (modern Tipaza, Algeria) was a Roman colonia in the province of Mauretania Caesariensis, situated on the Mediterranean coast west of Algiers. Originally a Punic trading post, it became a Roman military colony under Claudius and developed into a prosperous city with a forum, theater, amphitheater, baths, and temples. The city is especially notable for its early Christian remains, including the Great Basilica — one of the largest in North Africa — and the tomb-basilica of the local martyr St. Salsa. Declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1982, the ruins are among the most evocative Roman sites in the Maghreb, set dramatically between the sea and mountains.

25263.extract: Ganzak was an ancient town in northwestern Iran, south of Lake Urmia.
25263.romanEraExtract: Ganzak (Gazaka/Ganzaca) was an ancient city in Media Atropatene (modern northwestern Iran), south of Lake Urmia. The Persian nobleman Atropates, who gave his name to Azerbaijan (Atropatene), may have chosen it as his capital. The city housed a major Zoroastrian fire temple — Adur Gushnasp — one of the three great fires of Sasanian Iran. The Roman emperor Heraclius sacked the city and its fire temple in 624 AD during his campaign against the Sasanian Empire, an event that symbolized the last great Roman-Persian clash before the Arab conquests transformed both empires.

25264.extract: Phraaspa (Praaspa) was the capital of Media Atropatene in ancient Iran.
25264.romanEraExtract: Phraaspa (Praaspa) was the royal capital of Media Atropatene in northwestern Iran, famous as the site where Mark Antony's Parthian campaign of 36 BC came to grief. Antony besieged the city with a large Roman army but failed to take it — his siege train had been destroyed by the Parthians, and the onset of winter forced a devastating retreat through Armenian mountains, losing thousands of men. This failure, one of Rome's worst military disasters in the East, fatally weakened Antony's position against Octavian and contributed to his eventual defeat at Actium. The exact location of Phraaspa remains debated.

22587.extract: Trea was a Roman municipium in Picenum, modern Treia in the Marche region of Italy.
22587.romanEraExtract: Trea was a Roman municipium in the region of Picenum (modern Marche, central Italy), situated on a hill overlooking the Potenza River valley. The city was enrolled in the Roman tribe Velina and flourished as an agricultural center in the fertile Marche hills. Pliny the Elder listed its inhabitants among the peoples of Picenum. The modern town of Treia preserves the ancient name and occupies a nearby hilltop, while scattered Roman remains including inscriptions and architectural fragments have been found in the area.

8024.extract: Ruscino was a Roman town in Gallia Narbonensis, near modern Perpignan, France.
8024.romanEraExtract: Ruscino (near modern Perpignan, France) was a Roman oppidum and later town in the province of Gallia Narbonensis, strategically situated where the Via Domitia crossed the Têt River on its route from Italy to Spain. The settlement, on the hill of Château-Roussillon, served as the regional capital before being eclipsed by Perpignan in the Middle Ages. Pomponius Mela and Pliny both mention the city. Archaeological excavations have revealed a forum complex with a basilica and curia, making it one of the best-documented examples of Roman civic architecture in southern Gaul.

21862.extract: Meninx was an ancient city on the island of Djerba, Tunisia.
21862.romanEraExtract: Meninx (Meninge) was the principal city of the island of Djerba (ancient Meninx/Lotophagitis) off the coast of Tunisia, identified by ancient writers as the Land of the Lotus-Eaters visited by Odysseus in Homer's Odyssey. Under Roman rule, the city was an important center of purple dye production — the murex shells found abundantly in the surrounding waters were processed into the precious Tyrian purple. Djerba's unique radial settlement pattern and its remarkable Jewish community, centered on the El Ghriba synagogue (one of the oldest in Africa), reflect its millennia of continuous habitation.

25138.extract: Iptuci was a Roman city in Hispania Baetica, modern Andalusia, Spain.
25138.romanEraExtract: Iptuci was a pre-Roman and Roman city in the province of Hispania Baetica, located near modern Prado del Rey in Cádiz Province, Andalusia. The fortified hilltop site (Cabezo de Hortales) preserves remains of a circuit of walls, towers, and cisterns. Under Rome, the settlement was a stipendiary city that later received municipal status. The site's strategic position in the mountainous interior of Baetica gave it control over routes between the Guadalquivir valley and the coast of Gades (Cádiz).

21182.extract: Sebastopolis (modern Sulusaray, Turkey) was a Roman city in Pontus.
21182.romanEraExtract: Sebastopolis (modern Sulusaray, Tokat Province, Turkey) was a Roman city in the Pontus region of northern Anatolia. Named in honor of Augustus (Sebastos being the Greek equivalent of Augustus), the city served as a regional center in the province of Pontus Galaticus. It later became an episcopal see and was known as Heracleopolis in the Byzantine period. The site features natural hot springs, which were likely exploited in antiquity for Roman baths, contributing to the city's importance as a spa destination in the ancient world.

21527.extract: Neapolis was a town in ancient Pisidia, near Antioch.
21527.romanEraExtract: Neapolis was a small town in ancient Pisidia (south-central Anatolia), located a few miles south of the major colony of Antioch in Pisidia. Pliny the Elder mentions it among the towns of the Roman province of Galatia, which included parts of Pisidia. The settlement served as a bishopric in the Byzantine period, reflecting its continued importance as a local center even in late antiquity.

23829.extract: Laminium was a Carpetan oppidum in Roman Hispania.
23829.romanEraExtract: Laminium was a Carpetan oppidum in Hispania (near modern Alhambra, Ciudad Real, Spain), head of the Ager Laminitanus administrative district. Pliny the Elder describes it as the source of the Guadiana River (Anas) and notes the important road junction here — routes connected Laminium to Caesaraugusta (Zaragoza), Toletum (Toledo), and Castulo. The town minted its own coins and was a stipendiary city of the conventus of Carthago Nova.

21277.extract: Acmonia (Akmonia) was an ancient city in Phrygia, Asia Minor.
21277.romanEraExtract: Akmonia (Acmonia) was an ancient Phrygian city in the upper Banaz valley (modern Ahat Köyü, Uşak Province, Turkey). Under Roman rule, the city was part of the province of Asia and later Phrygia Pacatiana. It is notable for its early Jewish community — inscriptions record a woman named Julia Severa, a Roman priestess who also served as a patroness of the local synagogue, illustrating the complex religious landscape of Roman Anatolia. The city minted coins from the Hellenistic through Roman periods and served as a bishopric.

13915.extract: Salvium was a Roman municipium in Dalmatia, modern Glamoč, Bosnia.
13915.romanEraExtract: Salvium (modern Glamoč, Bosnia and Herzegovina) was a Roman municipium in the province of Dalmatia. The settlement served as a local administrative center in the mountainous interior of the Balkans. Roman inscriptions found at the site record the names of local magistrates and the municipal organization typical of Romanized settlements in Dalmatia. The area's pastoral economy and mineral resources sustained the community through the imperial period.

22628.extract: Lares was an ancient city in Africa Proconsularis, modern El Kef region, Tunisia.
22628.romanEraExtract: Lares (near modern Henchir Lorbeus, Tunisia) was a Roman colony in the province of Africa Proconsularis, established as a veteran settlement. The city served as a strategic military outpost on the frontier between settled Roman Africa and Numidian territory. It received colonial status under either Trajan or Hadrian. The site preserves remains of public buildings, inscriptions, and the typical infrastructure of a North African Roman colony, reflecting the progressive Romanization of the Tunisian interior.

22108.extract: Gadiaufala was a Roman-Berber city in Numidia, modern Algeria.
22108.romanEraExtract: Gadiaufala was a Roman-Berber settlement in the province of Numidia (modern Algeria). It was an episcopal see in the early Christian period — Bishop Donatianus of Gadiaufala attended the Council of Carthage in 411 AD, one of the great assemblies that brought Donatist and Catholic bishops face to face. The city's Berber name reflects the pre-Roman indigenous foundations that persisted alongside Roman colonial culture throughout North Africa.

22140.extract: Regiae was a Roman city in Numidia, modern Algeria.
22140.romanEraExtract: Regiae (modern Arris, Algeria) was a Roman city in the Aurès Mountains of Numidia. The city's Latin name ('Royal Palaces') suggests it may have been a Numidian royal residence before Roman conquest. Located in the rugged Aurès massif — the homeland of the Berber Zenata and Chaoui peoples — Regiae served as a Roman outpost in one of North Africa's most mountainous and strategically difficult regions. The area later became the heartland of the Berber revolt led by the legendary queen Dihya (Kahina) against the Arab conquests.

21539.extract: Poimanenon was an ancient city in Mysia, Asia Minor.
21539.romanEraExtract: Poimanenon was an ancient city in Mysia (northwestern Anatolia), situated in the province of Asia near the Propontis (Sea of Marmara). The city is known primarily from its coinage and from ecclesiastical records as a bishopric in the Byzantine period. Under Roman rule, it was part of the prosperous network of small cities that characterized the urbanized landscape of western Anatolia.

21526.extract: Pappa-Tiberiopolis was an ancient city in Lycaonia/Pisidia, modern Turkey.
21526.romanEraExtract: Pappa (later Pappa-Tiberiopolis after Emperor Tiberius) was an ancient city in the border region of Lycaonia and Pisidia in south-central Anatolia. The city received the additional name Tiberiopolis in honor of Emperor Tiberius, indicating imperial patronage during the early Principate. It served as a bishopric and is recorded in the acts of several ecumenical councils. The site near modern Yunuslar preserves scattered ancient remains.

21567.extract: Tripolis on the Meander was an ancient city on the borders of Phrygia, Caria, and Lydia.
21567.romanEraExtract: Tripolis on the Meander (also called Apollonia and Neapolis) was an ancient city at the junction of Phrygia, Caria, and Lydia, on the Maeander River road from Sardis to Laodicea ad Lycum. Its name ('Three Cities') reflects its formation from three earlier communities. Under Roman rule, coins from Tripolis bore the image of Leto, connecting it to the Letoid cult centers of the region. The city was an episcopal see from the 4th century — Bishop Agogius attended the First Council of Nicaea in 325 AD. The ruins near modern Yenicekent include a stadium, theater, baths, and necropolis.

16847.extract: Valeria was an important Roman city in the modern province of Cuenca, Spain.
16847.romanEraExtract: Valeria was a major Roman city in Hispania Citerior, situated dramatically on a promontory between limestone gorges at 1,000 meters altitude in modern Cuenca Province. Founded between 93 and 82 BC by the proconsul Valerius Flaccus, the city possessed one of the most complete Roman forum complexes in Spain, built on a massive artificial platform. Its Nymphaeum — over 100 meters long — is the largest known in the Iberian Peninsula and among the largest in the entire Roman Empire. A treasure of silver coins from c. 185 BC documents the city's early importance after the Second Punic War.

25252.extract: Carissa Aurelia was a Roman city in Hispania Baetica, Andalusia.
25252.romanEraExtract: Carissa Aurelia was a Roman city in the province of Hispania Baetica, near modern Bornos in Cádiz Province, Andalusia. The cognomen 'Aurelia' associates it with the gens Aurelia, possibly indicating a connection to the early 2nd century BC Roman magistrate who organized the region. Under Roman rule, it was a municipium with Latin rights. The archaeological site preserves the outline of a fortified hilltop settlement with views across the fertile Guadalete valley, along with a large Roman necropolis that has yielded important funerary monuments.

25142.extract: Suel was a Roman municipium on the Costa del Sol, Spain.
25142.romanEraExtract: Suel (near modern Fuengirola, Málaga Province, Spain) was a Phoenician and later Roman municipium on the Mediterranean coast of Hispania Baetica. The settlement was one of a chain of ancient trading posts along the Costa del Sol established by Phoenician merchants. Under Rome, it received municipal status and was part of the conventus of Gades. The Castillo de Sohail, a later Moorish fortress overlooking the coast at Fuengirola, is believed to occupy the site of the ancient settlement, preserving the name Suel in its Arabic form.

21536.extract: Fulvia was an ancient city in Phrygia, Asia Minor.
21536.romanEraExtract: Fulvia was an ancient settlement in Phrygia (central Anatolia, modern Turkey), named presumably after Fulvia, the politically active wife of Mark Antony, or another member of the gens Fulvia. The city's Roman name overlay an older Phrygian settlement and reflects the pattern of Roman political figures imprinting their names on Anatolian cities during the late Republican period. The city later served as a bishopric in the Byzantine period.

21264.extract: Appia was an ancient city in Phrygia, Asia Minor.
21264.romanEraExtract: Appia was an ancient city in Phrygia (central Anatolia), named after the Via Appia or a member of the Appian gens. It was situated in the upper Maeander valley region and served as a bishopric in the Byzantine period. The city is known from ancient coinage and from mentions by Hierocles and in conciliar records. Like many small Phrygian cities, it reflected the dense urbanization of interior Anatolia under Roman rule, where even minor settlements could possess the civic institutions of a Greek-Roman polis.

25141.extract: Sucia was a Roman settlement in Hispania Baetica.
25141.romanEraExtract: Sucia was a Roman settlement in the province of Hispania Baetica, in the Guadalquivir valley region of modern Andalusia. The site was part of the dense network of agricultural towns that made Baetica one of the richest provinces in the Roman Empire, producing vast quantities of olive oil exported to Rome. Many such small Baetican settlements are known primarily from ancient itineraries and inscriptions, reflecting the thorough Romanization of southern Spain.

25249.extract: Chalcis sub Libano (Chalcis ad Belum) was an ancient city in Syria.
25249.romanEraExtract: Chalcis sub Libano ('Chalcis below Lebanon', also Chalcis ad Belum) was a Hellenistic and Roman city in Syria, located in the Beqaa-like region between the Lebanon and Anti-Lebanon ranges. It was the capital of the small client kingdom of Chalcidice, ruled by the Ituraean dynasty under Roman suzerainty in the 1st centuries BC-AD. Herod Agrippa II later absorbed this territory. The city was an important strategic point on routes between the Mediterranean coast and the Syrian interior, near the modern Syrian-Lebanese border region.

25177.extract: Kierion (Cierium) was a city-state in Thessaliotis, ancient Thessaly.
25177.romanEraExtract: Kierion (Cierium) was an ancient city-state in the Thessaliotis district of Thessaly, identified by Stephanus of Byzantium as the successor to Homeric Arne — the legendary chief town of the Aeolian Boeotians before their migration to Boeotia. The city occupied a strategic position in the Thessalian plain, one of the great grain-producing regions of the ancient world. Under Roman rule, Thessaly was incorporated into the province of Achaea (later Thessalia). With 1,340 ancient text mentions, Kierion's literary importance far outstripped its modest physical remains.

25259.extract: Lancia was the most important city of the Astures in Roman Hispania.
25259.romanEraExtract: Lancia was the largest and most powerful city of the Astures people, located on a plateau between the Porma and Esla rivers near modern Villasabariego, León, Spain. In 25 BC, the Roman general Publius Carisius attacked and subdued the city during the Cantabrian Wars — Augustus's final conquest of the Iberian Peninsula. According to tradition, Carisius spared the city from burning so that intact it would serve as 'a better monument to Roman victory.' Under the Flavian dynasty, Lancia achieved the status of municipium Flavium, with Roman-style baths, a market hall, sewage network, and grid urban planning. The pre-Roman settlement had covered some 30 hectares.

21428.extract: Aigai was an ancient Greek and Roman city in Aeolis, Asia Minor.
21428.romanEraExtract: Aigai was an ancient Greek city in Aeolis (western Anatolia), one of the twelve cities of the Aeolian League mentioned by Herodotus and Strabo. It housed an important sanctuary of Apollo and had its most prosperous period under the Attalid dynasty of nearby Pergamon in the 3rd-2nd centuries BC. Under Roman rule, the city continued as a modest regional center in the province of Asia. The archaeological site near modern Nemrutkale preserves a well-preserved market hall (macellum) with three stories built into the hillside, along with traces of the ancient agora and city walls.

21521.extract: Derbe was a city in Galatia/Lycaonia in Asia Minor.
21521.romanEraExtract: Derbe was an ancient city in the border region of Galatia and Lycaonia (south-central Anatolia), important in early Christian history as one of the cities visited by the Apostle Paul on his first missionary journey (Acts 14). Paul and Barnabas fled here after being stoned in nearby Lystra. Derbe was also the hometown of Gaius, one of Paul's travelling companions. Under Roman administration, the city shifted between the provinces of Galatia, Lycaonia, and later Isauria and Cappadocia as provincial boundaries were redrawn. The site has been identified near Kerti Höyük in Karaman Province, Turkey.

21272.extract: Tabala was an ancient city in Lydia, Asia Minor.
21272.romanEraExtract: Tabala was an ancient city in Lydia (western Anatolia), located on a major east-west route through the interior of Asia Minor. The city served as a road junction connecting the Hermus valley with the routes leading south to the Maeander. Under Roman rule, it was part of the province of Asia and later a bishopric in the ecclesiastical province of Sardis. Tabala appears in Roman itineraries as a station on the imperial road system.

22592.extract: Tuficum was a Roman municipium in Umbria, central Italy.
22592.romanEraExtract: Tuficum was a Roman municipium in Umbria (modern Marche, central Italy), near modern Borgo Tufico, a hamlet of the commune of Fabriano. The city was enrolled in the Roman voting tribe Oufentina and served as a local administrative center in the Apennine foothills. Inscriptions found at the site document the municipal magistrates and the typical civic life of a small Italian town during the Roman Empire. The name survives in the modern hamlet of Borgo Tufico.

23501.extract: Contributa Iulia Ugultunia was a Roman city in Hispania Baetica.
23501.romanEraExtract: Contributa Iulia Ugultunia (near modern Medina de las Torres, Badajoz, Spain) was a Roman city in Hispania Baetica, created by merging (contributa) several smaller settlements into a single municipality under Julius Caesar or Augustus. The name 'Ugultunia' preserves the pre-Roman indigenous settlement name. The city was part of the conventus of Hispalis (Seville). Archaeological excavations have revealed a forum, baths, and domestic buildings, showing it was a prosperous if modest town in the agricultural heartland of southwestern Hispania.

25227.extract: Rhodopolis was an ancient city in Colchis, modern Georgia.
25227.romanEraExtract: Rhodopolis was an ancient city in the kingdom of Colchis (Lazica), in modern western Georgia. Mentioned by Pliny the Elder and Ptolemy, the city was part of the mythic landscape of the Argonauts and the Golden Fleece. Under Roman influence, Colchis served as a buffer state on the eastern Black Sea coast, and Rhodopolis was one of several fortified cities protecting Roman interests in the Caucasus. The region later became the focus of the prolonged Lazic War (541-562 AD) between the Byzantine and Sasanian empires.

21603.extract: Carpis was an ancient port city in Africa Proconsularis, Tunisia.
21603.romanEraExtract: Carpis was an ancient port city on the Cap Bon peninsula in the province of Africa Proconsularis (modern Tunisia). Strategically positioned on the coast facing Sicily across the narrow Strait of Sicily, the city served as a maritime link between North Africa and the Italian peninsula. Like many towns on the Cap Bon, it benefited from the region's fertile agricultural land and its proximity to the major trade routes of the central Mediterranean. The site preserves Roman-era ruins near modern Mraissa.

25215.extract: Ulia Fidentia was a Roman city in Hispania Baetica, modern Montemayor, Córdoba.
25215.romanEraExtract: Ulia Fidentia (modern Montemayor, Córdoba, Spain) was a fortified Roman city in Hispania Baetica, famous for its steadfast loyalty to Julius Caesar during the civil war against Pompey's sons. The cognomen 'Fidentia' ('Faithful') was bestowed by Caesar himself in recognition of the city's resistance during the siege by Pompey's forces in 45 BC. Ulia held out until Caesar could relieve it, contributing to his final victory at the Battle of Munda. The hilltop site overlooking the Guadalquivir valley preserves traces of its ancient fortifications.

13902.extract: Magnum was a Roman settlement in Dalmatia, modern Balina Glavica, Croatia.
13902.romanEraExtract: Magnum was a Roman settlement in the province of Dalmatia, located at modern Balina Glavica near Drniš in Croatia. It served as a station on the Roman road from Salona (Split) to Burnum, a legionary fortress in the Dalmatian interior. The site was part of the network of small fortified settlements that helped Rome control the rugged Dalmatian hinterland. Archaeological finds include Roman inscriptions, architectural fragments, and evidence of the standard roadside infrastructure (mansio) of the imperial cursus publicus.

21271.extract: Silandos was an ancient city in Lydia, Asia Minor.
21271.romanEraExtract: Silandos (Silandus) was an ancient city in Lydia (western Anatolia), situated in the Hermus valley. The city minted its own coins in the Roman period, some featuring the river god of the Hermus. It was part of the dense network of small cities in the province of Asia and later became a bishopric. Ancient inscriptions from the area include an important 'confession inscription' genre unique to Lydia and Phrygia, in which worshippers confessed sins against the local gods and recorded divine punishments — providing rare insight into popular religious practice in rural Anatolia.

25205.extract: Nabrissa was an ancient city in Hispania Baetica, modern Lebrija, Spain.
25205.romanEraExtract: Nabrissa (modern Lebrija, Seville Province, Spain) was an ancient Turdetanian and later Roman city in Hispania Baetica, situated near the ancient shoreline of Lake Ligustinus — a vast inland sea at the mouth of the Guadalquivir that has since silted up. Strabo mentions the city and its connection to the fertile marshlands of the Baetis estuary. Under Rome, Nabrissa was a stipendiary city of the conventus of Hispalis. The region's transformation from navigable lake to marshland (the modern Marismas del Guadalquivir) illustrates the dramatic environmental changes that have reshaped the ancient landscape.

25251.extract: Callet was a Roman city in Hispania Baetica.
25251.romanEraExtract: Callet (Callenses Aeneanici) was a Roman city in the province of Hispania Baetica, near modern Montellano in Seville Province, Spain. Pliny the Elder lists the Callenses among the stipendiary communities of the conventus of Hispalis. The cognomen 'Aeneanici' suggests a mythological connection to Aeneas, reflecting the Roman habit of grafting Trojan/Roman foundation myths onto Iberian settlements. The hilltop site commands views across the agricultural lands of the Guadalquivir basin.

25140.extract: Salaria was a Roman municipium in Hispania Tarraconensis, modern Úbeda la Vieja, Spain.
25140.romanEraExtract: Salaria was a Roman municipium in the upper Guadalquivir valley (modern Úbeda la Vieja, Jaén Province, Spain), situated near the rich silver-mining district of Castulo. The city's name may derive from the salt trade. Under Augustus or the Flavians, it received municipal status. Salaria was part of the conventus of Carthago Nova in the province of Hispania Tarraconensis. The site occupies a defensive hilltop position with views over the fertile olive-growing valley that remains the economic backbone of the region today.

22484.extract: Collippo was a Roman municipium in Lusitania, modern central Portugal.
22484.romanEraExtract: Collippo was a Roman municipium in the province of Lusitania, located near modern Leiria in central Portugal. The city received Latin rights and municipal status, probably under the Flavian dynasty. Pliny the Elder lists the Collippenses among the oppida of Lusitania. The hilltop site of São Sebastião do Freixo preserves remains of the Roman city, including parts of a cryptoporticus (underground gallery), baths, and mosaic pavements. Collippo was one of the most important Roman centers in the region between Olisipo (Lisbon) and Conimbriga.

11084.extract: Mogentiana was a Roman town in Pannonia Superior.
11084.romanEraExtract: Mogentiana was a Roman town in the province of Pannonia Superior (modern Tüskevár, western Hungary), situated on the road between Savaria (Szombathely) and Arrabona (Győr). The Itinerarium Antonini lists it as a station on this important Pannonian route. The settlement served as one of the modest but strategically placed road towns that maintained Roman communications across the Danubian provinces. Archaeological finds at the site include Roman building foundations and an early Christian cemetery.

25134.extract: Iliturgicola was a Roman municipium in Hispania Baetica.
25134.romanEraExtract: Iliturgicola was a Roman municipium in Hispania Baetica, near modern Fuente-Tójar in Córdoba Province, Spain. The name — a diminutive of Iliturgi — suggests a smaller settlement linked to that older Iberian city. The site preserves one of the finest Roman funerary monuments in Andalusia: a tower-tomb (turris) of the 1st century AD, reflecting the prosperity of the olive-producing towns of the Subbetic Cordillera. Pliny the Elder mentions the Iliturgiculani among the stipendiary peoples of Baetica.

13756.extract: Volgum was a Roman settlement in Noricum, modern Austria.
13756.romanEraExtract: Volgum was a Roman settlement in the province of Noricum, located in the Alpine region of modern Austria. It served as a waystation on the road network connecting the Danube frontier with Italy through the Alpine passes. Like many Norican settlements, it had pre-Roman Celtic origins — Noricum was one of the earliest Celtic regions to form a unified kingdom and to be peacefully absorbed into the Roman Empire in 15 BC.

25214.extract: Siarum was a Roman municipium in Hispania Baetica.
25214.romanEraExtract: Siarum (modern La Cañada, near Utrera, Seville Province, Spain) was a Roman municipium in Hispania Baetica, one of the many thriving small towns in the agricultural heartland of the Guadalquivir valley. The city produced olive oil for export to Rome — the Monte Testaccio in Rome, an artificial hill of discarded amphorae, contains fragments stamped with Baetican origin marks from this region. Siarum received municipal status, likely under the Flavian dynasty's broad grant of Latin rights to Hispanic communities.

25194.extract: Macomades was a Roman city in Numidia, North Africa.
25194.romanEraExtract: Macomades was a Roman city in the province of Numidia (modern Algeria), one of several North African settlements bearing this Punic-derived name meaning 'new town' or 'marketplace.' The city was part of the dense network of Roman settlements in the agricultural hinterland of Africa — the empire's breadbasket. North African grain, together with Egyptian supply, fed the city of Rome. Macomades later became an episcopal see, its bishops attending North African church councils.

22115.extract: Zabi was a Roman and Byzantine city in Mauretania Sitifensis.
22115.romanEraExtract: Zabi Iustiniana was a fortified city in the province of Mauretania Sitifensis (modern Algeria), that received the honorific 'Iustiniana' after its reconstruction by the Byzantine Emperor Justinian I during the reconquest of North Africa in the 6th century. The original Roman settlement was part of the limes — the chain of forts and towns guarding the southern frontier of Roman Africa against Berber raids. Justinian's North African reconquest (533-534 AD) briefly restored Roman rule to the region after a century of Vandal occupation.

25201.extract: Lauro was a Roman settlement in Hispania Tarraconensis.
25201.romanEraExtract: Lauro was a Roman settlement in Hispania Tarraconensis, famous as the site where Gnaeus Pompeius Magnus (Pompey the Great) defeated Sertorius in battle during the Sertorian War (c. 76 BC). Pompey is said to have watched from a hill as the city burned — a significant episode in the civil wars that convulsed the Roman Republic. The exact location remains debated, with candidates in the Vallès region of Catalonia and near Lora del Río in the Guadalquivir valley.

22625.extract: Cissi was a Roman town in Hispania, possibly near modern Guissona.
22625.romanEraExtract: Cissi (also Kissa or Cissa) was a settlement in Hispania Tarraconensis, known from the context of the Second Punic War. In 218 BC, Gnaeus Cornelius Scipio Calvus defeated Hanno near Cissi (or Cissa) in one of the first Roman engagements on Iberian soil, capturing the Carthaginian baggage train and establishing Rome's foothold in Spain. The identification with modern Guissona (ancient Iesso) or with a site near Tarragona remains debated among scholars.

22128.extract: Rusippisir was a Roman city in Mauretania Caesariensis.
22128.romanEraExtract: Rusippisir was a Roman city in the province of Mauretania Caesariensis (modern Algeria). The Punic-derived name reflects the Phoenician heritage of many North African place names that predated Roman conquest. The city was part of the rich belt of Roman urban settlement along the Algerian coast and Tell Atlas, where Roman engineers developed extensive olive orchards and grain production. It later served as a bishopric in the African church.

22262.extract: Tigava was a Roman municipium in Mauretania Caesariensis.
22262.romanEraExtract: Tigava Municipium (near modern El Attaf, Aïn Defla Province, Algeria) was a Roman municipium in the province of Mauretania Caesariensis. Situated in the Chelif valley — the longest river in Algeria — the city occupied one of the most fertile agricultural corridors in North Africa. The honorific 'Municipium' indicates it achieved full municipal status under Roman law, granting its citizens substantial self-governance. Archaeological remains include Latin inscriptions documenting the city's magistrates and public buildings.

21598.extract: Biia was a Roman settlement in Africa Proconsularis.
21598.romanEraExtract: Biia (also written Bi'a) was a Roman settlement in the province of Africa Proconsularis (modern Tunisia), part of the extraordinarily dense urbanization of Roman North Africa. The region between Carthage and Theveste contained hundreds of small towns, many of which — including Biia — are known primarily from episcopal lists and the records of church councils, reflecting the deep Christianization of African Roman society in the 4th and 5th centuries.

24859.extract: Bisica Lucana was a Roman city in Africa Proconsularis.
24859.romanEraExtract: Bisica Lucana was a Roman city in the province of Africa Proconsularis (modern northern Tunisia), near Djebba. The cognomen 'Lucana' may connect it to settlers from Lucania in southern Italy — a pattern seen across Roman Africa where colonists brought their Italian hometown names. The city was significant enough to serve as an episcopal see; its bishops attended the Council of Carthage in 411 AD, the great conference between Catholic and Donatist bishops that shaped the future of African Christianity.

24856.extract: Seressi was a Roman city in Africa Proconsularis.
24856.romanEraExtract: Seressi was a Roman city in Africa Proconsularis (modern Tunisia), part of the dense grid of agricultural towns that made Roman Africa the empire's richest grain-producing region. The site has yielded important Latin inscriptions, including details of municipal governance and religious dedications. Seressi later became an episcopal see — its bishops appear in the acts of North African church councils, documenting the vigorous ecclesiastical life of late Roman Africa.

21596.extract: Uluzibbira was a settlement in Roman Numidia.
21596.romanEraExtract: Uluzibbira was a settlement in the province of Numidia (modern Algeria), its distinctively Libyo-Punic name preserving the indigenous linguistic substrate beneath Roman administration. Many North African town names — unlike the Latinized forms of Europe — retained their pre-Roman Berber or Punic origins throughout the imperial period, reflecting a cultural continuity that survived Roman, Vandal, and Byzantine rule. The settlement is known primarily from ancient itineraries and ecclesiastical records.

22356.extract: Badias was a Roman settlement in Mauretania Caesariensis.
22356.romanEraExtract: Badias was a Roman settlement in the province of Mauretania Caesariensis (modern Algeria), located along the military road system that linked the coastal cities with the interior garrison towns. The settlement's name, like many in Mauretania, reflects indigenous Berber rather than Latin origins. Under Roman rule, the region was organized into a network of military posts and civilian towns that formed the western section of the African limes.

21753.extract: Aere was an ancient settlement in the Roman Empire.
21753.romanEraExtract: Aere was a settlement in the eastern provinces of the Roman Empire. The name appears in ancient itineraries as a road station, part of the vast network of mansiones (lodging stations) and mutationes (relay posts) that enabled the cursus publicus — Rome's state postal and transport system — to function across thousands of miles. These small stations, often spaced a day's journey apart, were the connective tissue of imperial administration.

21666.extract: Neue was an ancient settlement in Asia Minor.
21666.romanEraExtract: Neue was an ancient settlement in Asia Minor (modern Turkey). The name appears in Roman-era itineraries and geographical sources. Like hundreds of small towns in Anatolia, it was part of the dense urban fabric that made the Roman province of Asia one of the most urbanized regions in the ancient world — Strabo counted over 500 named settlements in western Asia Minor alone. Many of these smaller communities survive only as names in road lists and episcopal records.

25179.extract: Oichalia was an ancient city in Thessaly, Greece.
25179.romanEraExtract: Oichalia (Oechalia) was an ancient city in Thessaly (central Greece), identified in myth as the kingdom of Eurytus, the great archer whom Heracles defeated. Homer mentions Oechalia in the Iliad, and the city's legendary destruction by Heracles was the subject of a lost epic poem (the Capture of Oechalia). Multiple ancient sites claimed to be Oichalia — in Thessaly, Messenia, and Euboea — reflecting the pull of Homeric prestige. Under Roman rule, Thessaly was part of the province of Achaea.

23821.extract: Mantua is a city in Lombardy, Italy.
23821.romanEraExtract: Mantua (Mantova) was an Etruscan and later Roman city in Cisalpine Gaul (northern Italy), famous above all as the birthplace of Virgil (70 BC), Rome's greatest poet and author of the Aeneid, the Georgics, and the Eclogues. Virgil's pastoral poetry drew directly on the landscape around Mantua — the marshy plains of the Mincio River — giving the city an outsized place in Latin literary culture. Under Augustus, Mantua's territory was confiscated for veteran settlements after the Battle of Philippi (42 BC), an event Virgil laments in his first Eclogue.

23612.extract: Digdida Selorum was a Roman settlement in Numidia.
23612.romanEraExtract: Digdida Selorum was a Roman settlement in the province of Numidia (modern Algeria). The double name preserves both an indigenous Berber toponym (Digdida) and a tribal or ethnic qualifier (Selorum), illustrating how Roman administrative nomenclature layered Latin bureaucratic conventions over local identities. The settlement is documented in ancient sources as part of the agricultural hinterland of Numidia, the province that, along with Africa Proconsularis, supplied much of Rome's grain.

25217.extract: Asso was a Roman town in Numidia.
25217.romanEraExtract: Asso was a Roman town in the province of Numidia (modern Algeria), part of the agricultural hinterland that made Roman North Africa one of the most productive regions in the empire. The settlement served as a bishopric in late antiquity, its clergy participating in the vigorous theological debates — including the Donatist controversy — that defined African Christianity before the Islamic conquest.

25154.extract: Brigaecium was a Roman city in Hispania Tarraconensis.
25154.romanEraExtract: Brigaecium (near modern Dehesa de Morales, Zamora Province, Spain) was a Roman city in Hispania Tarraconensis, the chief settlement of the Asturian Brigaecini. The Celtic-derived name (briga = fortification) places it among the pre-Roman hillforts of northwestern Iberia. Under Rome, it became a municipium and served as an administrative center in the conventus of Asturica Augusta. The site overlooks the Esla River valley and preserves traces of Roman urbanization in this frontier zone between Celtic and Roman cultures.

22232.extract: Iliturgi was an Iberian and Roman city in Hispania.
22232.romanEraExtract: Iliturgi was a fortified Iberian city in the upper Guadalquivir valley (modern Mengíbar, Jaén Province, Spain), notorious in the Second Punic War for its destruction by Scipio Africanus in 206 BC. After the city's population had massacred Roman troops during Hannibal's invasion, Scipio sacked it utterly as an example. Livy describes the slaughter in vivid detail. The rebuilt Roman settlement was enrolled in the tribe Galeria and later received municipal status. Archaeological excavations at Cerro de la Muela have revealed both the Iberian and Roman phases of the city.

22442.extract: Ilorci was an Iberian city in Hispania.
22442.romanEraExtract: Ilorci was an Iberian city in southeastern Hispania, famous as the site where the elder Scipio brothers — Publius Cornelius Scipio and Gnaeus Cornelius Scipio Calvus — were killed in battle against the Carthaginians in 211 BC. Their deaths marked Rome's darkest hour in the Spanish theater of the Second Punic War and prompted the unprecedented appointment of the 25-year-old Scipio Africanus to avenge them. The exact location remains debated, with candidates near Lorca (Murcia) and in the Jaén highlands.

22069.extract: Belalis Maior was a Roman city in Africa Proconsularis.
22069.romanEraExtract: Belalis Maior (Henchir el-Faouar, northern Tunisia) was a Roman city in Africa Proconsularis that preserves one of the best-documented examples of a small provincial Roman city. French excavations revealed a complete forum complex, baths, temples, and residential quarters — a vivid snapshot of urban life in Roman Africa. The city's oil-pressing installations testify to the olive monoculture that made the Tunisian Tell one of the empire's most profitable agricultural regions.

21645.extract: Thala was an ancient city in Numidia, modern Tunisia.
21645.romanEraExtract: Thala (modern Thala, Kasserine Governorate, Tunisia) was an ancient Numidian and Roman city, mentioned by Sallust as a stronghold of King Jugurtha during the Jugurthine War (112-106 BC). When the Roman general Metellus approached, the inhabitants reportedly burned their treasure and the city itself rather than let it fall to Rome. Under Roman rule, Thala was rebuilt and flourished as a prosperous agricultural town in the high steppe of Africa Proconsularis, later serving as a Byzantine fortress.

21700.extract: Thmouis was an ancient city in the Nile Delta, Egypt.
21700.romanEraExtract: Thmouis (Tell Timai, modern Egypt) was a major city in the eastern Nile Delta, capital of the Mendesian nome. After the decline of neighboring Mendes, Thmouis inherited its role as the regional center. Under Roman rule, the city was known for its perfume industry — the Mendesian unguent was one of the most prized fragrances in the ancient world. Archaeological excavations have revealed extensive Roman-period domestic quarters, bath complexes, and evidence of the city's commercial prosperity in the Nile Delta's fertile agricultural landscape.

21699.extract: Mendes was an ancient city in the Nile Delta, Egypt.
21699.romanEraExtract: Mendes (Tell el-Rub'a, modern Egypt) was one of the great cities of the Nile Delta, capital of the Mendesian nome and briefly of all Egypt during the 29th Dynasty (399-380 BC). The city was famous for its cult of the sacred ram (Ba-neb-Djed) and for the production of Mendesian perfume, exported across the Mediterranean world. By the Roman period, the city had largely ceded its prominence to neighboring Thmouis, though its temples and heritage remained. Ptolemy, Strabo, and Herodotus all mention Mendes.

25257.extract: Forum Limicorum was a Roman town in Hispania (Gallaecia).
25257.romanEraExtract: Forum Limicorum (near modern Xinzo de Limia, Ourense, Spain) was a Roman town in Gallaecia (northwestern Hispania), the chief settlement and market center (forum) of the Limici, a Celtic tribe mentioned by Strabo and Pliny. The town lay in the fertile Limia valley, and the Limici were known in Roman sources for their superstitious dread of crossing the River Limia — which they identified with the mythical River Lethe (River of Forgetfulness). A Roman commander famously crossed first to prove his troops would not lose their memories.

21437.extract: Gagai was a coastal city in ancient Lycia.
21437.romanEraExtract: Gagai (also Gagae) was an ancient coastal city in Lycia (southern Anatolia, modern Turkey), mentioned by Pliny the Elder and Ptolemy. The city served as a minor port on the rugged Lycian coast between Olympos and Phaselis. Under Roman rule, it was part of the Lycian League, the celebrated federal system that the American Founding Fathers later studied as a model for representative government. Gagai later became a bishopric in the ecclesiastical province of Myra.

25210.extract: Sabora was a Roman municipium in Hispania Baetica.
25210.romanEraExtract: Sabora (near modern Cañete la Real, Málaga Province, Spain) was a Roman municipium in Hispania Baetica, known from a famous bronze tablet preserving a letter from the Emperor Vespasian (77 AD). In the letter, Vespasian grants the city permission to move from its hilltop to the plain below and to take his name — Municipium Flavium Sabora. This is one of the most vivid surviving documents of the relationship between a small provincial town and the imperial center, showing how communities petitioned Rome for favors.

22426.extract: Ocuri was a Roman city in Hispania Baetica, near Ubrique, Cádiz.
22426.romanEraExtract: Ocuri (near modern Ubrique, Cádiz Province, Spain) was a fortified Roman city dramatically perched on the Salto de la Mora hill in the Serranía de Ronda mountains of Hispania Baetica. The site preserves remarkable cyclopean walls built from massive limestone blocks, a columbarium tomb, Roman baths, and a forum area. The city controlled a strategic mountain pass between the coast and the Guadalquivir interior. Its pre-Roman origins are visible in the megalithic construction technique blended with Roman urban planning.

22265.extract: Arva was a Roman municipium in Hispania Baetica.
22265.romanEraExtract: Arva (near modern Alcolea del Río, Seville Province, Spain) was a Roman municipium in Hispania Baetica on the Guadalquivir River (Baetis). The city was a major center of olive oil production, with its amphora stamps (tituli picti) found on the Monte Testaccio in Rome — the artificial hill of discarded oil containers that provides the most complete record of the Roman oil trade. Arva's location on the navigable Baetis gave it direct access to the riverine transport that shipped Baetican oil to Ostia and Rome.

22266.extract: Canama was a Roman municipium in Hispania Baetica.
22266.romanEraExtract: Canama (modern Alcolea del Río area, Seville Province, Spain) was a Roman municipium on the Guadalquivir River in Hispania Baetica. Like its neighbor Arva, the city was embedded in the Baetican olive oil industry that supplied Rome with hundreds of thousands of amphorae annually. The name is pre-Roman (Turdetanian), reflecting the continuity of indigenous settlement patterns beneath Roman urbanization in the rich Guadalquivir valley.

25139.extract: Sacili Martialium was a Roman municipium in Hispania Baetica.
25139.romanEraExtract: Sacili Martialium (near modern Pedro Abad, Córdoba Province, Spain) was a Roman municipium in Hispania Baetica on the Guadalquivir River. The cognomen 'Martialium' links it to the cult of Mars. The city was part of the dense network of riverside towns that made the middle Guadalquivir one of the most urbanized landscapes in the western Roman Empire. Pliny the Elder lists it among the oppida of the conventus of Corduba. Archaeological remains include inscriptions and traces of the Roman road from Corduba to Castulo.

25206.extract: Naeva was a Roman settlement in Hispania Baetica.
25206.romanEraExtract: Naeva (near modern Cantillana, Seville Province, Spain) was a Roman settlement on the lower Guadalquivir in Hispania Baetica. Strabo mentions the navigability of the Baetis up to Hispalis and beyond, and towns like Naeva served as river ports in the chain that connected the agricultural interior with the export harbor at Hispalis (Seville). The site preserves traces of Roman construction along the riverbank.

25207.extract: Nescania was a Roman municipium in Hispania Baetica.
25207.romanEraExtract: Nescania (near modern Valle de Abdalajís, Málaga Province, Spain) was a Roman municipium in Hispania Baetica, situated in the dramatic limestone gorge of El Chorro at the foot of the Torcal mountains. The site has yielded a rich collection of Roman bronzes, including honorific decrees and a lex (charter) documenting municipal governance. Its position near the important road from Malaca (Málaga) to Anticaria (Antequera) made it a strategic node in the Baetican road network.

25221.extract: Cisimbrium was a Roman municipium in Hispania Baetica.
25221.romanEraExtract: Cisimbrium (near modern Zambra, Lucena, Córdoba Province, Spain) was a Roman municipium Flavium in Hispania Baetica. A bronze tablet from the site records the grant of Roman citizenship by Domitian to a Cisimbrium cavalryman who had served 25 years in the auxiliary forces — one of the clearest surviving examples of how military service provided a path to citizenship for provincial subjects. The town was part of the conventus of Astigi (Écija).

16843.extract: Tucci was a major Roman colony in Hispania Baetica.
16843.romanEraExtract: Tucci (Colonia Augusta Gemella Tucci, modern Martos, Jaén Province, Spain) was a major Roman colony in Hispania Baetica, founded by Augustus for veterans of the Legio X Gemina and Legio IV Macedonica. The double-legion settlement (hence 'Gemella') reflects the scale of Augustan colonization in southern Spain. The colony's high status as a colonia civium Romanorum — the most privileged form of Roman settlement — made it one of the chief cities of the upper Guadalquivir. The dramatic rock outcrop of the Peña de Martos still dominates the landscape.

22435.extract: Ucubi was a Roman colony in Hispania Baetica.
22435.romanEraExtract: Ucubi (Colonia Claritas Iulia Ucubi, modern Espejo, Córdoba Province, Spain) was a Roman colony in Hispania Baetica, founded by Julius Caesar or Augustus as part of the massive colonization that followed the civil wars in Spain. The cognomen 'Claritas Iulia' honors its Caesarian foundation. The colony was enrolled in the voting tribe Galeria and served as an administrative center in the conventus of Corduba. The hilltop site preserves medieval fortifications that likely overlie the Roman colonial foundations.

22609.extract: Segida was a Roman settlement in Hispania.
22609.romanEraExtract: Segida Augurina (possibly near modern Burguillos del Cerro, Badajoz, Spain) was a Roman municipium in Hispania Baetica. The cognomen 'Augurina' may link it to an augural foundation rite. Several towns named Segida existed in Roman Hispania — the name is Celtic (from sego- = victory/strength), reflecting the Celtiberian substrate. Pliny lists the Segidenses among the stipendiary communities of the conventus of Hispalis, and the town minted its own coinage in the Republican period.

22598.extract: Turirecina was a Roman settlement in Hispania Baetica.
22598.romanEraExtract: Turirecina (possibly near modern Casas de Reina, Badajoz, Spain) was a Roman settlement in Hispania Baetica, near the important colony of Regina Turdulorum. The site lies in the agricultural zone of southern Extremadura, where Roman towns exploited the grasslands and grain fields of the Guadalquivir watershed. The nearby theater of Regina, seating 1,000, demonstrates the cultural aspirations even of modest provincial towns in this region.

16844.extract: Igabrum was a Roman municipium in Hispania Baetica.
16844.romanEraExtract: Igabrum (modern Cabra, Córdoba Province, Spain) was a Roman municipium Flavium in Hispania Baetica, situated in the olive-rich Subbetic Cordillera. The town received municipal status under the Flavian dynasty's broad grant of Latin rights (the ius Latii). Cabra's vicinity has yielded one of the finest Roman sculptures found in Spain — a bronze figure of Aphrodite — testifying to the wealth of Baetican landowners. The city was part of the conventus of Astigi.

16816.extract: Pitinum Mergens was a Roman municipium in Umbria.
16816.romanEraExtract: Pitinum Mergens (near modern Acqualagna, Pesaro e Urbino, Italy) was a Roman municipium in the Apennine foothills of Umbria (modern Marche). The city was distinguished from its neighbor Pitinum Pisaurense by the cognomen 'Mergens.' Situated on the Via Flaminia — the great consular road from Rome to Rimini — it served travelers crossing the Apennines at the Furlo Pass (Petra Pertusa), where a tunnel carved through the rock by Emperor Vespasian still stands. The pass was one of the most spectacular engineering works on the Italian road network.

21848.extract: Garama was the capital of the Garamantes kingdom in the Sahara.
21848.romanEraExtract: Garama (modern Germa, Fezzan, Libya) was the capital of the Garamantes, a remarkable Saharan kingdom that flourished from around 500 BC to 700 AD. Far from being mere desert nomads, the Garamantes built a sophisticated urban civilization with an underground irrigation system (foggara) that sustained agriculture in the Sahara. They were Rome's most important trading partners south of the limes — exchanging gold, slaves, and exotic animals for Roman manufactured goods. Several Roman expeditions reached Garama, including Cornelius Balbus's campaign in 19 BC, for which he received the last triumph awarded to a non-member of the imperial family.

22324.extract: Artemita was a Hellenistic city in Mesopotamia.
22324.romanEraExtract: Artemita was a Hellenistic city in Mesopotamia (modern Iraq), founded or renamed during the Seleucid period, perhaps after the goddess Artemis. It lay on the route between Seleucia on the Tigris and the Iranian plateau, making it a node on the great east-west trade routes. Isidore of Charax mentions it in his Parthian Stations — a 1st-century AD road guide through the Parthian Empire. The city lay in the fertile zone east of the Tigris that was contested between Roman and Parthian/Sasanian powers throughout antiquity.

28867.extract: Alexandrou Limen was a port on the Indian Ocean coast.
28867.romanEraExtract: Alexandrou Limen ('Harbor of Alexander') was a port on the coast of the Indian Ocean, mentioned in the Periplus of the Erythraean Sea — a 1st-century AD Greek merchant's guide to the Red Sea and Indian Ocean trade routes. The port was part of the network that connected Roman Egypt with India, Arabia, and East Africa, through which flowed spices, silk, incense, and precious stones. Roman gold coins have been found as far as southern India, attesting to the scale of this maritime commerce.

26359.extract: Aquincum Castra was a Roman legionary fortress on the Danube.
26359.romanEraExtract: Aquincum Castra was the legionary fortress at Aquincum (modern Óbuda, Budapest, Hungary), one of the most important military bases on the Danube frontier. Home to the Legio II Adiutrix from the late 1st century AD, the fortress anchored Roman defenses in the central Danube sector and served as the capital of the province of Pannonia Inferior. The military camp and the adjacent civilian town together formed the largest Roman settlement in what is now Hungary, with amphitheaters, baths, and aqueducts.

25163.extract: Iamneia Paralios was the coastal portion of ancient Yavne.
25163.romanEraExtract: Iamneia Paralios ('Iamneia on the Coast') was the port town of ancient Iamnia (Yavne) in Judaea/Palaestina, on the Mediterranean coast south of Joppa (Jaffa). The inland town of Iamnia is famous in Jewish history as the seat of the Sanhedrin after the destruction of Jerusalem in 70 AD, where Rabban Yohanan ben Zakkai established the academy that preserved rabbinic Judaism. The coastal settlement served as the port for this important center, handling maritime traffic along the Levantine coast.

25164.extract: Maioma Ascalonitis was a port settlement near Ascalon.
25164.romanEraExtract: Maioma Ascalonitis was the port suburb (maiumas) of Ascalon (Ashkelon) on the coast of Palaestina Prima. In the Roman and Byzantine periods, many Levantine cities had separate port settlements called 'maiumas,' which sometimes received independent civic status. The main city of Ascalon was one of the great Philistine and later Hellenistic cities, and its port handled trade across the eastern Mediterranean. Ascalon's most famous exports were its scallions (the word 'shallot' derives from Ascalon) and its fine wines.

21842.extract: Turris Tamalleni was a Roman frontier fort in southern Tunisia.
21842.romanEraExtract: Turris Tamalleni (modern Telmine, southern Tunisia) was a Roman fortified settlement on the southern frontier of Africa Proconsularis, at the edge of the Saharan salt lakes (chotts). The 'Turris' (tower) designation indicates its role as a watchtower and garrison post on the limes Tripolitanus, the chain of forts protecting the settled agricultural zone from Saharan raids. The oasis at Telmine, fed by artesian springs, made sustained habitation possible in this arid zone between the chotts and the Grand Erg Oriental.

21860.extract: Zitha was a Roman frontier town on the Euphrates.
21860.romanEraExtract: Zitha (also Zita, near modern Suruç area, southeastern Turkey) was a Roman frontier town on the upper Euphrates, part of the network of forts and settlements that guarded the eastern frontier against Parthia and later Sasanian Persia. The town appears in the Tabula Peutingeriana and other Roman itineraries as a station on the military road along the Euphrates. This frontier zone — the limes Orientalis — was the most heavily contested boundary in the ancient world, seeing centuries of Roman-Persian warfare.

22119.extract: Satafis was a Roman colony in Mauretania Sitifensis.
22119.romanEraExtract: Satafis (modern Aïn el-Kebira, Sétif Province, Algeria) was a Roman colony in Mauretania Sitifensis. Originally a military outpost, the settlement grew into a full colony under the Severan dynasty. Satafis is notable for its well-preserved Latin inscriptions, which document the civic and religious life of a garrison town on the frontier of Roman Africa. The site has also yielded early Christian inscriptions and funerary art, reflecting the rapid Christianization of North Africa in the 3rd-4th centuries.

21648.extract: Bagai was a Roman city in Numidia.
