21648.romanEraExtract: Bagai (Bagaï, modern Ksar Baghaï, Khenchela Province, Algeria) was a Roman city in the province of Numidia, later a major Byzantine fortress. The city played a prominent role in the Donatist controversy — the great schism that divided North African Christianity in the 4th century — when a Donatist council met there in 394 AD. Under Justinian's reconquest of North Africa (534 AD), Bagai was rebuilt as one of the key Byzantine fortresses, its massive walls still visible today, guarding the approaches to the Aurès Mountains.

22107.extract: Nattabutum was a Roman settlement in Africa.
22107.romanEraExtract: Nattabutum was a Roman settlement in the province of Africa Proconsularis (modern Tunisia). The Libyo-Berber toponym — characteristic of pre-Roman North African place names — survived intact throughout the Roman period. The settlement was one of hundreds of small agricultural communities in the Tunisian Tell that collectively produced the grain surplus that fed the city of Rome. It later served as a bishopric, its clergy attending the great Council of Carthage in 411 AD.

25223.extract: Thugga Terebenthina was a Roman city in Africa Proconsularis.
25223.romanEraExtract: Thugga Terebenthina was a Roman city in Africa Proconsularis (modern Tunisia), not to be confused with the famous Thugga (Dougga) — one of the best-preserved Roman cities in North Africa. The cognomen 'Terebenthina' (from terebinth, a Mediterranean tree) distinguishes this smaller settlement from its more famous namesake. Like much of Roman Africa, the area was intensively cultivated for grain and olives under the imperial estate system, where vast imperial landholdings employed tenant farmers.

27326.extract: Rhapta was the southernmost known trading port on the East African coast.
27326.romanEraExtract: Rhapta was the southernmost trading port known to Greco-Roman geographers on the East African coast, described in the Periplus of the Erythraean Sea (1st century AD) and by Ptolemy. The name may derive from the sewn boats (rhapton ploiarion) used by its inhabitants. Located somewhere on the coast of modern Tanzania, possibly near the Rufiji Delta, Rhapta was a marketplace where Arab and Greek merchants exchanged iron tools, weapons, and glass for ivory, tortoiseshell, and rhinoceros horn — marking the furthest reach of Rome's Indian Ocean trade network.

27327.extract: Sabatha was a trading settlement on the East African coast.
27327.romanEraExtract: Sabatha was a trading settlement on the East African coast, mentioned in ancient geographical sources as one of the ports along the Azania coast (modern Kenya-Tanzania). The Periplus of the Erythraean Sea describes a chain of markets along this coast, governed from Muza in Yemen under Himyarite authority. These East African emporiums connected the Roman-dominated Mediterranean trade system with the ivory, incense, and exotic goods of sub-Saharan Africa and the Indian Ocean world.

28980.extract: Ozene (Ujjain) was a major Indian trading city known to Roman merchants.
28980.romanEraExtract: Ozene (ancient Ujjain, Madhya Pradesh, India) was one of the major Indian cities known to Roman merchants, mentioned in the Periplus of the Erythraean Sea as an important inland market. The city served as a node in the overland trade routes connecting the western Indian ports (Barygaza/Bharuch) with the interior of the subcontinent. Roman gold coins, Mediterranean pottery, and amphorae found across western India document the immense volume of Indo-Roman trade — Pliny complained that India drained Rome of 50 million sesterces annually for spices, gems, and textiles.

32425.extract: Paithana was a major trading city in Deccan India.
32425.romanEraExtract: Paithana (Pratishthana, modern Paithan, Maharashtra, India) was the capital of the Satavahana dynasty and a major node in the Indo-Roman trade network. The Periplus of the Erythraean Sea names it as a significant inland market, connected by overland routes to the western Indian ports of Kalliena and Barygaza. Roman coins, amphorae, and fine pottery found at sites across the Deccan plateau testify to the scale of commerce. Paithana was one of the furthest inland points that Roman-era Mediterranean goods regularly reached.

32453.extract: Tagara was an Indian inland trading city mentioned by Ptolemy.
32453.romanEraExtract: Tagara (identified with modern Ter, Maharashtra, India) was an inland trading city of the Deccan plateau mentioned by Ptolemy and the Periplus of the Erythraean Sea. The city served as a collection point for textiles, semiprecious stones, and other goods bound for the western Indian ports and ultimately the Roman Empire. Excavations at Ter have yielded Roman coins and Mediterranean artifacts, confirming the ancient literary accounts of active Indo-Roman commerce in the heart of the Indian subcontinent.

29026.extract: Patala was the port city at the Indus Delta.
29026.romanEraExtract: Patala was the ancient port city at the head of the Indus Delta (modern Sindh, Pakistan), where Alexander the Great's fleet under Nearchus departed for the voyage along the coast to the Persian Gulf in 325 BC. The city served as the administrative center of the Indus Delta region and a key point of contact between the Mediterranean and Indian Ocean worlds. The Periplus of the Erythraean Sea and Ptolemy both describe the Indus region's trade connections with the Roman Empire, exchanging cotton textiles, spices, and ivory for Roman gold and manufactured goods.

33574.extract: Tamna was the capital of the Qataban kingdom in ancient Yemen.
33574.romanEraExtract: Tamna (Timna, modern Yemen) was the capital of the ancient kingdom of Qataban, one of the great South Arabian incense states. Situated in the Wadi Bayhan, the city controlled a vital section of the Incense Route that carried frankincense and myrrh from the Arabian Peninsula to the Mediterranean world. The Roman appetite for Arabian incense was enormous — Pliny describes vast camel caravans crossing Arabia to supply Rome's temples, funerals, and perfume industry. Tamna's ruins include a massive city wall, temples, and inscriptions in the South Arabian script.

28904.extract: Kanogiza was a trading settlement on the Indian Ocean coast.
28904.romanEraExtract: Kanogiza was a trading settlement mentioned in ancient sources documenting the Indian Ocean trade network. The settlement was part of the chain of markets and ports that connected the Roman-controlled Red Sea with the spice and textile markets of India and East Africa. This maritime commerce — the largest long-distance trade system in the ancient world — moved goods across thousands of miles, from Italian workshops to Indian markets and back, powered by the seasonal monsoon winds that Greek mariners had learned to exploit by the 1st century AD.

27307.extract: Karna was a settlement in ancient East Africa.
27307.romanEraExtract: Karna was a settlement on the East African coast, documented in ancient geographical sources on the Azania coast (modern East Africa). The chain of ports along this coastline — from Adulis in Eritrea south to Rhapta in Tanzania — formed the African wing of the Indian Ocean trade network. Ivory was the primary African export, so valued in the Roman world for furniture, statuary, and luxury goods that elephant populations in coastal East Africa were significantly depleted by the trade's intensity.

25145.extract: Hurmizd Ardashir was a Sasanian city at the Persian Gulf.
25145.romanEraExtract: Hurmizd Ardashir (identified with modern Ahvaz or a nearby site in Khuzestan, Iran) was a Sasanian city in the Persian Gulf region, named after its founder from the Ardashir dynasty. The city was part of the Sasanian Persian Empire's heartland — Rome's great eastern rival from the 3rd to 7th centuries AD. The Sasanian cities of Khuzestan and southern Iraq sat at the western terminus of the maritime silk route, connecting Persian Gulf trade with the overland routes to the Mediterranean, making them pivotal in the commerce that Rome and Persia both sought to control.

25261.extract: Thannuris was a Roman-Byzantine fortress on the eastern frontier.
25261.romanEraExtract: Thannuris (near modern Tuneinir, al-Hasakah Governorate, Syria) was a Roman and Byzantine frontier fortress on the eastern limes, facing Sasanian Persia. The site gained fame when Justinian I rebuilt it as a major fortification in the 530s AD, as described by Procopius in his Buildings. Thannuris was part of the heavily fortified zone between the Tigris and Euphrates that formed the most active military frontier in the ancient world — centuries of Roman-Persian warfare shaped its landscape with forts, watch-towers, and military roads.

25232.extract: Shadikanni was an ancient city on the Khabur River.
25232.romanEraExtract: Shadikanni (Arban, on the Khabur River, modern Syria) was an ancient city with roots stretching back to the Assyrian Empire, situated on the Khabur tributary of the Euphrates. In the Roman period, the Khabur valley formed part of the frontier zone between Rome and Parthia/Persia. The region's rich alluvial soil supported agriculture even in the semi-arid steppe, and the string of settlements along the Khabur served as stations on trade routes linking Mesopotamia with the Mediterranean coast.

25268.extract: Daskara was a city in Sasanian Mesopotamia.
25268.romanEraExtract: Daskara (Dastagerd-i Malik) was a city in Sasanian Mesopotamia (modern Iraq), situated on the route between Seleucia-Ctesiphon and the Iranian plateau. The settlement was part of the densely urbanized zone of central Mesopotamia that both the Roman and Sasanian empires contested. During Heraclius's great counter-offensive against Persia in 627-628 AD, Byzantine forces raided deep into this region, sacking Sasanian palaces — one of the most dramatic campaigns in late antique military history.

25280.extract: Dastagird was a major Sasanian palace complex.
25280.romanEraExtract: Dastagird was a major Sasanian palace complex and royal residence near modern Baghdad, Iraq, famous as the site of Khosrow II's treasure palace — one of the most lavish buildings in the ancient world. When the Byzantine Emperor Heraclius sacked it in 628 AD during his desperate counter-invasion of Persia, his troops reportedly found vast quantities of silk, gold, silver, and luxury goods, including relics believed to include the True Cross captured from Jerusalem. The sack of Dastagird was a turning point that destroyed Khosrow's prestige and led to his overthrow.

25279.extract: Vologesias was a Parthian city in southern Mesopotamia.
25279.romanEraExtract: Vologesias (Vologesocerta) was a Parthian city in southern Mesopotamia (modern Iraq), founded by King Vologases I (51-78 AD) to rival the Hellenistic commercial center of Charax Spasinu. The city was established to divert the lucrative overland silk and spice trade away from the older Greek-founded cities and into Parthian-controlled commerce. Vologesias thus represents a rare example of deliberate economic warfare through urban planning in the ancient world — the Parthian state competing with Rome not just militarily but commercially for control of the east-west trade routes.

25282.extract: Eran-Khurra-Shapur was a Sasanian city in Khuzestan.
25282.romanEraExtract: Eran-Khurra-Shapur ('Glory of Eran, [city of] Shapur') was a Sasanian royal foundation in Khuzestan (southwestern Iran), part of the ambitious building program of the Sasanian dynasty. The Sasanians — Rome's final and most formidable eastern rival — founded numerous cities bearing elaborate royal names, asserting their ideology of divinely-sanctioned kingship. Khuzestan (ancient Susiana) was the agricultural heartland of the Sasanian Empire, its irrigation systems supporting one of the densest populations in the ancient Near East.

25147.extract: Veh-Andiyok-Shapur was a Sasanian city in Mesopotamia.
25147.romanEraExtract: Veh-Andiyok-Shapur ('Better-than-Antioch-[built-by]-Shapur') was a Sasanian city in Mesopotamia, founded by Shapur I after his dramatic capture of the Roman Emperor Valerian at the Battle of Edessa in 260 AD. The city was reportedly settled with Roman prisoners of war, and its boastful name — claiming superiority over Antioch, Rome's greatest eastern city — commemorated Shapur's humiliation of Rome. Roman captives brought their engineering skills, and traces of Roman-style construction techniques have been identified at several Sasanian sites from this period.

25234.romanEraExtract: Veh-az-Amid-Kavad ('Better-than-Amida-[built-by]-Kavad') was a Sasanian royal foundation, named to celebrate King Kavad I's capture of the Byzantine fortress city of Amida (modern Diyarbakır) in 502 AD. Like Veh-Andiyok-Shapur before it, this triumphalist naming convention — 'better than [captured Roman city]' — was a deliberate Sasanian propaganda tool, commemorating victories over Rome/Byzantium in the names of new urban foundations. The Persian kings routinely resettled captured Roman populations in these new cities.

25283.extract: Shahrazor was a plain and district in the Zagros Mountains.
25283.romanEraExtract: Syarazur (Shahrazor) was a fertile plain and district in the Zagros Mountains of modern Iraqi Kurdistan, mentioned by Ptolemy and other ancient geographers. The Shahrazor plain was a natural corridor connecting Mesopotamia with the Iranian plateau, making it strategically vital in Roman-Parthian and Byzantine-Sasanian conflicts. Trajan's legions passed through the region during his Mesopotamian campaign of 115-117 AD, and Julian's disastrous Persian expedition of 363 AD campaigned nearby. The plain remains one of the most productive agricultural zones in Kurdistan.

25276.extract: Maskin was a settlement in Mesopotamia.
25276.romanEraExtract: Maskin was a settlement in Mesopotamia (modern Iraq), located near the Tigris River. The site is historically significant as the approximate location of the Battle of the Zab (750 AD), where the Abbasid revolution overthrew the Umayyad Caliphate — though this event falls outside the classical period. In the Roman and Sasanian eras, the settlement was part of the agricultural heartland of central Mesopotamia, the region that had sustained urban civilization since the Sumerian period and that both empires fought to control.

25155.extract: Abitinae was a Roman city in Africa Proconsularis.
25155.romanEraExtract: Abitinae (modern Chouhoud el-Batin, Tunisia) was a Roman city in Africa Proconsularis, famous in Christian history as the site of a dramatic mass arrest of 49 Christians during the Great Persecution under Diocletian (304 AD). The Acta Martyrum Saturnini documents their trial before the proconsul Anullinus — they had been caught celebrating the Eucharist in defiance of the imperial edict. Their fate became a cause célèbre in the Donatist controversy, as the Donatists used the Abitinian martyrs' example to condemn those who had surrendered scriptures to Roman authorities.

25156.extract: Aunobaris was a Roman settlement in North Africa.
25156.romanEraExtract: Aunobaris was a Roman settlement in North Africa, documented in ancient itineraries as a road station in the province of Africa Proconsularis or Numidia. Its Libyo-Berber name typifies the indigenous toponymy that persisted alongside Latin administrative language throughout the Roman period. The settlement was part of the agricultural infrastructure of Roman Africa, the network of small towns and estates that made North Africa the empire's granary.

25157.extract: Chidibbia was a Roman settlement in Africa Proconsularis.
25157.romanEraExtract: Chidibbia was a Roman settlement in Africa Proconsularis (modern Tunisia), known primarily from episcopal lists — its bishops attended North African church councils in late antiquity. The profusion of bishoprics in Roman Africa is itself remarkable: by the early 5th century, Africa Proconsularis alone had over 600 bishops, more than any other region of the Roman Empire, reflecting both the density of urbanization and the intensity of African Christian life.

21843.extract: Thiges was a Roman oasis settlement in Africa.
21843.romanEraExtract: Thiges was a Roman oasis settlement at the edge of the Sahara in southern Africa Proconsularis (modern Tunisia), near the line of salt lakes (chotts) that marked the approximate southern limit of Roman settlement. The oasis communities of this zone — sustained by springs and qanats — served as trading posts between the settled Roman provinces and the Saharan peoples, including the Garamantes. Thiges is mentioned in ancient geographical sources as a frontier settlement where the cultivated world gave way to desert.

21830.extract: Gabboula was a Roman settlement in North Africa.
21830.romanEraExtract: Gabboula was a Roman settlement in the province of Africa or Numidia (modern Algeria or Tunisia). Like many small North African communities, it is known primarily from its appearance in ecclesiastical records and ancient itineraries. The hundreds of such settlements — too small for major literary sources but large enough for bishops — document the extraordinary density of Roman urbanization in North Africa, where even modest agricultural communities participated in the institutions of Roman civic and Christian religious life.

25159.extract: Ureu was a Roman settlement in North Africa.
25159.romanEraExtract: Ureu was a Roman settlement in the province of Africa Proconsularis (modern Tunisia). The name preserves indigenous Libyo-Berber roots, part of the older linguistic landscape that Roman administration never fully replaced. The settlement served as a bishopric in late antiquity, adding to the dense ecclesiastical geography of Roman Africa. Its bishop attended the Council of Carthage in 411 AD, the landmark conference that attempted to resolve the century-old Donatist schism.

25160.extract: Uzali Sar was a Roman settlement in Africa Proconsularis.
25160.romanEraExtract: Uzali Sar (modern El-Alia, Bizerte Governorate, Tunisia) was a Roman settlement in Africa Proconsularis, notable for its production of Christian hagiographic literature. The city's name preserves both a Berber element (Uzali) and a Punic element (Sar = 'rock' or 'chief'). In the 5th century, the miraculous translation of the relics of Saint Stephen to Uzali Sar was recorded in a detailed text that provides valuable insights into late Roman provincial life and popular religious practice.

24857.extract: Semta was a Roman settlement in Africa Proconsularis.
24857.romanEraExtract: Semta was a Roman settlement in the province of Africa Proconsularis (modern Tunisia), part of the agricultural heartland of the Medjerda (Bagradas) valley — the most productive grain-growing region in Roman Africa. The Bagradas valley was to Africa what the Nile was to Egypt: the source of the grain surplus that sustained the annona, Rome's public food supply. Semta was one of the many small agricultural communities that dotted this fertile landscape.

22261.extract: Tucca was a Roman city in North Africa.
22261.romanEraExtract: Tucca was a Roman city in the province of Africa Proconsularis or Numidia (modern Tunisia or Algeria). Multiple cities named Tucca existed in Roman North Africa — Tucca Terebenthina and Thugga (Dougga) are better-known variants. The name may derive from a Libyo-Punic root. Like its namesakes, this Tucca was part of the dense urban network of Roman Africa, where Punic, Berber, and Latin cultures merged into a distinctive provincial civilization.

22109.extract: Zattara was a Roman settlement in Numidia.
22109.romanEraExtract: Zattara was a Roman settlement in the province of Numidia (modern Algeria), situated in the agricultural hinterland of the Aurès Mountains region. The settlement is known from Latin inscriptions and ecclesiastical lists, its bishops attending the great North African church councils. The Aurès region was one of the last areas of North Africa to be fully pacified by Rome, and its Berber-speaking population maintained a strong sense of local identity even under Roman rule.

25184.extract: Adatha was a settlement in Roman Syria.
25184.romanEraExtract: Adatha was a settlement in the province of Syria (or Osrhoene), documented in ancient sources as a waypoint on routes through the Syrian Jazira. The region between the Euphrates and Tigris (the 'Island' or Jazira in Arabic) was heavily contested between Rome and Persia for centuries. Under Diocletian's reorganization, this frontier zone was garrisoned with limitanei — frontier soldiers who both farmed and defended the border, blurring the line between civilian and military settlement.

25137.extract: Ipsca was a Roman municipium in Hispania Baetica.
25137.romanEraExtract: Ipsca (Cortijo de Izcar, near Baena, Córdoba Province, Spain) was a Roman municipium in Hispania Baetica, probably receiving its municipal charter under the Flavian dynasty. The site occupies a fortified hilltop in the Subbetic Cordillera — the rolling limestone hills south of the Guadalquivir that were blanketed with olive groves in antiquity as they are today. Pliny lists the Ipscenses among the communities of the conventus of Astigi. Archaeological finds include Roman inscriptions, sculptures, and ceramic workshops.

13900.extract: Splonum was a Roman settlement in Dalmatia.
13900.romanEraExtract: Splonum was a Roman settlement in the province of Dalmatia (modern Bosnia and Herzegovina), situated in the mountainous interior of the western Balkans. The site lay on the network of roads that connected the Dalmatian coast with the mining districts of the interior — silver, lead, and iron mines were the primary economic drivers of Roman investment in the Balkan highlands. The indigenous Illyrian population was gradually Romanized, though pre-Roman settlement patterns persisted in the rugged terrain.

46097.extract: Themisonion was an ancient city in Phrygia, Asia Minor.
46097.romanEraExtract: Themisonion (also Themisonium) was an ancient city in Phrygia (southwestern Anatolia, modern Turkey), situated in the upper Maeander region near modern Dodurga. The city was named after Themison, possibly a local dynast. Under Roman rule, it was part of the province of Asia and later a bishopric. Themisonion is known from ancient coins and inscriptions, and the site preserves scattered architectural remains. The city was one of the numerous small Phrygian poleis that dotted the fertile highland valleys of central-western Anatolia.

25153.extract: Palaia Chersonesos was an ancient settlement in the Crimea.
25153.romanEraExtract: Palaia Chersonesos ('Old Chersonesos') was an ancient Greek settlement in the Crimean Peninsula, predating or associated with the better-known colony of Chersonesos Taurica (modern Sevastopol). The Crimea was a key region in the Roman Black Sea strategy — Chersonesos served as a Roman ally and later garrison point, protecting the northern trade routes and monitoring the Scythian and Sarmatian peoples of the steppe. Roman troops were stationed in the region from the 1st century AD.

21766.extract: Pisida was a settlement in Asia Minor.
21766.romanEraExtract: Pisida was a settlement in Pisidia (southern Anatolia, modern Turkey), the mountainous region between Lycia, Pamphylia, and Phrygia. The Pisidians were among the most independent-minded peoples of Anatolia, resisting Hellenistic and Roman authority from their mountain strongholds. Augustus established a ring of veteran colonies (Antioch, Cremna, Comama, Olbasa, Parlais) specifically to contain Pisidian brigandage — one of the most systematic colonization programs in the Roman Empire.

25255.extract: Planina was a Roman settlement in Dalmatia.
25255.romanEraExtract: Planina was a Roman settlement in the province of Dalmatia (modern Bosnia or Montenegro). The Slavic name (meaning 'mountain/plateau') was applied in the medieval period to older Roman sites — a common pattern in the Balkans where Slavic migrations in the 6th-7th centuries replaced Latin and Illyrian toponyms. The original Roman name is uncertain. The site lay in the mountainous interior where Roman control centered on road stations and mining settlements.

21523.extract: Ouasada was a city in Isauria/Lycaonia, Asia Minor.
21523.romanEraExtract: Ouasada (Vasada) was a city in the border region of Isauria and Lycaonia (south-central Anatolia, modern Turkey). The city lay in the Isaurian highlands, a notoriously lawless region where mountain bandits — the Isaurians — posed a persistent security threat to Roman authority. The Emperor Probus fought a major campaign against them in 278 AD, and Isaurian raiders continued to trouble the region until the 6th century. The city later served as a bishopric.

25162.extract: Betomolachon was a settlement in Roman Palestine.
25162.romanEraExtract: Betomolachon was a settlement in the province of Palaestina, its Semitic name (Bet = 'house of') typical of the many small towns and villages that dotted the agricultural landscape of Roman-era Palestine. The region was one of the most densely settled in the Roman Empire, with towns, villages, and agricultural estates producing olive oil, wine, and grain. The Galilee and Judean highlands supported hundreds of such settlements, many documented in the Onomasticon of Eusebius and in archaeological surveys.

21691.extract: Gazaion Limen was the port of ancient Gaza.
21691.romanEraExtract: Gazaion Limen ('Harbor of Gaza') was the port facility serving the ancient city of Gaza (Palaestina), located at the Mediterranean coast about 3-4 km west of the inland city. As a maiumas (port suburb), it sometimes received independent civic status. Gaza was one of the great trading cities of the Levant, a terminus of the overland incense trade from Arabia and a major center for the export of wine and dates. The port handled maritime commerce connecting Egypt, the Levant, and the broader Mediterranean trade network.

21692.extract: Sykomazon was a settlement in Roman Palestine.
21692.romanEraExtract: Sykomazon was a settlement in the province of Palaestina, mentioned in Byzantine-era geographical sources. The Greek name (from sykon = 'fig') suggests a connection to the fig cultivation that was widespread in the Levantine lowlands. Many small Palestinian settlements of the Roman and Byzantine periods are known from the Madaba Map — a 6th-century mosaic floor map in Jordan that provides the most detailed ancient cartographic depiction of the Holy Land and its hundreds of named places.

25192.extract: Patigga was a settlement in Roman Syria.
25192.romanEraExtract: Patigga was a settlement in the province of Syria, part of the dense network of towns and villages in the Syrian countryside that has been extensively documented by modern archaeological surveys. The 'Dead Cities' of northern Syria — hundreds of well-preserved late Roman and Byzantine settlements — reveal a prosperous agricultural society based on olive oil production and export. Many of these communities thrived from the 4th through 6th centuries before being slowly abandoned after the Islamic conquest altered trade patterns.

41091.extract: Kaine Parembole was a Roman military camp in Egypt.
41091.romanEraExtract: Kaine Parembole ('New Camp') was a Roman military installation in Egypt, its Greek name directly translating the Latin castra nova. Military camps in Roman Egypt served both as frontier garrisons and as police posts controlling the desert routes that connected the Nile valley with the Red Sea ports. The soldiers stationed at such outposts protected the lucrative trade caravans carrying Indian spices and Arabian incense from the Red Sea ports (Myos Hormos, Berenike) to the Nile and onward to Alexandria.

41088.extract: Tracheia was a settlement in Roman Egypt.
41088.romanEraExtract: Tracheia ('Rough Place') was a settlement in Roman Egypt, its Greek name suggesting a rocky or rugged terrain. The name was common in the Greco-Roman world — Cilicia Tracheia in Anatolia uses the same adjective. Settlements in the Egyptian Eastern Desert served as waystations on the routes connecting the Nile valley with the Red Sea ports and the stone quarries (porphyry, granite) that supplied Rome's monumental building projects. The Mons Porphyrites quarries alone employed thousands of workers under military guard.

41331.extract: Siphrios was a settlement in Roman Egypt.
41331.romanEraExtract: Siphrios was a settlement in Roman Egypt, part of the network of towns and villages in the Nile valley or Delta that constituted one of the most densely populated landscapes in the ancient world. Egypt's unique geography — a narrow strip of fertile land along the Nile — supported perhaps 7-8 million people in the Roman period, making it the most populous province in the empire. The annual Nile flood guaranteed agricultural productivity that made Egypt indispensable to Roman food supply.

25272.extract: Hazza was a city in Mesopotamia, ancient Arbela region.
25272.romanEraExtract: Hazza (also Hadyab/Adiabene) was a settlement in the Arbela region of northern Mesopotamia (modern Iraqi Kurdistan), part of the kingdom of Adiabene — famous in Roman-era history for its royal family's conversion to Judaism in the 1st century AD. Queen Helena of Adiabene made a celebrated pilgrimage to Jerusalem and donated gifts to the Temple. The region was a buffer zone between Rome and Parthia, and Trajan's armies passed through during his Mesopotamian campaign of 115-117 AD.

25273.extract: Bechchouphrein was a settlement in Mesopotamia.
25273.romanEraExtract: Bechchouphrein was a settlement in Mesopotamia (modern Iraq), its Aramaic-derived name reflecting the linguistic landscape of the Fertile Crescent where Aramaic was the lingua franca long before and during Roman times. The settlement was part of the agriculturally productive zone between the Tigris and Euphrates that sustained the great cities of Mesopotamia. This region — the heartland of ancient civilization — was contested between the Roman/Byzantine and Parthian/Sasanian empires for over five centuries.

25275.extract: al-Sinn was a settlement on the Tigris in Mesopotamia.
25275.romanEraExtract: Al-Sinn was a settlement on the Tigris River in Mesopotamia (modern Iraq), its Arabic name ('the tooth/point') likely referring to a rocky promontory along the river. The Tigris was the eastern artery of Mesopotamian civilization, and settlements along its banks served as agricultural communities, river crossings, and trading posts. During Roman-Persian conflicts, control of Tigris crossing points was critical — Julian's army marched down the Tigris during his fateful 363 AD campaign against Sasanian Persia.

25277.extract: Skaphe was a settlement in Mesopotamia.
25277.romanEraExtract: Skaphe (Greek: 'boat' or 'trough') was a settlement in Mesopotamia, its Greek name suggesting a Hellenistic-era foundation or renaming, part of the enduring Greek cultural imprint left by Alexander the Great and the Seleucid Empire on the Near East. Many Mesopotamian settlements bore both Semitic and Greek names, reflecting the bilingual (and often trilingual) nature of the region from the 4th century BC onward.

25144.extract: al-Madhar was a settlement in southern Mesopotamia.
25144.romanEraExtract: Al-Madhar was a settlement in southern Mesopotamia (modern southern Iraq), in the marshy lowlands between the lower Tigris and Euphrates. This region — ancient Sumer and later the Sasanian province of Meshan — was one of the oldest continuously cultivated landscapes on Earth. Under Sasanian rule, elaborate canal systems supported date palm cultivation and rice agriculture. The area was at the limits of Roman reach, penetrated only during the deepest Roman offensives into Persia.

25149.extract: Forat was a settlement in Mesopotamia.
25149.romanEraExtract: Forat was a settlement in Mesopotamia (modern Iraq), its name deriving from the Arabic/Aramaic word for the Euphrates River (al-Furat/Prat). The settlement was situated along the Euphrates, the western boundary river of Mesopotamia that also served as the approximate border between the Roman and Parthian/Sasanian empires for much of antiquity. Towns along the Euphrates were vital nodes in both the military frontier system and the long-distance trade routes connecting the Mediterranean with the Persian Gulf.

25236.extract: Matezzish was a settlement in northern Mesopotamia.
25236.romanEraExtract: Matezzish was a settlement in northern Mesopotamia, documented in ancient Assyrian and later sources. Northern Mesopotamia — the Jazira region between the upper Tigris and Euphrates — was one of the most strategically vital landscapes in antiquity. Under Roman rule (as the provinces of Mesopotamia and Osrhoene from the 2nd-3rd centuries AD), the region was organized around fortress cities like Nisibis and Singara. Smaller settlements served as agricultural communities and road stations connecting these military centers.

25233.extract: Dabrum was a settlement in Mesopotamia.
25233.romanEraExtract: Dabrum was a settlement in Mesopotamia (modern Iraq), known from ancient geographical sources. The site was part of the irrigated agricultural zone of central Mesopotamia that had sustained urban civilization since the 4th millennium BC. In the Roman period, Mesopotamia was divided between the Roman provinces (in the north) and the Parthian/Sasanian Empire (in the south), with the boundary shifting through centuries of warfare.

25239.extract: Omana was an ancient port on the Persian Gulf coast.
25239.romanEraExtract: Omana was an important ancient port on the coast of the Persian Gulf (modern Oman/UAE region), mentioned in the Periplus of the Erythraean Sea and by Ptolemy. The port handled the maritime trade between Mesopotamia, India, and the Arabian Peninsula. Ships from Omana carried frankincense, pearls, and Indian goods to the Mesopotamian ports, from where goods were transshipped to the Roman Mediterranean via overland routes. The region that bears the name Oman today may derive from this ancient emporium.

25271.extract: Girumu was a settlement in Mesopotamia.
25271.romanEraExtract: Girumu was a settlement in Mesopotamia, documented in ancient sources. The Akkadian-derived name suggests deep roots in the pre-Hellenistic linguistic landscape of the Tigris-Euphrates valley. Like many Mesopotamian settlements, it would have been sustained by the canal irrigation systems that had supported agriculture in the alluvial plain for millennia. These canals required constant maintenance — their neglect during periods of political instability could depopulate entire districts.

25254.extract: Dard was a Roman settlement in Dalmatia or Upper Moesia.
25254.romanEraExtract: Dard (or Dardapara) was a Roman settlement in the western Balkans, possibly associated with the Dardani — an Illyrian-Thracian tribe that gave their name to the Dardanelles strait and to the Roman province of Dardania. The Dardani occupied the central Balkans (modern Kosovo and southern Serbia) and were among the last Illyrian peoples to be fully subjugated by Rome, resisting both Macedonian and Roman expansion until final conquest under Augustus.

32211.extract: Chaberis Emporion was a trading port on the Indian coast.
32211.romanEraExtract: Chaberis Emporion ('Market of Chaberis') was a trading port on the southeastern coast of India (modern Tamil Nadu region), mentioned by Ptolemy in his Geography. The port was part of the chain of emporiums on the Coromandel Coast that connected the Roman Mediterranean with the kingdoms of southern India. Archaeological excavations at Arikamedu and other Tamil sites have yielded Roman coins, amphorae, and Mediterranean pottery, documenting a flourishing Indo-Roman trade in spices, textiles, gemstones, and pearls from the 1st century BC to the 3rd century AD.

32220.extract: Dounga was a settlement in ancient India known to Ptolemy.
32220.romanEraExtract: Dounga was a settlement in the Indian subcontinent, documented by Ptolemy in his 2nd-century AD Geography — the most ambitious ancient attempt to map the known world using coordinates. Ptolemy's knowledge of India, derived from merchant reports and earlier geographical traditions, extended deep into the subcontinent. His Indian geography, while containing systematic distortions, demonstrates how thoroughly the Indo-Roman trade connected the Mediterranean with South Asian markets.

32228.extract: Hippokoura was a settlement in India mentioned by Ptolemy.
32228.romanEraExtract: Hippokoura was a settlement in the Indian subcontinent documented by Ptolemy, its Greek name ('Horse Village') perhaps a translation of a local Indian toponym. The Greek naming of Indian places reflects the Hellenistic cultural layer deposited by Alexander's campaigns and the Indo-Greek kingdoms (2nd-1st centuries BC) that preceded direct Roman trade contact. By the 1st century AD, Roman merchants were sailing annually to India, and settlements across western and southern India appear in Greco-Roman geographical texts.

32233.extract: Kaliour was a settlement in India mentioned by Ptolemy.
32233.romanEraExtract: Kaliour was a settlement in the Indian subcontinent, recorded in Ptolemy's Geography. The name may be an early form of Kalur or a similar Indian toponym filtered through Greek transcription. India's presence in Roman geographical literature — Ptolemy lists dozens of Indian cities, rivers, and peoples — reflects not just trade contacts but a genuine Roman curiosity about the subcontinent. Pliny, Strabo, and the anonymous author of the Periplus all contributed to a substantial ancient literature on India.

30833.extract: Virata was an ancient Indian city.
30833.romanEraExtract: Virata (identified with Bairat/Viratnagar in Rajasthan, India) was an ancient Indian city known in the Mahabharata as the capital of King Virata, where the Pandavas spent their year of exile in disguise. In the historical period, the site is notable for its Mauryan-period remains, including Ashokan rock edicts (3rd century BC). The city lay on inland trade routes that connected the Indo-Gangetic plain with the western Indian ports from which goods were shipped to the Roman Empire.

23005.extract: Adrianople (modern Edirne) was a major Roman and Byzantine city in Thrace.
23005.romanEraExtract: Adrianople (Hadrianopolis, modern Edirne, Turkey) was a major Roman city in Thrace, refounded and named by Emperor Hadrian. It is most famous as the site of the Battle of Adrianople (378 AD), where the Visigoths under Fritigern annihilated the Roman army and killed Emperor Valens — one of the most catastrophic military defeats in Roman history, often cited as a turning point that foreshadowed the fall of the Western Empire. The city controlled the strategic junction of roads from Constantinople to the Danube frontier and from the Aegean to the Black Sea.

363.extract: Aachen was a Roman spa town and later Charlemagne's capital.
363.romanEraExtract: Aachen (Aquae Granni) was a Roman spa town in Germania Inferior, built around hot sulfurous springs sacred to the Celtic deity Grannus. Roman baths and a temple complex were constructed here from the 1st century AD. The settlement served as a rest and recuperation center for Roman soldiers from the nearby Rhine frontier. Its fame as a thermal resort continued through late antiquity and into the early medieval period, when Charlemagne made it the capital of the Carolingian Empire — deliberately evoking Roman imperial grandeur.

1526.extract: Bari was a Roman port city on the Adriatic coast of Apulia.
1526.romanEraExtract: Barium (modern Bari, Puglia, Italy) was a Roman port city on the Adriatic coast of Apulia, connected to Rome by the Via Traiana — Trajan's great highway that provided a faster coastal alternative to the Via Appia for travel to Brundisium and the eastern Mediterranean. The city served as a fishing port and waystation for traffic crossing the Adriatic to Greece. Under the late Empire, Bari grew in importance as a naval base. It later became a Byzantine stronghold and the capital of the Catepanate of Italy.

16724.extract: Amalfi was a Roman settlement on the Campanian coast.
16724.romanEraExtract: Amalfi was a modest Roman settlement on the dramatic Campanian coast south of Naples, nestled in a narrow ravine between limestone cliffs. Roman villas dotted this coastline, which was part of the luxury resort zone extending from Baiae to Sorrento. In the early medieval period, Amalfi became one of the great maritime republics of Italy, its traders rivaling Venice across the Mediterranean. The Tabula Amalphitana (Amalfitan Table) — a maritime code — helped govern Mediterranean trade for centuries.

109.extract: Rouen was the Roman city of Rotomagus in Gallia Lugdunensis.
109.romanEraExtract: Rotomagus (modern Rouen, Normandy, France) was the capital of the Gaulish Veliocasses tribe and became a major city in the Roman province of Gallia Lugdunensis (later Lugdunensis Secunda). Situated at the lowest bridging point of the Seine, the city controlled river traffic between the interior of Gaul and the English Channel. Under Rome, it possessed a large amphitheater (the third-largest in Gaul), baths, temples, and a riverside port. In the late Empire, it became the capital of the province of Lugdunensis Secunda.

33.extract: Metz was the Roman city of Divodurum Mediomatricorum in Gallia Belgica.
33.romanEraExtract: Divodurum Mediomatricorum (modern Metz, Lorraine, France) was one of the largest cities in Roman Gaul, capital of the Mediomatrici tribe in the province of Gallia Belgica. The city boasted one of the largest amphitheaters in the Roman world (seating ~25,000, rivaling those of Autun and Rome itself), extensive baths, and an aqueduct system. Strategically located at the confluence of the Moselle and Seille rivers, Divodurum controlled routes between the Rhine frontier and the interior of Gaul. Its late Roman walls enclosed over 70 hectares.

48.extract: Laon was a hilltop Roman settlement in Gallia Belgica.
48.romanEraExtract: Lugdunum Clavatum (modern Laon, Picardy, France) was a Roman settlement in the province of Gallia Belgica, dramatically perched on a steep-sided butte rising 100 meters above the surrounding plain. The elevated site made it a natural strongpoint — the Romans recognized its strategic value on the road from Reims to the northern frontier. In late antiquity, the city became an episcopal see and its natural defenses ensured its continued importance as a fortified center through the medieval period.

116.extract: Troyes was the Roman city of Augustobona Tricassium in Gallia Lugdunensis.
116.romanEraExtract: Augustobona Tricassium (modern Troyes, Champagne, France) was a Roman city in Gallia Lugdunensis, capital of the Tricasses tribe. Situated on the Seine in the heart of the Champagne plain, the city was a node on the road network connecting Lyon with the Channel coast. In 451 AD, the Battle of the Catalaunian Plains was fought nearby — the climactic confrontation where Aetius and the Visigoths halted Attila the Hun's invasion of Gaul, one of the decisive battles in European history.

40.extract: Tournai was the Roman city of Tornacum in Gallia Belgica.
40.romanEraExtract: Tornacum (modern Tournai, Belgium) was a Roman city in Gallia Belgica on the Scheldt River. The city is notable for the discovery of the tomb of Childeric I (d. 481/482), father of Clovis — the most important Frankish royal burial ever found, containing a gold signet ring, weapons, and over 300 golden bees (later adopted by Napoleon as an imperial symbol). Under Rome, Tournai was a modest road station that grew in importance during the Frankish period, becoming one of the first capitals of the Merovingian dynasty.

4.extract: Worms was the Roman city of Borbetomagus on the Rhine.
4.romanEraExtract: Borbetomagus (modern Worms, Rhineland-Palatinate, Germany) was a Roman city on the upper Rhine, capital of the Celtic Vangiones tribe. Situated on the west bank of the Rhine, the city was part of the frontier zone (limes) that guarded the empire against Germanic peoples. Roman Borbetomagus possessed a forum, temples, and a bridge across the Rhine. In the Nibelungenlied — the great medieval German epic — Worms is the capital of the Burgundians, reflecting its historical role as the center of the Burgundian kingdom before its destruction by the Huns in 437 AD.

15967.extract: Ferrara was a late Roman and Byzantine settlement in the Po Delta.
15967.romanEraExtract: Ferrara emerged as a settlement in the Po Delta region of northeastern Italy during the late Roman and early Byzantine periods. The shifting courses of the Po River created and destroyed settlements in this marshy landscape. The area was part of the Exarchate of Ravenna — the last Byzantine territory in northern Italy — and Ferrara grew as refugees from coastal towns fled inland from Lombard invasions and the silting of older ports. The city's position on a branch of the Po made it a natural crossing point between Ravenna and the Venetian lagoon.

13708.extract: León originated as the camp of the Legio VII Gemina in Hispania.
13708.romanEraExtract: Legio VII Gemina (modern León, Spain) originated as the permanent legionary fortress of the Legio VII Gemina, established in 74 AD in northwestern Hispania Tarraconensis. The city's very name derives from 'Legio' (legion). It was the only permanent legionary base in the Iberian Peninsula, tasked with protecting the gold mines of Las Médulas — the largest open-pit gold mine in the Roman Empire, which Pliny describes in vivid detail. The rectangular layout of León's old city still traces the outline of the original Roman castrum.

1447.extract: Mantua was a Roman city in Cisalpine Gaul, birthplace of Virgil.
1447.romanEraExtract: Mantua (Mantova) was an Etruscan and later Roman city in Cisalpine Gaul, famous as the birthplace of Virgil (70 BC) — Rome's greatest poet and author of the Aeneid, the Georgics, and the Eclogues. Virgil's pastoral poetry drew directly on the landscape around Mantua, the marshy plains of the Mincio River, giving the city an outsized place in Latin literary culture. Under Augustus, Mantua's territory was confiscated for veteran settlements after the Battle of Philippi (42 BC), an event Virgil laments in his first Eclogue.

22942.extract: Amarna was the short-lived capital of Pharaoh Akhenaten.
22942.romanEraExtract: Amarna (Akhetaten, modern Tell el-Amarna, Egypt) was the revolutionary capital built by Pharaoh Akhenaten (r. c. 1353-1336 BC) for his monotheistic worship of the Aten (sun disk). The city was abandoned shortly after Akhenaten's death and the restoration of traditional religion. Though predating Rome by over a millennium, the Amarna Letters — an archive of diplomatic correspondence found here — document the international system of the Late Bronze Age that set the stage for the Mediterranean world Rome would later dominate. In the Roman period, the site was uninhabited desert.

28474.extract: Avaris was the Hyksos capital in the Nile Delta.
28474.romanEraExtract: Avaris (Tell el-Dab'a, eastern Nile Delta, Egypt) was the capital of the Hyksos dynasty (c. 1650-1550 BC), Semitic rulers who controlled Lower Egypt for over a century. The city was a major Bronze Age trading hub with strong connections to the Levant, Cyprus, and the Aegean — Minoan-style frescoes discovered here stunned archaeologists. Though the city was destroyed and replaced by Pi-Ramesses under Ramesses II, the site documents the deep Mediterranean trading networks that prefigured the Greco-Roman commercial world.

22330.extract: Kish was one of the foremost Sumerian city-states in Mesopotamia.
22330.romanEraExtract: Kish (Tell Uhaimir/Tell Ingharra, central Iraq) was one of the earliest and most prestigious Sumerian city-states, whose king list reaches back to the legendary period 'after the Flood.' The title 'King of Kish' became a claim to universal sovereignty in early Mesopotamia. Though the city had declined long before the Roman period, the site remained inhabited into Parthian and Sasanian times as part of the irrigated agricultural landscape of central Mesopotamia. The area lay within the Parthian Empire, beyond direct Roman control but connected through trade.

33887.extract: Dur-Kurigalzu was a Kassite-period Babylonian city.
33887.romanEraExtract: Dur-Kurigalzu (Aqar Quf, near Baghdad, Iraq) was a major Kassite-period Babylonian city, its massive ziggurat — still standing 57 meters high — being one of the most impressive ancient monuments in Iraq. Built by King Kurigalzu I (c. 14th century BC), the city served as a royal residence and religious center. Though predating the Roman period by over a millennium, the ziggurat remained a landmark visible across the Mesopotamian plain throughout antiquity, and the site lay within the Parthian and later Sasanian domains that bordered Rome's eastern frontier.

21358.extract: Baeza was the Roman city of Vivatia in Hispania Tarraconensis.
21358.romanEraExtract: Vivatia (modern Baeza, Jaén Province, Spain) was a Roman city in the upper Guadalquivir valley of Hispania Tarraconensis, near the great mining and trade center of Castulo. The city occupied a commanding hilltop position overlooking the Guadalquivir. Under the Visigoths, it became the seat of a bishopric. The region was critical during the Second Punic War — Scipio Africanus's campaigns against Carthage in Spain centered on this olive-rich highland corridor between the Mediterranean coast and the Guadalquivir basin.

23669.extract: Tunis grew as a settlement near ancient Carthage.
23669.romanEraExtract: Tunis (Tynes/Tunes) was a modest settlement overshadowed by its mighty neighbor Carthage, just 16 km to the northeast. During the Punic Wars, Tunis served as a staging ground for forces attacking Carthage — Agathocles of Syracuse camped here in 310 BC, and the Roman general Regulus was based nearby during the First Punic War. After Rome's total destruction of Carthage in 146 BC and its refounding as a colony under Augustus, Tunis remained a secondary town. It only surpassed Carthage after the Arab conquest, when the new rulers deliberately chose Tunis over the old Roman capital.

1887.extract: Antwerp was a minor Gallo-Roman settlement on the Scheldt.
1887.romanEraExtract: Antwerp (possibly Roman Anderpus) was a Gallo-Roman settlement on the Scheldt River in the province of Gallia Belgica. Archaeological excavations beneath the medieval city have uncovered a 2nd-3rd century Roman settlement with pottery, coins, and building foundations. The site's position on the tidal Scheldt — navigable from the North Sea — gave it natural harbor advantages, though the settlement remained minor during the Roman period. Antwerp's rise to commercial greatness came only in the medieval period, when it became one of Europe's busiest ports.

330.extract: Ghent was a Gallo-Roman settlement at the confluence of the Lys and Scheldt.
330.romanEraExtract: Ganda (modern Ghent/Gent, Belgium) was a Gallo-Roman settlement at the confluence of the Lys (Leie) and Scheldt rivers in the province of Gallia Belgica. Archaeological finds include a Roman-period vicus (small town) with pottery workshops and evidence of river trade. The settlement's strategic position at the junction of two navigable rivers ensured its growth, though it remained modest under Roman rule. In the 7th century, Saint Amand founded an abbey here, and Ghent grew into one of the largest cities in medieval Europe.

10972.extract: Bratislava area was the site of the Roman fort Gerulata on the Danube limes.
10972.romanEraExtract: The Bratislava area was part of the Danube limes, with the Roman auxiliary fort of Gerulata (modern Rusovce, a suburb of Bratislava) guarding the river frontier. A larger fort existed at Carnuntum, the legionary fortress just 40 km downstream. The region was inhabited by the Celtic Boii and later by Germanic Quadi, against whom Marcus Aurelius campaigned during the Marcomannic Wars (166-180 AD) — tradition holds that he wrote parts of his Meditations while wintering at Carnuntum.

10842.extract: Esztergom was the site of the Roman fort Solva on the Danube.
10842.romanEraExtract: Solva (modern Esztergom, Hungary) was a Roman auxiliary fort on the Danube limes in the province of Pannonia. The fort guarded a strategic bend in the Danube where the river turns south. Under the late Empire, the site was part of the fortified frontier system (ripa Pannonica) that attempted to hold the Danube line against Gothic and Hunnic incursions. After the fall of Roman Pannonia, Esztergom became the first capital of the Kingdom of Hungary and seat of the Hungarian Catholic primate.

8394.extract: Caen has Gallo-Roman origins in Normandy.
8394.romanEraExtract: The Caen area (Calvados, Normandy, France) shows evidence of Gallo-Roman settlement along the Orne River, though the city itself emerged as a significant center only in the medieval period under William the Conqueror. The surrounding region was part of the civitas of the Viducasses in the province of Gallia Lugdunensis. Nearby Roman sites include the major town of Augustodurum (Bayeux) and scattered rural villas that exploited the fertile Norman plain for grain production.

14138.extract: Montpellier area had Roman settlement in Gallia Narbonensis.
14138.romanEraExtract: The Montpellier area in the province of Gallia Narbonensis was surrounded by important Roman settlements — Nemausus (Nîmes), Sextantio (Castelnau-le-Lez), and the Via Domitia, Rome's first road in Gaul (built 121 BC). While Montpellier itself emerged as a city only in the 10th century, the surrounding landscape was thoroughly Romanized with villas, aqueducts, and the coastal road connecting Spain to Italy. The nearby oppidum of Sextantio was the predecessor settlement in the area.

361.extract: Frankfurt area was the site of the Roman town Nida.
361.romanEraExtract: The Frankfurt area was the site of Nida (modern Frankfurt-Heddernheim), a significant Roman town in the province of Germania Superior. Nida served as the civilian capital (civitas) of the region and was one of the most important settlements along the Wetterau limes — the frontier line that extended northeast of the Rhine. The town had a forum, baths, temples (including a Mithraeum), and a population estimated at 10,000. When the limes fell to Alamannic pressure around 260 AD, Nida was abandoned in favor of fortified positions on the Rhine.

28535.extract: Tinnis was an island city in Lake Manzala, Egypt.
28535.romanEraExtract: Thennesos/Tinnis (Tell Tinnis, Lake Manzala, Egypt) was an island city in the lagoon of the eastern Nile Delta. In late antiquity and the early Islamic period, Tinnis became one of the most prosperous cities in Egypt, famous for producing the finest linen textiles in the medieval world — Tinnis cloth was prized by caliphs and sultans. Under Roman and Byzantine rule, the settlement was part of the network of Delta towns that exploited both agriculture and fishing in the rich lagoon environment.

33913.extract: Samarra was a settlement on the Tigris in Mesopotamia.
33913.romanEraExtract: The Samarra area on the Tigris (modern Iraq) had ancient roots as an agricultural settlement and military outpost. During the Roman-Sasanian wars, the middle Tigris was contested territory. The site of Samarra later became the temporary capital of the Abbasid Caliphate (836-892 AD), when the caliphs built a vast city stretching 40 km along the Tigris — its Great Mosque's spiral minaret (the Malwiya) remains one of the most iconic monuments of Islamic architecture. Roman military expeditions under Trajan and Julian passed through this region.

33981.extract: Isfahan was the Sasanian city of Aspadana/Jay in central Iran.
33981.romanEraExtract: Aspadana (modern Isfahan, Iran) was a major Sasanian city in central Iran, known as Jay (Gabae/Gabiana to Greek geographers). Situated on the Zayandeh River at the crossroads of routes connecting Mesopotamia, Persia, and Central Asia, the city was a significant center in the Sasanian Empire — Rome's great eastern rival. Ptolemy mentions Aspadana among the cities of Media. Isfahan lay beyond Rome's military reach but was deeply connected to the Roman world through the silk trade and diplomatic exchanges between the two empires.

27144.extract: Basra was founded in 636 AD near the ancient Mesopotamian port region.
27144.romanEraExtract: The Basra area in southern Mesopotamia (modern Iraq) was part of the ancient region of Characene/Mesene, centered on the great port of Charax Spasinu at the head of the Persian Gulf. This region was the terminus of the maritime spice trade connecting Rome with India via the Gulf. Alexander the Great's admiral Nearchus sailed through these waters, and Roman merchants frequented the Gulf ports. Basra itself was founded in 636 AD as an Arab military camp, but its location near the Shatt al-Arab inherited the ancient port function of Charax.

23106.extract: Bukhara was a major oasis city on the Silk Road in Central Asia.
23106.romanEraExtract: The Bukhara oasis in Transoxiana (modern Uzbekistan) was one of the great nodes of the Silk Road connecting China with the Roman Mediterranean. Though far beyond Rome's direct control, Bukhara lay in the region the Greeks knew from Alexander's campaigns and the Seleucid and Greco-Bactrian kingdoms. Chinese silk destined for Roman markets passed through Central Asian trading cities like Bukhara, and Roman gold coins have been found across the region. The oasis's irrigated agriculture supported a large urban population from ancient times.

31245.extract: Jerez de la Frontera area was the site of Roman Asta Regia.
31245.romanEraExtract: Asta Regia (near modern Jerez de la Frontera, Cádiz Province, Spain) was a Roman city in Hispania Baetica, giving its name to the conventus Astigitanus. The city's cognomen 'Regia' indicates its pre-Roman importance as a Turdetanian royal center. Situated in the lower Guadalquivir near the ancient coastline (which has since retreated), Asta controlled access to the river's mouth and the Atlantic trade routes. Caesar and Pompey both campaigned in this region during the civil wars, and the nearby Battle of Munda (45 BC) decided the fate of the Roman Republic.

37449.extract: Kherson was founded in 1778 near ancient Greek colonial territory.
37449.romanEraExtract: The Kherson area on the lower Dnieper (modern Ukraine) was part of the territory known to the Greeks as Scythia, the vast steppe north of the Black Sea inhabited by nomadic peoples. The nearby Greek colony of Olbia (at the Bug-Dnieper estuary) was one of the most important Milesian foundations on the Black Sea, and Roman influence extended to the region through trade and diplomatic contacts with the Scythian and Sarmatian peoples. Roman coins and goods have been found throughout the northern Black Sea steppe.

22029.extract: Sudak was a Byzantine and later Genoese port in the Crimea.
22029.romanEraExtract: Sougdaia (modern Sudak, Crimea) was a Byzantine-era port on the southeastern coast of the Crimean Peninsula, founded around the 3rd century AD. The site lay in the zone of Roman and Byzantine influence that extended across the northern Black Sea, where Greek colonies like Chersonesos and Pantikapaion had maintained Mediterranean civilization since the 6th century BC. Sudak became a major trading center connecting the Byzantine Empire with the Khazar Khaganate and the Silk Road trade routes of Central Asia.

27021.extract: Tlemcen was the Roman military post Pomaria in Mauretania Caesariensis.
27021.romanEraExtract: Pomaria (modern Tlemcen, Algeria) was a Roman military post in the province of Mauretania Caesariensis, its Latin name meaning 'orchards' — reflecting the fertile, well-watered site in the Tell Atlas mountains near the Moroccan border. The garrison was part of the western sector of the African limes, guarding the frontier between Roman-controlled agricultural lands and the semi-arid regions to the south. The site's springs and mild climate made it one of the most pleasant postings on the African frontier.

44257.extract: Ulm area had a Roman settlement on the upper Danube.
44257.romanEraExtract: The Ulm area on the upper Danube (modern Baden-Württemberg, Germany) was part of the Roman province of Raetia, situated near the Danube frontier. Roman forts and watchtowers guarded this section of the river, and a small settlement existed near the later medieval city. The region was part of the Agri Decumates — the territory between the Rhine and Danube that Rome annexed in the 1st century AD and abandoned under Alamannic pressure around 260 AD. Ulm itself rose to prominence only in the medieval period.

25316.extract: Aberdeen area was the site of the Roman camp Devana in Caledonia.
25316.romanEraExtract: Devana (near modern Aberdeen, Scotland) was a Roman site in Caledonia, mentioned by Ptolemy as one of the northernmost settlements known to the Roman world. The area lay beyond the permanent frontier of Hadrian's Wall and even the Antonine Wall, in territory that Rome periodically invaded but never permanently held. Agricola's campaigns (77-84 AD) pushed Roman forces through this region to the Battle of Mons Graupius, the northernmost pitched battle in Roman history.

27114.extract: Siraf was a major Persian Gulf port in the Sasanian and Islamic periods.
27114.romanEraExtract: Siraf (modern Bandar Taheri, Bushehr Province, Iran) was a major port on the Persian Gulf coast, flourishing from the Sasanian period through the early Islamic era as one of the busiest maritime trading centers in the world. Ships from Siraf sailed to India, China, and East Africa — the same Indian Ocean trade routes that connected with the Roman Mediterranean. The port city reached its peak in the 9th-10th centuries, when it rivaled Baghdad in wealth, before being destroyed by an earthquake in 977 AD.

33863.extract: Qazvin was an ancient city in Media, Iran.
33863.romanEraExtract: Qazvin (ancient Rhagiana region, Iran) was a settlement in the Sasanian province of Media, at the western edge of the Alborz Mountains guarding the route to the Caspian Gates — one of the most strategically important mountain passes in the ancient world. The Caspian Gates controlled access between Mesopotamia and Central Asia, and Alexander the Great pursued the fleeing Darius III through this pass. The city lay beyond Roman reach but within the diplomatic and commercial sphere connecting the two great empires.

33989.extract: Qom was a Sasanian-era settlement in central Iran.
33989.romanEraExtract: Qom (ancient Goyman/Kumis area, central Iran) was a settlement in the Sasanian heartland, located on the route connecting Isfahan with Rayy (Tehran) across the central Iranian plateau. The region was part of the Sasanian Empire's core territory — the great rival civilization that confronted Rome and Byzantium across the Euphrates for over four centuries (224-651 AD). Qom rose to religious prominence in the Islamic period as a major Shia holy city.

32797.extract: Mosul lies across the Tigris from ancient Nineveh.
32797.romanEraExtract: Mosul (modern Iraq) lies on the western bank of the Tigris directly across from the ruins of Nineveh — the great Assyrian capital destroyed in 612 BC. In the Roman period, the site was within the Parthian and later Sasanian spheres, but Roman armies repeatedly campaigned in the region. Trajan's forces reached the area in 116 AD, and the region was contested during centuries of Roman-Persian warfare. The crossing at Nineveh/Mosul was one of the key strategic points on the upper Tigris.

chandler-cologne-800.extract: Cologne is the fourth-most populous city of Germany and the largest city of the German state of North Rhine-Westphalia with nearly 1.1 million inhabitants in the city proper and over 3.1 million people in the Cologne Bonn urban region. Cologne is also part of the Rhine-Ruhr metropolitan region, the second biggest metropolitan region by GDP in the European Union. Centered on the left bank of the Rhine, Cologne is located on the River Rhine, about 35 kilometres (22 mi) southeast of the North Rhine-Westphalia state capital Düsseldorf and 22 kilometres (14 mi) northwest of Bonn, the former capital of West Germany.
chandler-cologne-800.romanEraExtract: Colonia Claudia Ara Agrippinensium (modern Cologne) was founded as a Roman colony in 50 AD by Emperor Claudius at the request of his wife Agrippina, who was born there. Originally the site of the Oppidum Ubiorum, the settlement of the Germanic Ubii tribe, it became the capital of the province of Germania Inferior. The city was a major military base on the Rhine frontier and an important center of Roman glass production and trade. Its name preserves its colonial status to this day.

2098.extract: Bruges is the capital and largest city of the province of West Flanders, in the Flemish Region of Belgium. It is located in the northwest of the country, and is the sixth most populous city in Belgium. The predominant language is Dutch.
2098.romanEraExtract: Bruges is the capital and largest city of the province of West Flanders, in the Flemish Region of Belgium. It is located in the northwest of the country, and is the sixth most populous city in Belgium. The predominant language is Dutch.

4156.extract: Lutetia, also known as Lutecia and Lutetia Parisiorum, was a Gallo–Roman town and the predecessor of modern-day Paris. Traces of an earlier Neolithic settlement have been found nearby, and a larger settlement was established around the middle of the third century BC by the Parisii, a Gallic tribe. The site was an important crossing point of the Seine, the intersection of land and water trade routes.
4156.romanEraExtract: Lutetia, also known as Lutecia and Lutetia Parisiorum, was a Gallo–Roman town and the predecessor of modern-day Paris. Traces of an earlier Neolithic settlement have been found nearby, and a larger settlement was established around the middle of the third century BC by the Parisii, a Gallic tribe. The site was an important crossing point of the Seine, the intersection of land and water trade routes.

43.extract: Arras is the prefecture of the Pas-de-Calais department, which forms part of the region of Hauts-de-France; before the reorganization of 2014 it was in Nord-Pas-de-Calais. The historic centre of the Artois region, with a Baroque town square, Arras is in northern France at the confluence of the rivers Scarpe and Crinchon.
43.romanEraExtract: Arras is the prefecture of the Pas-de-Calais department, which forms part of the region of Hauts-de-France; before the reorganization of 2014 it was in Nord-Pas-de-Calais. The historic centre of the Artois region, with a Baroque town square, Arras is in northern France at the confluence of the rivers Scarpe and Crinchon.

5.extract: Speyer, historically known in English as Spires, is a city in Rhineland-Palatinate in the western part of the Federal Republic of Germany with approximately 50,000 inhabitants. Located on the left bank of the river Rhine, Speyer lies 25 km south of Ludwigshafen and Mannheim, and 21 km south-west of Heidelberg. Founded by the ancient Romans as a fortified town on the northeast frontiers of their Roman Empire, it is one of Germany's oldest cities. Speyer Cathedral, a number of other churches, and the Altpörtel dominate the Speyer landscape. In the cathedral, beneath the high altar, are the tombs of eight Holy Roman Emperors and German kings.
5.romanEraExtract: Speyer, historically known in English as Spires, is a city in Rhineland-Palatinate in the western part of the Federal Republic of Germany with approximately 50,000 inhabitants. Located on the left bank of the river Rhine, Speyer lies 25 km south of Ludwigshafen and Mannheim, and 21 km south-west of Heidelberg. Founded by the ancient Romans as a fortified town on the northeast frontiers of their Roman Empire, it is one of Germany's oldest cities. Speyer Cathedral, a number of other churches, and the Altpörtel dominate the Speyer landscape. In the cathedral, beneath the high altar, are the tombs of eight Holy Roman Emperors and German kings.

34031.extract: Prague is the capital and largest city of the Czech Republic and the historical capital of Bohemia. Located on the Vltava River, the city has a population of about 1.4 million, making it the twelfth-largest city in the European Union. Its metropolitan area is home to approximately 2.3 million people.
34031.romanEraExtract: Prague is the capital and largest city of the Czech Republic and the historical capital of Bohemia. Located on the Vltava River, the city has a population of about 1.4 million, making it the twelfth-largest city in the European Union. Its metropolitan area is home to approximately 2.3 million people.

172.extract: Avignon is the prefecture of the Vaucluse department in the Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur region of southern France. Located on the left bank of the river Rhône, the commune has a population of 92,188 (2023), with about 16,000 living in the ancient town centre enclosed by its medieval walls. The Communauté d'agglomération du Grand Avignon, a cooperation structure of 16 communes, had 197,102 inhabitants in 2022.
172.romanEraExtract: Avignon is the prefecture of the Vaucluse department in the Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur region of southern France. Located on the left bank of the river Rhône, the commune has a population of 92,188 (2023), with about 16,000 living in the ancient town centre enclosed by its medieval walls. The Communauté d'agglomération du Grand Avignon, a cooperation structure of 16 communes, had 197,102 inhabitants in 2022.

187.extract: Toulon is a city in the Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur region of southeastern France. Located on the French Riviera at the eastern extremity of the Gulf of Lion, it formed part of the historical region of Provence. The city functions as the prefecture of the Var department.
187.romanEraExtract: Toulon is a city in the Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur region of southeastern France. Located on the French Riviera at the eastern extremity of the Gulf of Lion, it formed part of the historical region of Provence. The city functions as the prefecture of the Var department.

304.extract: Dijon is a city and the prefecture of the Côte-d'Or department of the Bourgogne-Franche-Comté region in eastern France. As of 2023, the population of the commune was 161,830, making it the most populous commune in Bourgogne-Franche-Comté.
304.romanEraExtract: Dijon is a city and the prefecture of the Côte-d'Or department of the Bourgogne-Franche-Comté region in eastern France. As of 2023, the population of the commune was 161,830, making it the most populous commune in Bourgogne-Franche-Comté.

22.extract: Geneva is the second-most populous city in Switzerland and the most populous in French-speaking Romandy. Situated in the southwest of the country, where the Rhône exits Lake Geneva, it is the capital of the Republic and Canton of Geneva. Geneva is a global city, an international financial centre, and a worldwide centre for diplomacy, which has led to it being called the "Peace Capital".
22.romanEraExtract: Geneva is the second-most populous city in Switzerland and the most populous in French-speaking Romandy. Situated in the southwest of the country, where the Rhône exits Lake Geneva, it is the capital of the Republic and Canton of Geneva. Geneva is a global city, an international financial centre, and a worldwide centre for diplomacy, which has led to it being called the "Peace Capital".

26939.extract: Almería is a city and municipality of Spain, located in Andalusia. It is the capital of the province of the same name. The city lies in southeastern Iberia, extending primarily in between the eastern fringes of the Sierra de Gádor and the Andarax riverbed along the coastline of the Gulf of Almería, a large inlet of the Mediterranean Sea. The municipality has a population of 201,946.
26939.romanEraExtract: Almería is a city and municipality of Spain, located in Andalusia. It is the capital of the province of the same name. The city lies in southeastern Iberia, extending primarily in between the eastern fringes of the Sierra de Gádor and the Andarax riverbed along the coastline of the Gulf of Almería, a large inlet of the Mediterranean Sea. The municipality has a population of 201,946.

22422.extract: Ceuta is an autonomous city of Spain on the North African coast. Bordered by Morocco, it lies along the boundary between the Mediterranean Sea and the Atlantic Ocean. Ceuta is one of the special member state territories of the European Union.
22422.romanEraExtract: Ceuta is an autonomous city of Spain on the North African coast. Bordered by Morocco, it lies along the boundary between the Mediterranean Sea and the Atlantic Ocean. Ceuta is one of the special member state territories of the European Union.

27108.extract: Mecca, officially Makkah al-Mukarramah, is a city in the Hejaz region of western Saudi Arabia and is the capital of Mecca Province. Mecca is considered the birthplace of Islam and the Islamic prophet Muhammad, as well as being the holiest city in Islam.
27108.romanEraExtract: Mecca, officially Makkah al-Mukarramah, is a city in the Hejaz region of western Saudi Arabia and is the capital of Mecca Province. Mecca is considered the birthplace of Islam and the Islamic prophet Muhammad, as well as being the holiest city in Islam.

10569.extract: Genoa is the sixth-largest city in Italy and the capital of the Italian region of Liguria. As of 2025, 565,301 people live within the city's administrative limits. While its metropolitan city has 818,629 inhabitants, more than 1.5 million people live in the wider metropolitan area stretching along the Italian Riviera.
10569.romanEraExtract: Genoa is the sixth-largest city in Italy and the capital of the Italian region of Liguria. As of 2025, 565,301 people live within the city's administrative limits. While its metropolitan city has 818,629 inhabitants, more than 1.5 million people live in the wider metropolitan area stretching along the Italian Riviera.

1437.extract: Ticinum was an ancient city of Gallia Transpadana, founded on the banks of the river of the same name a little way above its confluence with the Padus (Po).
1437.romanEraExtract: Ticinum was an ancient city of Gallia Transpadana, founded on the banks of the river of the same name a little way above its confluence with the Padus (Po).

chandler-gallipoli-1000.extract: The Gallipoli Peninsula is located in the southern part of East Thrace, the European part of Turkey, with the Aegean Sea to the west and the Dardanelles strait to the east.
chandler-gallipoli-1000.romanEraExtract: The Gallipoli Peninsula (ancient Thracian Chersonesus) was one of the most strategically vital regions in the ancient world, controlling the narrow Hellespont strait between Europe and Asia. The peninsula was colonized by Greeks from the 7th century BC and passed through Persian, Athenian, Macedonian, and Roman hands. Under Rome it was part of the province of Thracia, and its position made it a critical crossing point for armies and trade between the empire's European and Asian territories.

chandler-constantinople--100.extract: Constantinople, the modern Istanbul, was the capital of the Roman and Byzantine Empire from 330 until its takeover by the Ottoman Empire in 1453. After this, it continued to function as the Ottoman capital until the abolition of the Ottoman sultanate in 1922. It was located at the core of the modern city of Istanbul, on a peninsula at the southeastern tip of Thrace in Europe; with the Bosporus strait and the ancient cities of Chalcedon and Chrysopolis in Bithynia, Anatolia to the east; the Golden Horn and the citadel of Galata (Pera) to the north; the Sea of Marmara to the south; and the Princes' Islands to the southeast.
chandler-constantinople--100.romanEraExtract: Constantinople (modern Istanbul) was founded by Emperor Constantine I in 330 AD as the new eastern capital of the Roman Empire, built on the site of the ancient Greek colony of Byzantium. Strategically positioned on the Bosphorus strait between Europe and Asia, it became the largest and wealthiest city in Europe for most of the medieval period. As capital of the Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire, it was protected by the massive Theodosian Walls and served as a center of Christianity, commerce, and Greco-Roman culture until its fall to the Ottoman Turks in 1453.

28915.extract: Samarkand is a city in southeastern Uzbekistan and among the oldest continuously inhabited cities in Central Asia. Samarkand is the capital of the Samarkand Region and a district-level city, that includes the urban-type settlements Kimyogarlar, Farhod and Khishrav. With 551,700 inhabitants (2021), it is the third-largest city in Uzbekistan.
28915.romanEraExtract: Samarkand is a city in southeastern Uzbekistan and among the oldest continuously inhabited cities in Central Asia. Samarkand is the capital of the Samarkand Region and a district-level city, that includes the urban-type settlements Kimyogarlar, Farhod and Khishrav. With 551,700 inhabitants (2021), it is the third-largest city in Uzbekistan.

21140.extract: Coptic Cairo is a part of Old Cairo which encompasses the Babylon Fortress, the Coptic Museum, the Hanging Church, the Greek Church of St. George and many other Coptic churches and historical sites. It is believed in Christian tradition that the Holy Family visited this area and stayed at the site of Saints Sergius and Bacchus Church. Coptic Cairo was a stronghold for Christianity in Egypt both before and during the Islamic era, as most of its churches were built after the Muslim conquest of Egypt in the 7th century.

It is believed in Christian tradition that the Holy Family visited this area and stayed at the site of Saints Sergius and Bacchus Church. Coptic Cairo was a stronghold for Christianity in Egypt both before and during the Islamic era, as most of its churches were built after the Muslim conquest of Egypt in the 7th century.

30686.extract: Medina, officially al-Madinah al-Munawwarah, also known as al-Madinah an-Nabawiyyah and Taybah, and known in pre-Islamic times as Yathrib, is the capital and administrative centre of Medina Province in the Hejaz region of western Saudi Arabia. It is one of the oldest and most important places in Islamic history. The second holiest city in Islam, the population as of 2022 is 1,477,023, making it the fourth-most populous city in the country. Around 58.5% of the population are Saudi citizens and 41.5% are foreigners. Located at the core of the Medina Province in the western reaches of the country, the city is distributed over 589 km2 (227 sq mi), of which 293 km2 (113 sq mi) constitutes the city's urban area, while the rest is occupied by the Hejaz Mountains, empty valleys, agricultural spaces, and older dormant volcanoes.
30686.romanEraExtract: Medina, officially al-Madinah al-Munawwarah, also known as al-Madinah an-Nabawiyyah and Taybah, and known in pre-Islamic times as Yathrib, is the capital and administrative centre of Medina Province in the Hejaz region of western Saudi Arabia.
